Their research focuses on understanding how the brain maintains and manipulates visual information, how attention selects and processes perceptual inputs, and how these cognitive processes shape behavior and neural representations. Dr. Chunharas has significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating the simultaneous representation of working memory contents and sensory inputs in early visual cortex, revealing adaptive distortions in visual working memory, and showing the beneficial effects of depth separation on visual working memory performance.

0100.name: Brigitte Röder
0100.institution: Biological Psychology and Neuropsychology, University of Hamburg; Jasti V Ramanamma Children’s Eye Center, Child Sight Institute, LV Prasad Eye Institute, Hyderabad, India
0100.bio: Dr. Connor investigates the neural mechanisms underlying object perception and shape processing within higher-level visual cortex. His research aims to elucidate how the brain transforms visual images into representations of 3D object structure and encodes large-scale spatial environments. His lab has demonstrated rotational stability of object representations in anterior inferotemporal cortex and elucidated neural coding of object value and utility in the ventral visual pathway.

0101.name: Bruno Olshausen
0101.institution: Redwood Center for Theoretical Neuroscience, University of California, Berkeley
0101.department: Herbert Wertheim School of Optometry and Vision Science; Helen Wills Neuroscience Institute, University of California, Berkeley
0101.bio: This researcher investigates the interplay between visual perception and higher-level cognition, particularly focusing on the limits of top-down influences and the nature of physical intuition. Their work addresses fundamental questions regarding how humans represent and reason about objects, events, and even absences within visual scenes. They have significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating limitations of cognitive penetrability in perception, revealing automatic physical intuitions, and providing empirical evidence for the perception of absences.

0102.name: Bruno Rossion
0102.institution: Université de Lorraine, CNRS; Université de Lorraine, CHRU-Nancy, Service de Neurologie; Université de Lorraine, CHRU-Nancy, Service de Neurochirurgie
0102.bio: Dr. Shen's research focuses on developing statistical learning and data science methodologies for analyzing high-dimensional and complex datasets. His work addresses fundamental problems in quantifying dependence, extracting low-dimensional representations, and developing scalable algorithms for large-scale data analysis. A significant contribution includes the development of distance correlation and multiscale graph correlation measures, alongside theoretical advancements in distance-based hypothesis testing and novel algorithms for feature selection and dimension reduction.

0103.name: Buetti Simona
0103.bio: Dr. Olman investigates the neural mechanisms underlying scene perception and contextual modulation in early visual cortex. Her research addresses how local image features are integrated to form representations of scenes and objects, and how these processes are influenced by internal templates and surrounding context. Her contributions include advancing high-resolution fMRI techniques for characterizing layer-specific activity in V1 and elucidating the neural basis of contour integration and figure-ground segmentation.

0104.name: Carlos Ponce
0104.department: Kempner Institute for the Study of Natural and Artificial Intelligence; Harvard Medical School
0104.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying nonverbal communication and emotion recognition, with a specific focus on cross-cultural variations in body language. Their research addresses questions regarding the influence of culture on emotional expression, the development of culturally sensitive emotion databases, and the neural correlates of team flow states. A significant contribution is the development of an Asian-based bodily movement database for emotional communication and the identification of neural signatures associated with team flow states using EEG.

0105.name: Carolina Diaz-Piedra
0105.department: Mind, Brain, and Behavior Research Center
0105.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, with a focus on color vision, face perception, visual attention, and binocular vision. Their work addresses fundamental questions regarding how the human visual system processes complex information, how different visual features interact, and the cognitive and neural bases of face perception. Key contributions include developing computational models of color vision and face perception, elucidating neural mechanisms of binocular rivalry and stereopsis, and advancing understanding of facial expression recognition and the role of visual attention.

0106.name: Caroline Blais
0106.bio: Dr. Baker investigates the neural mechanisms underlying high-level visual perception, particularly object and scene recognition, and how these representations are modulated by learning, experience, and cortical plasticity. Their research addresses how the structure, function, and selectivity of the cortex change with experience or impairment, and aims to elucidate the neural basis of complex visual stimuli representations. Dr. Baker's significant contributions include demonstrating rapid face pareidolia processing, revealing multidimensional mental representations of natural objects, identifying distinct medial parietal cortex subdivisions for recollection, and elucidating neural object representations during occlusion.

0107.name: Caroline E. Robertson
0107.bio: Dr. Vater investigates the perceptual-cognitive mechanisms underlying skilled performance in dynamic sports environments, with a particular emphasis on gaze behavior and decision-making. His research addresses questions regarding the utilization of peripheral vision, optimal gaze strategies, and the trainability of perceptual-cognitive skills to enhance athletic performance. He has significantly contributed to the field by identifying distinct gaze strategies employed by athletes, demonstrating the contextual modulation of gaze anchoring, and developing virtual reality methodologies for studying sports decision-making.

0108.name: Catherine L. Reed
0108.bio: Dr. Wallraven investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying multisensory integration, face processing, and aesthetic perception. Their research addresses how humans integrate information across modalities, recognize facial expressions, and categorize objects, with a focus on bridging the gap between human perception and computational models. Wallraven's contributions include developing perceptually-grounded computational recognition systems, elucidating the role of dynamic information in facial expression recognition, and pioneering work on the perceptual realism of animated faces and multimodal object categorization.

0109.name: Cathleen M. Moore
0109.bio: Dr. Windischberger investigates the neural mechanisms underlying cognitive processes, particularly visuo-spatial imagery and creativity, using advanced functional neuroimaging techniques. His research focuses on optimizing fMRI methodologies, including acquisition and analysis, and understanding how transcranial magnetic stimulation modulates brain activity and connectivity. He has significantly contributed to the field through pioneering concurrent TMS-fMRI methodology, developing novel fMRI techniques like single-trial fMRI, and elucidating the neural correlates of creative insight using ultra-high-field 7T fMRI.

0110.name: Chai-Youn Kim
0110.bio: This researcher investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying second-order visual processing, texture perception, boundary detection, and the psychophysical basis of trypophobia. Their work addresses how the visual system integrates second-order information, segments textures, detects boundaries, and responds to aversive visual patterns. Key contributions include developing machine learning models of second-order boundary perception, differentiating neural mechanisms for density-based versus feature-based texture segmentation, and providing evidence for trypophobia as an overgeneralized disease-avoidance response.

0111.name: Chaipat Chunharas
0111.institution: Cognitive Clinical & Computational Neuroscience Lab, Faculty of Medicine, Chulalongkorn University
0111.bio: Dr. Chuan Hou investigates the neural and perceptual mechanisms underlying binocular vision, particularly its disruption in amblyopia and strabismus. Her research aims to elucidate how interocular suppression is modulated by selective visual attention and to characterize the spatiotemporal dynamics of visual processing in individuals with compromised binocularity. Dr. Hou's significant contributions include developing dichoptic attention paradigms for amblyopic training and employing EEG source imaging to reveal the neural substrates of interocular suppression and visual processing dynamics in binocular disorders.

0112.name: Chaoxiong Ye
0112.institution: University of Jyvaskyla; Sichuan Normal University
0112.bio: Dr. Lin investigates the cognitive and computational mechanisms underlying person perception and social cognition. Their research addresses how individuals form trait inferences from faces and behaviors, and how these inferences subsequently influence mental state attributions and social decision-making. Dr. Lin's work has identified key dimensions of facial trait impressions, demonstrated the influence of these impressions on mental state inferences, and developed computational models to predict social judgments, while also cautioning against uncritical reliance on deep learning approaches.

0113.name: Charles E. Connor
0113.bio: Dr. Teng investigates the influence of top-down cognitive processes, such as working memory and cognitive control, on visual perception. Her research aims to elucidate the neural mechanisms and temporal dynamics underlying the interactions between visual working memory and visual perception, particularly focusing on the role of proactive and reactive control processes. Dr. Teng's work has demonstrated that visual working memory modulates perceptual sensitivity for low-level visual features and has developed a flexible control model to quantify proactive and reactive control in working memory-perception interactions.

0114.name: Chaz Firestone
0114.bio: Dr. Bearfield investigates the cognitive and perceptual mechanisms underlying human interaction with data visualizations. Her research addresses how design choices in visualizations influence perception, interpretation, and decision-making processes. Her work has demonstrated the impact of visual representation and viewer salience on data interpretation, developed methods for measuring trust in human-data interaction, and identified how textual annotations and grouping cues affect bias perception and comparative judgments.

0115.name: Chencheng Shen
0115.bio: Dr. Hickey investigates the influence of reward processing and incentive salience on visual attention and perception. Her research addresses how reward history modulates attentional capture, elucidating the neural mechanisms underlying these effects, particularly the role of dopaminergic systems. Her work has demonstrated that reward-associated features capture attention independently of top-down control and has identified neural correlates of reward-driven attentional capture, contributing to a deeper understanding of how reward shapes visual salience and attentional selection.

0116.name: Cheryl Olman
0116.bio: Dr. Clifford investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying visual perception, with a particular focus on contextual modulation, face/gaze processing, and sensory coding. His research addresses how context, prior expectations, and uncertainty influence visual processing and decision-making, aiming to elucidate the neural basis of visual awareness. He has significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating the functional impact of adaptation, characterizing contextual modulation in visual cortex, and revealing perceptual biases in gaze processing through psychophysical, neuroimaging, and computational modeling approaches.

0117.name: Chia-Huei Tseng
0117.institution: Research Institute of Electrical Communication, Tohoku University, Sendai, Japan
0117.bio: The researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual information processing using transcranial interference stimulation (tIS). Their research aims to elucidate how targeted brain stimulation can modulate visual perception and cognition. This work has significantly contributed to the development of innovative neuroimaging techniques and the understanding of brain function in mental illness.

0118.name: Chien Chung Chen
0118.bio: Dr. Cong Yu investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying visual perceptual learning and plasticity. His research addresses questions regarding the rules governing the specificity and transfer of perceptual learning, as well as the influence of top-down cognitive factors on low-level perceptual processes. Dr. Yu's work has demonstrated complete transfer of perceptual learning across retinal locations via double training, established a rule-based learning theory, and shown that perceptual learning can improve vision in adult amblyopia through cognitive compensation.

0119.name: Chien-Chung Chen
0119.bio: Dr. Bohil investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying categorization and decision-making, with a focus on human interaction with technology. Her research addresses how individuals learn to classify environmental elements, the cognitive processes involved in decision-making, and how cognitive science principles can enhance human performance.  She has made significant contributions through the development of computational cognitive models, the application of cognitive neuroscience methods like fNIRS to study prefrontal cortex function, and the investigation of human-technology interaction within virtual reality environments.

0120.name: Ching-Yi Wang
0120.bio: The Miller Lab investigates the neural and behavioral mechanisms underlying primate vocal communication and social cognition, particularly in marmosets. Their research addresses how primate brains process and produce vocalizations within natural social contexts, and how evolutionary and developmental factors shape these behaviors. This lab has significantly contributed to the field by identifying neural correlates of vocal production and perception in primate frontal cortex, demonstrating turn-taking dynamics in marmoset vocalizations, and elucidating cross-modal integration of social cues in the hippocampus.

0121.name: Chris Baker
0121.bio: Dr. Turner's research focuses on the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying memory-guided attention in visual search. She investigates the temporal dynamics and organizational properties of attentional target templates, as well as how the brain prioritizes objects competing for attentional resources. As a postgraduate researcher, Dr. Turner's work is ongoing, and definitive major contributions are not yet established based on the provided information.

0122.name: Christian H. Poth
0122.bio: Dr. de la Malla investigates the spatiotemporal dynamics of visual information processing during oculomotor behavior and its influence on sensorimotor control. Her research addresses how the visual system integrates foveal and peripheral information, processes temporal cues, and adapts to delays in visual feedback to optimize interceptive actions. Her work has demonstrated distinct temporal dynamics in foveal and peripheral vision, revealed the benefits of pursuit eye movements on velocity perception, and elucidated the role of predictive and online visual information in enhancing the temporal precision of interception.

0123.name: Christian Vater
0123.bio: Dr. Smith's research focuses on the intersection of machine learning, natural language processing, and computer vision, particularly within the subdisciplines of deep learning, reinforcement learning, and generative models. His work addresses fundamental questions regarding the development of efficient deep learning architectures, the ethical implications of artificial intelligence, the interpretability of machine learning models, and effective approaches for few-shot learning in NLP. While specific contributions are not detailed, his research aims to advance the theoretical and practical understanding of these areas within artificial intelligence.

0124.name: Christian Wallraven
0124.bio: Dr. Baker investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying human visual and multisensory perception, with a particular focus on binocular vision. His research addresses how the brain integrates sensory signals, especially in binocular vision, and how deficits in these processes manifest in clinical conditions such as amblyopia. Dr. Baker's significant contributions include demonstrating binocular combination in amblyopia, developing computational models of contrast perception incorporating divisive surround-suppression, and advancing the understanding of neural markers of suppression in impaired binocular vision.

0125.name: Christian Windischberger
0125.bio: Dr. Dilks investigates the functional organization and plasticity of high-level visual cortex, particularly as it relates to scene processing and visual recognition. His research addresses how the functional architecture of visual cortex develops from infancy through adulthood and how it adapts in response to experience. His work has revealed rapid perceptual elongation following visual cortical deprivation, identified the vertical luminance gradient as a key feature for cortical scene processing, and demonstrated specialized processing in the occipital place area for visually guided navigation.

0126.name: Christoph Bledowski
0126.institution: Institute of Medical Psychology, Goethe University Frankfurt am Main; Cooperative Brain Imaging Center, Frankfurt am Main
0126.bio: Dr. Fiset investigates the perceptual mechanisms underlying face processing, specifically focusing on the visual information used for face and facial expression recognition. Her research addresses how spatial frequencies and viewing distance influence facial expression categorization, and how individual differences impact these processes. Fiset's significant contributions include demonstrating the critical role of mid-range spatial frequencies in facial expression recognition and developing the "Bubbles" technique to reveal diagnostic visual information used in face perception.

0127.name: Christopher DiMattina
0127.bio: Dr. Kaiser investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception of complex, naturalistic scenes, with a focus on how statistical regularities shape visual processing. Their research addresses how the brain efficiently represents structured natural scenes and integrates fragmented visual inputs. Dr. Kaiser's work has demonstrated the role of alpha-frequency feedback in coherent naturalistic vision, revealed neural predictions for material behaviors, and linked integrative processing to perceived beauty in natural images.

0128.name: Christopher L. Striemer
0128.bio: Dr. Linares investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying visual perception, focusing on contrast sensitivity, motion perception, and spatial suppression. Their research aims to elucidate the perceptual deficits observed in schizophrenia, disentangling the contributions of attentional processes, medication effects, and underlying glutamatergic dysfunction. Dr. Linares has significantly contributed to the field through meta-analyses quantifying contrast sensitivity deficits in schizophrenia, development of improved psychophysical methods for measuring spatial suppression, and investigation of potential confounding factors in schizophrenia perception research.

0129.name: Christopher Tyler
0129.bio: Dr. Coates investigates the mechanisms underlying spatial and color vision, particularly in the context of peripheral perception, letter recognition, and crowding. His research addresses the neural and computational underpinnings of visual crowding and its influence on letter recognition and reading, as well as the differences between foveal and peripheral visual processing. Dr. Coates has significantly advanced the field through the development of novel psychophysical paradigms for studying peripheral vision, contributing to computational models of letter recognition, and improving statistical methodologies for analyzing psychophysical data.

0130.name: Chuan Hou
0130.bio: Dr. Schneider's research investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying attention and working memory, particularly within the context of human factors and ergonomics. He examines how these processes operate in complex, real-world work environments and how they are affected by factors such as fatigue, stress, and cognitive load. His work has significantly advanced the understanding of the neural correlates of working memory, the interaction between attention and working memory, and the impact of cognitive load on workplace design, utilizing EEG/ERP methodologies.

0131.name: Chujun Lin
0131.bio: This researcher investigates the functional organization and development of the human visual cortex, with a particular focus on scene perception and navigation. Their work addresses how the adult visual cortex is functionally organized, how this organization develops in childhood, and how it changes in adulthood following brain damage or sensory deprivation. Key contributions include the identification and characterization of the Occipital Place Area (OPA), demonstrating cortical reorganization in adults with macular degeneration, and elucidating the development of scene-selective regions in the human brain.

0132.name: Chunyue Teng
0132.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms of experience-dependent plasticity, particularly focusing on the cognitive effects of action video game play and sensory deprivation. Their research addresses questions regarding how specific experiences, such as video game training or deafness, induce adaptive changes in attention, perception, and cognitive control across the lifespan. Key contributions include demonstrating the enhancement of visual attention and attentional control through action video game play, as well as revealing compensatory plasticity in the visual system of deaf individuals.

0133.name: Cindy Xiong Bearfield
0133.bio: Dr. Fougnie investigates the cognitive architecture underlying capacity limits in visual attention and working memory. Her research addresses fundamental questions regarding the sources of these limitations, the relationship between attention and memory, and the nature of representations guiding these processes. Fougnie's contributions include demonstrating the monitoring of working memory degradation, revealing variability in memory quality independent of stimulus or attention, and developing novel methodologies like the Auditory Reproduction Task.

0134.name: Claus-Christian Carbon
0134.bio: Dr. Wilson investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying attentional control and its influence on visual cognition and memory. His research addresses how attentional control processes enable flexible control of perceptions, memories, and actions, while also exploring the limitations of these processes and their impact on shaping our perception of the world. He has significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating practice effects in visual search, identifying selection biases in attentional cueing, elucidating synchrony effects in memory retrieval, and providing a memory retrieval account of long-term inhibition of return.

0135.name: Clayton E Curtis
0135.institution: Department of Psychology, New York University

0136.name: Clayton Hickey
0136.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and computational mechanisms underlying perceptual decision-making, with a particular emphasis on confidence and metacognition. Their work addresses how individuals form confidence judgments, how these judgments relate to objective performance and task difficulty, and how confidence influences subsequent behavior and cognitive control. A significant contribution has been demonstrating the role of confidence as a priority signal in decision-making, alongside the development of computational models of confidence formation in perceptual tasks.

0137.name: Colin Clifford
0137.bio: Dr. Alais investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying multisensory integration, visual perception, and binocular rivalry. Their research addresses questions regarding how the brain combines auditory and visual information, the processes involved in face pareidolia, and the interaction between visual and vestibular signals.  Alais's work has significantly contributed to the understanding of face pareidolia, serial dependence in visual perception, and audiovisual integration, employing psychophysical experiments and neuroimaging techniques.

0138.name: Cong Yu
0138.bio: This researcher investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying human visual perception, with a particular focus on color vision, color constancy, and visual adaptation. Their work addresses how the visual system estimates object properties from retinal signals and the contributions of intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells to visual processing. A significant contribution is the development of quantitative models of color constancy and adaptation, alongside the widely adopted Psychophysics Toolbox for vision research.

0139.name: Corey Bohil
0139.bio: Dr. Osher's research focuses on elucidating the relationship between structural brain connectivity and neural function in the domains of visual perception and attention. Their work addresses the fundamental question of how structural connectivity constrains and predicts functional responses within the brain, particularly in the context of visual processing. A significant contribution is demonstrating that structural connectivity fingerprints can predict cortical selectivity for multiple visual categories and individual functional responses, thereby advancing understanding of structure-function relationships at a fine-grained level.

0140.name: Cory Miller
0140.bio: This researcher focuses on the functional organization and development of neural circuits within the visual cortex, employing systems neuroscience approaches. Their research investigates the synaptic, cellular, and network mechanisms underlying cortical neuron response properties and the influence of visual experience on cortical circuit plasticity. They have significantly contributed to defining the functional architecture of cortical circuits, elucidating rules of intracortical connectivity, and pioneering the use of in vivo imaging to study synaptic organization in the visual cortex.

0141.name: Courtney Turner
0141.bio: The Brainard Lab investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying human color vision and visual perception. Their research addresses how the visual system infers object properties from retinal signals, particularly focusing on color constancy, object identification, and the role of intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells. Significant contributions include the development of computational models of color constancy and appearance, advancements in display characterization, and the co-development of the Psychophysics Toolbox, a widely used software package for vision research.

0142.name: Cristina de la Malla
0142.bio: Dr. Linhardt's research focuses on optimizing and validating population receptive field (pRF) mapping techniques using ultra-high field functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI). He investigates the reproducibility, reliability, and potential biases inherent in pRF mapping methodologies, particularly in the context of clinical applications and data processing pipelines. His contributions include the development of artificial scotoma pRF mapping for assessing visual field deficits and the demonstration of high reproducibility of pRF mapping results at 7T, alongside the optimization of stimulus types for improved retinotopic mapping coverage.

0143.name: Curtis L. Baker Jr.
0143.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying active perception, focusing on the temporal dynamics of visual processing. Their work addresses how the brain integrates information across saccades to construct a stable and coherent representation of the environment, and how attention modulates this process. Their contributions include advancing the understanding of trans-saccadic perception and memory, elucidating the role of temporal integration in visual cognition, and characterizing the neural basis of natural perceptual representations.

0144.name: Daniel Baker
0144.bio: Dr. Soto investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying conscious and non-conscious processing in domains such as perception, learning, memory, and language. Their research seeks to delineate the functional distinctions between conscious and non-conscious processes, elucidate the neural substrates of metacognition, and characterize the interplay between visual attention and working memory. Dr. Soto's contributions include advancing the understanding of working memory and attention interactions, developing novel methodologies for studying unconscious cognition, and characterizing the neurodevelopmental basis of metacognition using neuroimaging and psychophysical techniques.

0145.name: Daniel D. Dilks
0145.bio: Dr. Souto investigates the interplay between dynamic vision, eye movements, and visuo-motor learning in both controlled laboratory settings and ecologically valid virtual environments. Their research addresses fundamental questions regarding motion perception during pursuit, the neural mechanisms underlying visuo-motor adaptation, and the cognitive processes involved in real-world tasks like road crossing. Souto's work has revealed asymmetries in motion integration during pursuit, demonstrated use-dependent plasticity in inhibitory control through gaze-typing, and advanced the understanding of eye movement strategies in road-crossing, while also contributing to the development of gaze-controlled assistive communication technologies.

0146.name: Daniel Denman
0146.institution: University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus
0146.department: Medical Scientist Training Program; Neuroscience Graduate Program; Department of Physiology and Biophysics
0146.bio: This researcher focuses on the neural mechanisms underlying scene perception and object recognition within the human visual cortex. Their work investigates how functional connectivity and fine-grained organization in visual cortex relate to perceptual processes, particularly during naturalistic viewing. A significant contribution includes pioneering connectopic mapping techniques to reveal functional gradients in human primary visual cortex and demonstrating the representation of spatial and non-spatial properties in scene-selective regions.

0147.name: Daniel Fiset
0147.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, attention, and visually guided action in complex, dynamic environments. Their work addresses how the visual system localizes objects, processes motion, and extracts statistical summaries from cluttered scenes, while also compensating for inherent neural processing delays. Key contributions include the discovery of serial dependence in perception, advancements in understanding ensemble perception, and elucidating the impact of visual crowding on object recognition.

0148.name: Daniel Kaiser
0148.department: Center for Mind, Brain, and Behavior
0148.bio: Dr. Lindsey investigates human color vision and color cognition, employing psychophysical and neuroimaging techniques. His research addresses the neural mechanisms underlying color perception, the influence of language on color categorization, and the evolutionary origins of color vision. He has significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating universal motifs in color naming across cultures, developing statistical methods for analyzing color naming data, and proposing the lens-brunescence hypothesis.

0149.name: Daniel Linares
0149.department: Vision and Control of Action Group; Department of Cognition, Development, and Psychology of Education; Institute of Neurosciences
0149.bio: Dr. Pelli investigates the perceptual and neural mechanisms underlying object recognition, reading, and crowding in human vision. His research addresses the limits of visual sensitivity and acuity by examining how crowding affects feature integration and letter identification. He has significantly contributed to the field through the development of the Pelli-Robson Contrast Sensitivity Chart, pioneering work on crowding as a fundamental limit to object recognition, and the creation of the VideoToolbox software for visual psychophysics.

0150.name: Daniel R Coates
0150.bio: Dr. Wu investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying numerical cognition, linguistic representations, and verbal short-term memory, utilizing both behavioral and neuroimaging techniques. Their research aims to elucidate the functional and anatomical substrates of magnitude concepts, long-term linguistic storage, and the cognitive processes involved in maintaining verbal information in short-term memory. Dr. Wu's contributions include characterizing the neural correlates of number word merging, identifying universal brain signatures of proficient reading across languages, and exploring the cognitive mechanisms of Chinese character identification.

0151.name: Daniel Schneider
0151.institution: Leibniz Research Centre for Working Environment and Human Factors
0151.bio: Dr. Arnold investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, focusing on the integration of sensory information and its relationship to decision-making and confidence. Her research addresses how the brain constructs conscious visual experiences, particularly exploring the effects of adaptation, prediction, and individual differences in visual imagery. Arnold's work has significantly advanced the understanding of motion-induced spatial conflict, binocular rivalry, and the neural correlates of aphantasia, contributing novel experimental paradigms for studying perception and confidence.

0152.name: Danny D. Dilks
0152.bio: Dr. Beck investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual attention, awareness, and perceptual organization. Her research addresses how attentional selection, capacity limitations, and real-world statistical regularities shape visual representations. Dr. Beck's work has significantly contributed to understanding competitive interactions in visual cortex, elucidating neural mechanisms of visual awareness, and demonstrating the impact of statistical regularities on visual processing.

0153.name: Daphne Bavelier
0153.department: Faculty of Psychology and Educational Science
0153.bio: Dr. Dövencioğlu investigates the perceptual mechanisms underlying 3D shape and material property perception, focusing on both visual and haptic modalities. Her research addresses how sensory cues, exploratory procedures, and cross-modal interactions contribute to the construction of object representations. Her work has significantly advanced the understanding of softness perception as a multidimensional construct and illuminated the role of sound symbolism in cross-modal sensory integration.

0154.name: Daryl Fougnie
0154.bio: Dr. Samaras' research focuses on developing robust computational models for analyzing visual data across computer vision, computer graphics, and medical imaging. He investigates the interaction between 3D shape and illumination, applies machine learning to complex visual data like fMRI and pathology images, and creates statistical models of non-rigid motion. His pioneering contributions include modeling 3D shape and illumination, developing machine learning frameworks for fMRI analysis, and advancing facial appearance modeling and computational pathology.

0155.name: Daryl Wilson
0155.bio: This researcher investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying high-level sensory perception of complex, real-world visual environments. Their work addresses how the brain performs scene categorization and recognition, with a particular focus on the role of mid-level visual features and task context in shaping neural representations. Key contributions include demonstrating the sufficiency of simple line drawings for fMRI decoding of scene categories, identifying the importance of contour junctions in scene representation, and developing computational tools for analyzing structural properties of real-world images.

0156.name: Dasha Zdvizhkova
0156.bio: Dr. Kerzel investigates the cognitive and oculomotor mechanisms underlying visual attention and object perception. Their research addresses how attention is allocated in complex scenes, the factors influencing attentional capture, and the interplay between eye movements and visual processing. Kerzel's work has demonstrated top-down modulation of attentional capture, elucidated the relationship between smooth pursuit and motion perception, and advanced understanding of representational momentum and the role of eye movements in visual search.

0157.name: David Aguilar-Lleyda
0157.department: Vision and Control of Action Group; Department of Cognition, Development, and Psychology of Education; Institute of Neurosciences
0157.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual attention and distractor suppression, particularly focusing on the role of statistical learning. Their work addresses how learned regularities in the environment, including spatial and feature-based expectations, modulate attentional selection and the proactive inhibition of irrelevant information. They have demonstrated that statistical learning leads to proactive distractor suppression mediated by distinct neural mechanisms, challenging existing models of anticipatory attentional control.

0158.name: David Alais
0158.bio: Dr. Rahnev investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying perceptual decision-making and visual metacognition. His research addresses how the brain constructs subjective visual reality from ambiguous sensory input, focusing on the sources of suboptimality in perceptual decisions and the hierarchical organization of the prefrontal cortex. Dr. Rahnev's contributions include demonstrating that perceptual noise can paradoxically increase confidence, developing improved models of visual metacognition, and establishing a hierarchical structure in the prefrontal cortex for perceptual decision-making, as well as creating the Confidence Database.

0159.name: David Brainard
0159.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying the perception of magnitudes, particularly focusing on time processing and its interaction with space and number. Their work seeks to elucidate the neural substrates and functional organization involved in temporal perception, estimation, and integration with spatial and numerical information. This research has demonstrated a functional hierarchy for space and time processing in the cortex and identified distinct neural encoding mechanisms for time across different brain regions, contributing to a deeper understanding of magnitude representation.

0160.name: David E Osher
0160.bio: Dora Hermes investigates the neural substrates of human perception and cognition using computational cognitive neuroscience approaches. Her research addresses fundamental questions regarding the role of neural oscillations in perception and cognition, the development of brain-computer interfaces, and the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception. Her work contributes to improved understanding of human brain function and the development of advanced neuroimaging and electrophysiology techniques for clinical applications, particularly in epilepsy and neuroprosthetics.

0161.name: David Fitzpatrick
0161.institution: Max Planck Florida Institute for Neuroscience
0161.bio: Based on the provided information, a three-sentence technical summary is not possible. The provided text lacks specific details regarding the individual's research focus, questions, and contributions. More information is needed to generate an accurate and informative summary.

0162.name: DAVID LEOPOLD
0162.department: Section on Cognitive Neurophysiology and Imaging; Neurophysiology Imaging Facility
0162.bio: Dr. Awh's research investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual working memory and selective attention, with a focus on their interaction. His work addresses questions regarding the capacity limits of visual working memory, the neural substrates of attentional control, and the relationship between working memory and long-term memory. Awh's significant contributions include demonstrating item limits in visual working memory, developing neural decoding techniques to track online memories, and identifying distinct neural signatures for spatial attention and working memory encoding.

0163.name: David Linhardt
0163.institution: High Field MR Center, Medical University of Vienna
0163.department: Center for Medical Physics and Biomedical Engineering
0163.bio: This cognitive neuroscientist investigates the interplay between visual working memory and selective attention, focusing on the neural mechanisms that govern working memory capacity and precision. Their research addresses how the brain represents and maintains visual information, examining individual differences in working memory and attentional control. Awh's work has significantly advanced the field through the development of the contralateral delay activity (CDA) as a neural correlate of visual working memory, demonstrating fixed capacity limits, and elucidating the roles of attentional selection, distractor suppression, and alpha-band oscillations in working memory processes.

0164.name: David Melcher
0164.department: Psychology Program, Division of Science; Center for Brain and Health, NYUAD Research Institute
0164.bio: Dr. Ester investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual working memory and its role in flexible behavior. Their research addresses how working memory represents and integrates diverse information sources, and how individual differences in working memory relate to broader cognitive functions. Dr. Ester's contributions include demonstrating the decodability of visual working memory representations from early visual cortex activity and elucidating the role of retrospective cues in mitigating information loss during working memory storage.

0165.name: David Soto
0165.institution: Basque Center on Cognition, Brain and Language
0165.bio: Dr. Vogel investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual attention and working memory, with a focus on individual differences. His research addresses the neural basis of capacity limitations in visual working memory and the interaction between attention and working memory systems. Vogel's work has demonstrated a fixed capacity limit of visual working memory, developed electrophysiological measures for assessing working memory load, and revealed a strong relationship between working memory capacity and fluid intelligence.

0166.name: David Souto
0166.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying the interaction between attention and visual working memory, with a focus on individual differences in cognitive capacity. Their work addresses how the brain controls access to working memory, filters irrelevant information, and the electrophysiological markers associated with these processes. A significant contribution is the discovery and characterization of the Contralateral Delay Activity (CDA) as an electrophysiological correlate of visual working memory storage, alongside demonstrating capacity limits and neural signatures of individual differences.

0167.name: David Watson
0167.bio: This researcher investigates the neural representations underlying object recognition and visual perception within the occipitotemporal cortex. Their work addresses how object space is organized, focusing on the organizational principles governing neural representations of visual stimuli and the interaction between category-level distinctions and low-level visual properties. They have demonstrated the primacy of category over shape in cortical organization and identified distinct networks for position-tolerant action representations, contributing novel fMRI analysis techniques for decoding semantic information.

0168.name: David Whitney
0168.bio: This researcher investigates the interplay between perceptual organization, visual aesthetics, and individual differences in perception. Their work aims to elucidate the mechanisms underlying aesthetic preferences and Prägnanz, particularly concerning the influence of order and complexity. Their contributions include developing a systematic framework for studying the relationship between order, complexity, and aesthetic appreciation, advancing methods for investigating individual differences in visual working memory and aesthetic preferences, and applying Bayesian techniques to model perceptual and aesthetic evaluations.

0169.name: Delwin Lindsey
0169.bio: Dr. Merriam investigates the neural basis of visual perception and attention, focusing on the functional organization of the visual cortex. His research addresses how visual information is represented and processed in the human brain, and how different visual areas interact to support visual cognition. His significant contributions include demonstrating the attentional modulation of microsaccades, modeling facial viewpoint selectivity, revealing coarse-scale orientation selectivity in human visual cortex using natural scenes, and showing representational drift in V1 with stable downstream readouts.

0170.name: Denis G Pelli
0170.bio: Dr. Akyürek investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying working memory, selective attention, and temporal integration in visual perception. Her research addresses how the brain integrates information over time to form coherent percepts and how attention interacts with working memory. Akyürek's work has significantly advanced the understanding of temporal integration in vision, feature-based selection in attention, and the EEG/ERP correlates of visual cognitive processes.

0171.name: Denise Hsien Wu
0171.bio: Dr. Striem-Amit investigates the principles of brain organization and plasticity by examining neural processing in individuals with congenital sensory and motor deprivation. Her research addresses how early-onset deprivation shapes cortical function, focusing on the extent and nature of brain reorganization in the absence of typical sensory or motor experience. Striem-Amit's work has demonstrated that individuals born without hands recruit hand-related motor cortex for foot control and revealed unique, stable, individual-specific connectivity patterns within the visual cortex of congenitally blind individuals.

0172.name: Dennis Levi
0172.bio: Dr. Bricolo investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual attention, spatial cognition, and their interactions with other sensory modalities. Her research addresses questions regarding the neural correlates of spatial neglect, the relationship between eye movements and cognitive processes, and how visual illusions reveal fundamental principles of perception. Dr. Bricolo's contributions include demonstrating the interaction between numbers and action-related processes, investigating visuo-haptic integration in spatial neglect, and exploring the uncanny valley effect in virtual faces, utilizing methodologies such as eye-tracking, EEG, and computational modeling.

0173.name: Derek Arnold
0173.bio: Dr. Cooper's research focuses on the adaptive mechanisms of human stereoscopic vision and its implications for display technology design. Their work investigates how 3D vision functions in natural environments, explores the perceptual eyebox for near-eye displays, and examines the relationship between natural image statistics and perceptual stability. Dr. Cooper's contributions include demonstrating the adaptive nature of stereopsis for natural environments, revealing the influence of camera focal length on depth perception, and advancing accommodation-invariant computational near-eye displays.

0174.name: Diane M. Beck
0174.bio: Dr. Grossman investigates the cognitive and neural substrates of visual perception, with a specific focus on biological motion processing. Her research aims to elucidate the neural mechanisms underlying the perception of human actions and social interactions, particularly the interplay between visual and motor systems. Dr. Grossman's work has significantly advanced the field through the identification of key brain regions, such as the posterior superior temporal sulcus (pSTS), involved in biological motion perception and by demonstrating the role of the motor system in visual action understanding.

0176.name: Dimitris Samaras
0176.bio: Emily Martin investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying face perception, utilizing computational modeling techniques. Her research addresses how emotional expressions and familiarity modulate face recognition processes, aiming to elucidate the underlying computational architecture. While still in progress, her work contributes to the development and application of computational models for understanding visual processing, specifically within the domain of face perception.

0178.name: Dirk Bernhardt-Walther
0178.bio: Dr. Günseli investigates the interplay between working memory, episodic memory, and attention to elucidate the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying goal-directed behavior. His research addresses how long-term memory guides attention through reactivation in working memory, and how context influences memory retrieval processes. Günseli's work has demonstrated the involvement of the hippocampus and medial prefrontal cortex in preparing for attentional states guided by memory, and revealed that task-irrelevant context changes trigger long-term memory reactivation in working memory.

0179.name: Dirk Kerzel
0179.bio: Dr. Freud investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, object recognition, and visuomotor control. Her research addresses how these visual functions develop, are implemented in the brain, and are disrupted by brain damage or aging, with a particular focus on the interface between object recognition and visuomotor control. Dr. Freud's work has demonstrated dissociable neural representations for grasping real objects versus images, revealed the large-scale organization of shape processing in ventral and dorsal visual pathways, and identified impairments in face perception due to face masks.

0180.name: Dirk van Moorselaar
0180.bio: Erich Graf investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual and multisensory scene perception. His research addresses how humans resolve perceptual ambiguity, integrate multisensory information, and utilize prior knowledge to form coherent representations of the external world. Graf's significant contributions include demonstrating the malleability of perceptual priors through experience and advancing the understanding of multisensory integration in 3D world modeling.

0181.name: Dobromir Rahnev
0181.bio: Dr. Blaser's research investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual attention, object perception, and perceptual learning across the lifespan, with a particular focus on infant cognition and development. This work addresses fundamental questions regarding how humans visually track and identify objects, the nature of visual attention, and the development of visual cognition in infants and children, often in the context of Autism Spectrum Disorder. Dr. Blaser's contributions include the development of novel methodologies for studying visual attention and object tracking, advancements in understanding infant visual working memory, and elucidating perceptual learning mechanisms related to depth and scale perception.

0182.name: Domenica Bueti
0182.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying motor skill learning, cognitive-motor interactions, and the impact of aging on cognitive functions. They address questions regarding the neural substrates of motor skill acquisition and retention, the effects of exercise on cognitive decline in aging, and the interaction between consciousness states and cognitive processes. Their contributions include developing a cognitive function database for predicting cognitive changes in aging, applying information theory to standardize cognitive performance measures, and demonstrating the superior benefits of moderate-intensity exercise on cognitive abilities.

0183.name: Dominique Lamy
0183.bio: This researcher focuses on the cognitive neuropsychology of face recognition, particularly in the context of developmental prosopagnosia (DP). Their research investigates the underlying mechanisms of DP, effective assessment methods, and the relationship between subjective and objective measures of face recognition ability. They have contributed to the field through the validation of the Danish PI20, a comprehensive scoping review of DP research, and investigations into the correlation between self-reported and performance-based face recognition.

0184.name: Dora Hermes
0184.department: Department of Physiology and Biomedical Engineering
0184.bio: Dr. Gürcan investigates the developmental cognitive neuroscience of social cognition, particularly focusing on theory of mind and its atypical manifestations in autism spectrum disorders. Her research addresses how neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying social cognition are shaped by both typical development and cultural contexts. Gürcan's work has significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating cultural variations in theory of mind development, identifying atypical neural activation patterns in autistic children during social tasks, and pioneering eye-tracking paradigms for early autism risk assessment.

0185.name: Doreen Hii
0185.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying human perception of and interaction with artificial agents, particularly robots. Their work addresses questions regarding mind attribution to non-human entities and the modulation of human-robot interaction by agent appearance and behavior. They have demonstrated differential effects of agent characteristics on mind perception and social attention, and have developed implicit measures for assessing mind perception in social robotics.

0186.name: Dwight J. Kravitz
0186.institution: The George Washington University; US National Science Foundation
0186.bio: Dr. Wilson investigates the cognitive and developmental underpinnings of self-dehumanization, particularly within the context of moral psychology. Her research seeks to elucidate how children attribute human qualities to themselves, especially when engaging in immoral acts or experiencing low-power situations, and to compare these processes to those observed in adults. Dr. Wilson's primary contribution to date involves extending the study of self-dehumanization to child populations, developing novel methodologies for examining this phenomenon in young participants.

0187.name: Dylan Hunter
0187.institution: the University of Texas at San Antonio
0187.bio: Dr. Soto investigates the neurocomputational mechanisms underlying visual cognition, particularly face and object perception, learning, and generalization. His research addresses how faces and objects are encoded in the brain, how learning modifies these representations, and how existing visual representations shape subsequent learning processes. Dr. Soto's contributions include developing computational models of visual categorization, applying General Recognition Theory to face perception, and demonstrating the role of error-driven associative learning in view-invariant object recognition across species.

0188.name: E. J. Green
0188.bio: This researcher focuses on the development and application of objective perimetry techniques for the early detection and characterization of retinal diseases such as diabetic retinopathy and age-related macular degeneration. Their research aims to elucidate the structural and functional changes in the retina associated with these diseases and to optimize objective perimetry for improved diagnostic accuracy. A significant contribution includes the development and validation of multifocal pupillographic objective perimetry (mfPOP) and demonstrating its diagnostic capabilities in early-stage retinal pathologies.

0189.name: Ed Awh
0189.bio: Dr. Děchtěrenko investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual perception, attention, and memory for complex scenes. Their research addresses how humans process, store, and recall visual information, with a focus on the roles of eye movements, attention allocation, and scene typicality. They have significantly contributed to the field through the development of scanpath coherence analysis methods, the demonstration of false memory effects for visual scenes, and the application of convolutional neural networks to quantify perceptual similarity.

0190.name: Edward Awh
0190.bio: Dr. Durgin's research investigates the psychophysical mechanisms underlying spatial perception, particularly the perception of surface orientation, distance, and perceptual constancy during self-motion. Her work addresses how cognitive factors and experimental demand characteristics influence perceptual judgments and how perceived spatial layout relates to actual spatial layout. Durgin's contributions include demonstrating systematic biases in slant perception, developing models of self-motion's impact on visual perception, and advancing theories on the functional purposes of perceptual biases in spatial cognition.

0191.name: Edward F Ester
0191.bio: Dr. Tong investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, attention, and object recognition, with a focus on the interplay between early visual representations and higher-order cognitive processes. His research addresses how early visual representations contribute to cognitive operations and how attention and working memory modulate processing in early visual areas. Tong's work has significantly advanced the field through the development of fMRI decoding methods, characterization of visual working memory, and elucidation of object-based attentional selection, including pioneering studies on binocular rivalry and visual awareness.

0192.name: Edward K. Vogel
0192.bio: This researcher investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying visual perception, particularly focusing on motion, time, and spatial processing. Their work addresses how the brain integrates self-motion cues with visual input and how eye movements modulate attention and perception. Key contributions include elucidating the principles of motion aftereffects and bi-vectorial transparent motion perception, as well as pioneering the use of virtual reality and eye-tracking methodologies to study perception in dynamic environments.

0193.name: Eero P. Simoncelli
0193.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying the proactive use of working memory for anticipatory behavior and goal-directed action. Their work addresses how attention is dynamically allocated within working memory and how this interacts with action planning to facilitate efficient cognitive processing. Key contributions include demonstrating the utility of microsaccades as markers of internal attention in working memory and revealing the action-oriented nature of visual working memory through neural oscillatory signatures.

0194.name: Elahe' Yargholi
0194.bio: Dr. Gosselin investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying object and face recognition, with a particular emphasis on individual differences in mental representations. His research addresses questions regarding the role of low-level visual features in memory, the processing of spatio-temporal information, and the neural correlates of perceptual skill. He is most recognized for developing the "Bubbles" technique to reveal the visual information used in recognition, and for elucidating the importance of specific facial features and their temporal dynamics in face recognition.

0195.name: Eline Van Geert
0195.bio: Dr. Domini investigates the computational mechanisms underlying visual perception, particularly in the context of 3D vision and sensorimotor integration. His research addresses how the visual system extracts 3D information from 2D retinal images, integrates visual and haptic information, and influences motor planning and execution. He is renowned for developing the Intrinsic Constraint Model for stereo-motion integration and demonstrating that the visual system relies on heuristics and task-relevant information rather than veridical 3D reconstruction.

0196.name: Elisha Merriam
0196.department: National Institutes of Health; National Institute of Mental Health
0196.bio: This researcher investigates the neurobiological mechanisms underlying individual differences in literacy acquisition and learning disabilities, particularly dyslexia, using developmental cognitive neuroscience approaches. Their research aims to elucidate the neural bases of these differences, exploring the interplay of genetic and environmental factors in brain development and the potential of neuroimaging for predicting reading outcomes. A significant contribution has been the identification of neurobiological signatures of dyslexia independent of reading level, alongside the development of predictive models of reading outcomes using multimodal neuroimaging and the elucidation of compensatory mechanisms.

0197.name: Elissa L. Newport
0197.institution: Center for Brain Plasticity and Recovery, Georgetown University Medical Center
0197.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive neuroscience of high-level vision, specifically focusing on the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying face and person recognition. Their research addresses how perceptual, cognitive, and social systems integrate to accomplish person recognition, and how different person-related cues are processed and represented. This work has identified critical facial features for recognition, developed a framework for whole-person perception, and demonstrated the significant role of conceptual and social information in person recognition, contributing to a more comprehensive understanding of face processing areas.

0198.name: Elkan G. Akyürek
0198.bio: Dr. Brewer's research focuses on the interplay between attention, working memory, and long-term memory systems in the context of goal-directed behavior. His work investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying prospective memory, the influence of individual differences in cognitive abilities on task performance, and the interaction of attention and memory processes. Brewer's contributions include the development of validated cognitive tasks for measuring working memory and attention control, elucidating the role of working memory in prospective memory, and demonstrating how emotional and motivational factors modulate cognitive control and memory.

0199.name: Ella Striem-Amit
0199.bio: The Aguirre Lab investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, with a focus on retinal and cortical processing in both healthy individuals and those with neurological or ophthalmological disorders. Their research addresses questions regarding the organization and function of the human visual cortex, the impact of disease on visual perception, and the development of advanced neuroimaging techniques for studying brain function. Aguirre's group is particularly recognized for pioneering the use of fMRI for retinotopic mapping, developing multivariate pattern analysis techniques, and elucidating neural mechanisms of face perception and visual processing changes in blindness.

0200.name: Emanuela Bricolo
0200.bio: Dr. Woodman investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual attention, working memory, and cognitive control, focusing on how the brain selectively processes and stores visual information during complex scene processing. His research addresses questions regarding the interaction between visual working memory and long-term memory in influencing attentional control, as well as the neural correlates of figure-ground segregation. He is recognized for pioneering the use of event-related potentials to elucidate the rapid deployment of attention during visual search and for developing novel applications of transcranial direct-current stimulation to enhance cognitive functions.

0201.name: Emily A Cooper
0201.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive neuroscience of attention, perception, and awareness, focusing on the neural mechanisms underlying attentional selection and control. Their work addresses how top-down attentional control processes interact with bottom-up sensory processing to influence perception and awareness. A significant contribution includes pioneering the use of event-related brain potentials (ERPs) and combined EEG/fMRI to characterize the functional neuroanatomy of attentional control networks and develop models of top-down attentional modulation.

0202.name: Emily D Grossman
0202.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, attention, and short-term memory. Their work addresses fundamental questions regarding how the visual system processes information within brief temporal windows, the filtering mechanisms of visual attention, and the neural substrates of motion perception. Their seminal contributions include the discovery of iconic memory, the development of mathematical models of visual processes, the characterization of distinct motion perception systems, and the demonstration of quantal shifts in visual attention.

0203.name: Emily Oor
0203.bio: This researcher investigates the perceptual and oculomotor consequences of augmented reality displays on binocular vision. Their work addresses fundamental questions regarding accommodative control mechanisms under various clinical conditions and the impact of binocular misalignment in see-through augmented reality. Key contributions include demonstrating the sensory-motor balance in compensating for vertical misalignment in AR, developing improved models of accommodative control, and elucidating the neural mechanisms underlying the flash-lag effect using TMS.

0204.name: Emily R. Martin
0204.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual cognition, particularly focusing on attention and working memory processes. Their work addresses how the brain constructs and maintains stable visual representations, examining the influence of categorical and metric information on these representations. A significant contribution is the development of EEG-based decoding methods to track visual information with high temporal resolution, revealing neural evidence for categorical biases and dissociating spatial attention from working memory.

0205.name: Enrico Guarnuto
0205.bio: This researcher investigates the interplay between form and motion processing, and the influence of local and global visual information on perceptual organization. Their work addresses how these interactions reveal the functional architecture of the visual system and the neural mechanisms underlying conscious visual experiences. Key contributions include the discovery of the Dynamic Ebbinghaus illusion, demonstrating the role of non-local processing in perceptual grouping, and elucidating mechanisms underlying perceived angular velocity and the role of contour discontinuities in motion processing.

0206.name: Eren Günseli
0206.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying tool use, object affordances, and human-technology interaction. Their work addresses how humans perceive and interact with tools, the neural correlates of these interactions, and the influence of embodied cognition on technology use. They have significantly contributed to the field by pioneering research on the ecological approach to tool affordances, elucidating the role of mechanical and semantic knowledge in tool understanding, and advancing theoretical frameworks integrating embodied cognition and technological evolution.

0207.name: Erez Freud
0207.department: Department of Psychology, Centre for Vision Research
0207.bio: This individual's background does not indicate any research focus within vision science. Therefore, I cannot provide a three-sentence technical summary based on the provided information. The data describes a career trajectory in finance and investment, not vision science.

0208.name: Erich Graf
0208.bio: Dr. Zelinsky investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual attention, search, and scene perception using oculomotor behavior as a primary measure. His research addresses how humans allocate visual attention during complex tasks and the relationships between visual attention, working memory, and scene understanding. Zelinsky's significant contributions include developing computational models of visual search integrating eye movements and attention, demonstrating how eye fixation patterns reveal information about search targets, and elucidating the role of visual working memory in guiding attention.

0209.name: Erik Blaser
0209.bio: Dr. Zelinsky investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual search, attention, and scene perception using eye movement analysis. His research addresses how object representations, scene context, and peripheral vision influence gaze patterns and task performance in real-world environments. Zelinsky's significant contributions include developing computational models of visual search integrating eye movements and attention, pioneering eye tracking methodologies for real-world tasks, and bridging human and computer vision through neurocomputational modeling.

0211.name: Erling Nørkær
0211.bio: Dr. Maiello investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying binocular vision, depth perception, and visually-guided grasping. Their research addresses how the brain integrates sensory information to resolve ambiguities and guide motor behavior, particularly in the context of naturalistic tasks. Dr. Maiello's contributions include computational models predicting grasp locations, characterization of blur perception across the visual field, and elucidation of peripheral blur's role in binocular fusion.

0212.name: Esin Gürcan
0212.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual motion processing and sensorimotor integration in primates. Their work addresses how cortical networks implement dynamic visual processing, particularly concerning motion information and its influence on oculomotor responses. Key contributions include characterizing the behavioral receptive field for ocular following, demonstrating anticipatory responses in V1, and developing computational models of motion integration and normalization.

0213.name: Eva Wiese
0213.bio: Dr. Balaban investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying dynamic information processing, particularly at the intersection of visual working memory and intuitive physics. Her research addresses how visual working memory tracks moving objects, how intuitive physical expectations influence visual processing, and the capacity limits of mental simulation. Her work has demonstrated context-dependent updating of visual working memory, identified neural correlates of object continuity disruption, and revealed the serial nature of mental simulation, significantly advancing our understanding of the interplay between visual cognition and physical reasoning.

0214.name: Evelyn Wilson
0214.bio: Dr. Öǧmen's research focuses on the dynamic processes underlying visual perception, particularly the construction of coherent percepts from rapidly changing sensory input. His work investigates the neural mechanisms of visual masking, the synthesis of reference frames, and the temporal dynamics of sensory and working memory. A significant contribution is the development of influential models of visual masking phenomena, including metacontrast masking, and the proposal of a non-retinotopic framework for understanding motion perception and object recognition.

0215.name: Fabian Soto
0215.bio: Dr. Block investigates the neural mechanisms underlying sensorimotor integration and adaptation, with a particular focus on how the brain utilizes multisensory information to control movement and update body representation during skill learning. Her research addresses questions regarding the roles of specific brain regions, such as the cerebellum and parietal lobe, in sensorimotor processing and how these processes are altered following neurological injury. Dr. Block's contributions include elucidating the integration of visual and proprioceptive information in motor control, characterizing changes in body representation during skill acquisition, and applying non-invasive brain stimulation to probe and modulate sensorimotor circuits.

0216.name: Fang Fang
0216.institution: School of Psychological and Cognitive Sciences and Beijing Key Laboratory of Behavior and Mental Health, Peking University, Beijing, China
0216.department: Key Laboratory of Machine Perception; Peking-Tsinghua Center for Life Sciences; IDG/McGovern Institute for Brain Research
0216.bio: Dr. Op de Beeck investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual cognition, learning, and plasticity, with a focus on object representation and categorization. His research addresses how the brain processes visual information, how visual expertise develops, and how neurodevelopmental disorders affect visual processing. He has significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating the plasticity of object representations in extrastriate cortex, elucidating neural correlates of visual expertise, and advancing understanding of ventral stream organization using multimodal approaches.

0217.name: Faran Sabeti
0217.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive neuroscience of episodic memory, focusing on how individuals manage memory limitations through decision-making and information prioritization. Their work addresses the neural, cognitive, and social factors influencing learning and memory, particularly the dynamic interplay between eye movements and memory processes. They have demonstrated the hippocampus' role in saccadic information gathering and elucidated age-related differences in conceptual combination as an encoding strategy.

0218.name: Felix Franke
0218.institution: Institute of Molecular and Clinical Ophthalmology Basel; University of Basel, Faculty of Science; Eidgenössische Technische Hochschule Zürich, Department of Biosystems Science and Engineering
0218.bio: Dr. Slagter investigates the neural mechanisms underlying selective attention, conscious perception, and the modulatory influence of experience, particularly meditation training, on adaptive cognitive control. Her research addresses questions regarding the plasticity of core cognitive functions, the relationship between predictive processing and consciousness, and the potential of non-invasive brain stimulation to modulate these processes. Dr. Slagter's significant contributions include demonstrating attentional improvements following intensive meditation, developing a predictive processing account of deconstructive meditation, and elucidating the role of the ventral striatum in conscious perception and distractor suppression.

0219.name: Filip Děchtěrenko
0219.bio: Dr. Nienborg investigates the neural mechanisms underlying perceptual decision-making, focusing on the integration of cognitive and sensory signals within the visual cortex. Her research addresses how non-visual contextual cues, neuromodulation, and feedback influence visual encoding and guide behavior in mammalian brains. Nienborg's work has demonstrated that decision-related activity in sensory neurons is not solely driven by their causal influence on decisions and has elucidated the role of feedback in shaping sensory representations during perceptual decision-making.

0220.name: Frank Bremmer
0220.institution: Philipps-Universität Marburg; Universities of Marburg, Giessen, and Darmstadt
0220.department: Applied Physics and Neurophysics; Center for Mind, Brain and Behavior
0220.bio: This researcher investigates the developmental cognitive neuroscience of infant cognition, with a focus on visual statistical learning and face processing. They examine how infants extract statistical regularities, the neural mechanisms underlying face processing, and the influence of perceptual expertise and social cues on infant learning. Their work has demonstrated visual statistical learning abilities in newborns, elucidated the role of perceptual expertise in sequential rule learning, and identified directional biases in infants' sequence learning.

0221.name: Frank H. Durgin
0221.bio: This researcher investigates the genetic and neural circuit mechanisms underlying visual system development and sensorimotor integration. Their work addresses how neuronal circuits process sensory information, particularly visual inputs, to guide behavior in zebrafish models. Key contributions include elucidating molecular mechanisms of retinotectal map formation, identifying neural circuits for visual prey capture and social behavior, and pioneering the use of zebrafish for systems neuroscience through advanced imaging and genetic tools.

0222.name: Frank Tong
0222.department: Psychology Department; Vanderbilt Vision Research Center
0222.bio: This researcher focuses on the structural behavior and design of steel and composite structures, particularly tubular and thin-walled members, within the context of civil engineering. Their research investigates optimal design methodologies, deconstruction and reuse technologies, and the application of circular economy principles to enhance the sustainability and resilience of these structural systems. This scholar has significantly advanced the understanding of structural behavior in tubular and thin-walled steel structures, pioneered the application of circular economy principles in construction, and developed innovative design methodologies for steel and composite structures.

0223.name: Frans Verstraten
0223.bio: Dr. Wiese's research focuses on the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying face recognition, person memory, and the processing of person-related semantic information. His work addresses questions regarding how the brain recognizes familiar faces across variable appearances, the neural basis of own-group biases in face recognition, and lifespan changes in face processing.
