Dr. Wiese has made significant contributions by elucidating ERP correlates of familiar face recognition, demonstrating age-related changes in face memory, and characterizing own-age biases, often employing ecologically valid stimuli and multivariate ERP analyses.

0224.name: Freek van Ede
0224.bio: Dr. Xu investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying face perception and intergroup relations, with a specific focus on cultural influences. Her research addresses how cultural factors modulate face processing strategies, the neural substrates of intergroup biases, and the developmental origins of social categorization. Her work has significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating cultural variations in holistic face processing, identifying neural correlates of own-race bias, and elucidating the developmental trajectory of racial bias across cultures.

0225.name: Frédéric Gosselin
0225.bio: Dr. Sun's research investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual attention, implicit learning, and multisensory integration in spatial perception and navigation. His work addresses fundamental questions regarding how the brain processes visual information to guide attention, how implicit learning shapes visual perception, and how multisensory cues are integrated to inform spatial awareness. His contributions include elucidating the roles of egocentric and allocentric representations in spatial memory, characterizing mechanisms of contextual cueing in visual search, and investigating the integration of visual and non-visual cues in distance estimation and self-motion perception.

0226.name: Fuat Balci
0226.bio: Dr. Lu investigates the computational mechanisms underlying human learning and reasoning, particularly in domains characterized by sparse or ambiguous data. Their research addresses how humans leverage generic priors and structured representations to perform inference, with a focus on perceptual learning, action understanding, causal inference, and analogical reasoning. Dr. Lu's work has demonstrated the critical role of generic priors and structured representations in human inference and has advanced computational models integrating psychological, statistical, and neuroscientific principles to explain causal learning and analogical reasoning.

0227.name: Fulvio Domini
0227.bio: Dr. Li investigates the neural and computational underpinnings of cognitive functions including decision-making, working memory, and visual attention. Their research addresses how humans represent uncertainty during decision-making, how attention modulates perception, the format of working memory representations, and the dynamic transformation of visual memories into behavior. Li's work has demonstrated that mnemonic codes simultaneously represent past stimuli and future behavior, that visual working memory content and uncertainty are jointly encoded in retinotopic areas, and has elucidated distinct computational mechanisms for overt and covert spatial attention.

0228.name: Fumiko Hoeft
0228.bio: Dr. Liao investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying multisensory integration, perceptual decision-making, and social cognition, with a particular emphasis on the interplay between auditory and visual perception. Her research addresses how sensory systems integrate information across modalities, how pupillary responses reflect cognitive states, and how social factors influence perceptual processes. Dr. Liao's work has demonstrated the role of microsaccades in crossmodal integration, characterized pupillary responses to attractiveness perception, and established a correlation between baseline pupil size fluctuations and auditory bistability, providing insights into the physiological correlates of perceptual and cognitive phenomena.

0229.name: Gabriel J. Diaz
0229.institution: Rochester Institute of Technology Center for Imaging Science; University of Rochester Center for Visual Science
0229.bio: Dr. Chen investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual attention, working memory, conscious awareness, and decision-making, with a particular focus on their interactions. Her research addresses how attention and working memory interact during information processing and storage, the neural substrates of conscious awareness, and the cognitive processes involved in numerical cognition and decision-making. Dr. Chen's work has significantly contributed to the field through the discovery of attribute amnesia, the elucidation of the relationship between visual attention and working memory, and the development of novel paradigms for studying conscious awareness.

0231.name: Garikoitz Lerma-Usabiaga
0231.institution: Basque Center on Cognition, Brain & Language; Ikerbasque, Basque Foundation for Science
0231.bio: Dr. Pennock investigates the neural mechanisms underlying color perception and its contribution to object recognition within the ventral visual pathway. His research addresses how color information is represented and processed in the human visual system, particularly concerning its role in food recognition and the function of color-biased regions. His significant contribution lies in demonstrating the food-selective and color-biased nature of specific ventral stream regions, thereby refining our understanding of color's role in object perception.

0232.name: Gene Brewer
0232.bio: Dr. Serrano-Pedraza investigates the functional and neural mechanisms underlying human motion perception, spatial vision, and stereovision. Her research addresses questions regarding the spatio-temporal characteristics of stereoscopic processing, the role of surround suppression, and the neural representation of visual information in working memory. Her work has demonstrated impaired visual surround suppression in schizophrenia, developed novel stereoacuity measurement tools, and elucidated temporal mechanisms in stereomotion, revealing independent neuronal mechanisms underlying different measures of surround suppression.

0233.name: Geoffrey K Aguirre
0233.bio: Dr. Murakami's research investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying motion perception, temporal processing, and visual attention. He seeks to understand how the visual system integrates motion information with eye movements, resolves perceptual ambiguities in visual illusions, and encodes ensemble statistics. His significant contributions include the discovery of the jitter aftereffect, elucidating mechanisms of induced motion perception, and developing models of differential latency in visual processing.

0235.name: George A. Alvarez
0235.institution: Harvard University; Kempner Institute for the Study of Natural and Artificial Intelligence
0235.bio: Dr. Yildirim's research focuses on the computational mechanisms underlying the transformation of sensory inputs into conceptual representations for higher-level cognition. He investigates how minds and brains construct internal models of the physical world, formalizing perceptual and cognitive processes using engineering principles. His contributions include developing computational models bridging perception and cognition in visual object recognition and scene understanding, advancing efficient inverse graphics approaches to explain biological face processing, and integrating physics engines into cognitive models of intuitive physics.

0236.name: George R. Mangun
0236.bio: Dr. Hong investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual search and attention, with a focus on spatial attention, attentional learning, and visual foraging. Her research addresses how the cognitive system efficiently processes complex visual scenes, including mechanisms of search termination, the influence of habit-like attention, and the impact of interruptions on foraging behavior. Dr. Hong's work has demonstrated a distinction between habit-like and goal-driven attentional biases and has shown that secondary tasks during inter-patch travel do not significantly alter patch-leaving decisions in visual foraging.

0237.name: George Sperling
0237.bio: The Fine Lab investigates neural plasticity and sight recovery technologies, focusing on the effects of visual deprivation and the optimization of visual perception in individuals with visual impairments. Their research aims to elucidate the perceptual and neural mechanisms underlying adaptation to sensory loss and to develop computational models for improving retinal prosthesis function. Dr. Fine's significant contributions include pioneering work on cross-modal plasticity, groundbreaking research on sight recovery following long-term blindness, and the development of computational models for retinal prostheses.

0238.name: Gerrit Maus
0238.bio: This researcher investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying higher-level vision, particularly face and object recognition, and visual processing abnormalities in autism spectrum disorder. Their work addresses questions regarding the potential of visual adaptation to restore cortical function, the identification of diagnostic visual signatures in ASD, and the application of artificial intelligence to retinal image analysis. Key contributions include demonstrating adaptation-induced hypersensitivity in face identification, discovering characteristic visual abnormalities in ASD, and advancing deep learning techniques for retinal image analysis to detect systemic and neurodegenerative conditions.

0239.name: Gi-Yeul Bae
0239.bio: Dr. Sperandio investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual size perception, with a particular focus on size constancy and visual illusions. Her research addresses how sensory information is integrated with cognitive and embodied factors to construct stable percepts of object size across varying viewing conditions. Her work has demonstrated the temporal dynamics of size constancy processing, elucidated the roles of top-down and bottom-up mechanisms in visual illusions, and validated the use of afterimages as a tool in size perception research.

0240.name: Gideon Caplovitz
0240.bio: This researcher investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying visual object recognition, with a particular focus on perceptual expertise and category-specific processing. Their research addresses how the visual system organizes itself into specialized modules, the neural changes associated with acquiring expertise, and the influence of individual differences on object recognition. A significant contribution is the demonstration of experience-dependent plasticity in the fusiform face area, challenging the face-specificity hypothesis and establishing the "Greeble" paradigm for studying visual expertise.

0241.name: Giovanni Federico
0241.institution: Università degli Studi Suor Orsola Benincasa

0242.name: Grabriel Kreiman
0242.institution: Boston Children’s Hospital, Harvard Medical School
0242.department: Center for Brains, Minds and Machines
0242.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception and visually-guided behavior, focusing on cortical processing of motion, form, and color. Their work addresses how the brain encodes and decodes visual information, particularly concerning the neural computations involved in visual form processing and the cortical representation of visual information. They have made significant contributions to understanding motion perception, including elucidating the aperture problem, developing quantitative models of neuronal responses, and advancing knowledge of amblyopia's neural basis.

0243.name: Grace Taylor
0243.bio: This researcher focuses on the neural mechanisms underlying information representation and mapping across the cerebral cortex, particularly within visual and language processing. They investigate how the brain encodes and decodes complex, naturalistic stimuli and tasks, aiming to develop computational models that predict brain activity and reconstruct mental experiences. Their significant contributions include pioneering the use of natural stimuli in fMRI, developing advanced brain decoding techniques, and mapping semantic representations across the human cortex, demonstrating the influence of attention on these representations.

0244.name: Gregory J. Zelinsky
0244.bio: Dr. Feldman investigates the computational principles underlying visual perception, perceptual organization, and shape representation. His research addresses how the human visual system organizes complex scenes, forms concepts, and categorizes objects, with a focus on the cognitive mechanisms involved. Feldman's significant contributions include developing mathematical models of perceptual organization, proposing the simplicity principle in concept learning, and advancing Bayesian approaches to shape representation and contour integration.

0245.name: Greta Manini
0245.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying color and form processing in the human visual system. Their work addresses how parallel pathways encode and transmit visual information, and how these pathways develop across the lifespan, including in individuals with autism, schizophrenia, and glaucoma. They have significantly contributed to the field by characterizing parallel pathways using psychophysical and electrophysiological methods, developing techniques to isolate chromatic and luminance pathways, and identifying visual processing deficits in clinical populations.

0246.name: Guido Maiello
0246.bio: Dr. Herman investigates the neural circuits underlying visual perception, attention, and decision-making, with a particular focus on subcortical contributions. His research addresses how interactions between subcortical structures, such as the superior colliculus, and cortical areas support visually-guided behavior and cognitive processes. Dr. Herman's work has demonstrated the role of the superior colliculus in perceptual decisions during attention tasks and has shown that microsaccades are a marker, rather than a cause, of attention-related neural modulation.

0247.name: Guillaume S Masson
0247.institution: Aix-marseille Université - Institut de Neurosciences de la Timone - CNRS - Marseille, France
0247.bio: Dr. Hillis investigates human-machine intelligence within augmented reality, focusing on the interplay between eye movements, spatiotemporal vision, and head-mounted display technologies. His research addresses how perceptual and cognitive factors, particularly those related to eye movements and sensory integration, can be leveraged to optimize human-machine interaction in AR systems. He has significantly contributed to the field through developing models of visual perception and eye movements for AR applications, advancing understanding of sensory integration in 3D perception, and optimizing display technologies for AR/VR systems.

0248.name: Halely Balaban
0248.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying face recognition, perceptual expertise, and their development in both typical and autistic populations. Their work addresses how experience shapes face and object recognition abilities, and explores the efficacy of targeted interventions for improving face processing skills in autism. A significant contribution is the development of the part-whole effect paradigm demonstrating the reliance on configural/holistic processing in face recognition, along with the identification of atypical face processing patterns in autism spectrum disorders and the creation of the "Let's Face It!" intervention program.

0249.name: Haluk Öǧmen
0249.bio: Dr. Wiersema investigates the cognitive and neural underpinnings of autism spectrum disorder (ASD) and attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Her research addresses the mechanisms underlying social cognition deficits, the role of interoception in self-regulation, and the developmental trajectories of error monitoring in these populations. Through EEG and fMRI methodologies, she has demonstrated intact self-face recognition and typical neural processing of social interaction recognition in adults with ASD, while also identifying altered error monitoring processes in ADHD.

0250.name: Hamid Soltanian-Zadeh
0250.bio: Dr. Lewis-Peacock investigates the neural mechanisms underlying working memory, cognitive control, and the interaction between attention and memory. His research aims to elucidate how the brain encodes, maintains, and manipulates mental representations, particularly focusing on the neural correlates of competition and retrieval processes. He has significantly contributed to the field through the development of multivariate pattern analysis techniques for decoding working memory contents from fMRI data and demonstrating the role of competition in forgetting, while also pioneering the use of real-time fMRI neurofeedback for cognitive enhancement.

0251.name: Hannah Block
0251.bio: Dr. Mattingley investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying selective attention, prediction, and decision-making in the human brain. Their research addresses how attentional control, executive function, and multisensory integration regulate perceptual, cognitive, and motor processes, particularly in the context of brain plasticity and neurological disorders. Their significant contributions include elucidating the neural substrates of selective attention and executive control, advancing understanding of multisensory integration, and pioneering investigations into the role of attention in synaesthesia and the mirror neuron system.

0252.name: Hans Op de Beeck
0252.department: Department of Brain and Cognition, Leuven Brain Institute, Faculty of Psychology & Educational Sciences
0252.bio: Dr. Pratt investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual attention, working memory, and their influence on motor control across the lifespan. His research addresses how humans selectively acquire and prioritize visual information, how this information guides actions, and how aging impacts these processes. Pratt's work has significantly advanced understanding of visuomotor control, demonstrating the adaptability of perceptual input for reaching, revealing attentional biases toward animate stimuli, and elucidating age-related differences in visual information processing for limb movements.

0253.name: Heather Lucas
0253.bio: Dr. Saunders investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying 3D visual perception and visuomotor control. Her research addresses how humans integrate visual cues to perceive 3D structure and spatial layout, and how this information is used to guide motor planning and execution. Her contributions include elucidating mechanisms of 3D slant perception, demonstrating the role of continuous visual feedback in visuomotor control, and developing computational models of cue integration in 3D perception.

0254.name: Heiko H. Schütt
0254.bio: This researcher focuses on the psychophysiological and human factors aspects of warfighter performance in extreme operational environments. They investigate the impact of stressors such as prolonged wakefulness, cold exposure, and underwater conditions on cognitive readiness and physiological function, particularly within submarine crews. Their work has advanced the understanding of fatigue and autonomic responses in military personnel, leading to the development of wearable technologies and optimized scheduling strategies to mitigate performance decrements.

0255.name: Heleen A. Slagter
0255.bio: Dr. Wammes investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying learning and memory, with a particular focus on representational change and attentional modulation. Their research addresses how encoding strategies, such as drawing, and attentional states, like mind wandering, influence the formation and retrieval of memory representations. A significant contribution is the establishment of the "drawing effect" and the development of an integrated components model explaining how drawing enhances memory through the integration of elaborative, motoric, and pictorial processing.

0256.name: Hendrikje Nienborg
0256.institution: National Eye Institute, National Institutes of Health
0256.bio: Dr. Ristic investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying human attention, particularly within the domains of social cognition and interactive behavior. Her research addresses how social factors, such as gaze cues and emotional expressions, modulate attentional processes and how these processes develop across the lifespan. Ristic's pioneering work has demonstrated the fundamental role of social experiences in shaping cognition and has advanced the understanding of social attention through innovative experimental paradigms and the integration of cognitive and social perspectives.

0257.name: Hermann Bulf
0257.bio: Dr. Bosten investigates the neural and genetic underpinnings of individual differences in visual perception, with a focus on color vision and spatial information processing. Her research addresses how genetic variations contribute to diverse color experiences and how neural mechanisms mediate these perceptual differences across individuals. Bosten's significant contributions include elucidating the genetic basis of color perception variations, evaluating the efficacy of multi-notch filters for anomalous trichromacy, and developing virtual reality-based visual performance measures for clinical trials.

0258.name: Herwig Baier
0258.institution: Max Planck Institute for Biological Intelligence
0258.bio: This researcher investigates the genetic and neural mechanisms underlying individual differences in human color vision and visual perception. Their research addresses how the visual system calibrates to natural scene statistics, the neural basis of subjective color experience, and the influence of genetic variations on color vision. This work has significantly contributed to understanding the genetic basis of anomalous trichromacy, developing methods for assessing color vision deficiency in children, and advancing knowledge of visual snow syndrome and hallucinogen persisting perception disorder.

0260.name: Holger Wiese
0260.bio: Dr. Wilmer investigates individual differences in cognitive abilities, with a specific focus on face recognition and visual perception. Her research addresses the genetic and environmental contributions to variability in face recognition ability and seeks to develop robust measures of specific cognitive abilities. Her significant contributions include demonstrating the heritability and specificity of face recognition, developing the Cambridge Face Memory Test (CFMT), and pioneering large-scale web-based cognitive testing through TestMyBrain.org.

0261.name: Hong XU
0261.bio: Jeremy Wolfe investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual search and attention, particularly in complex scenes. His research addresses how attentional guidance, target prevalence, and observer expertise influence the efficiency and accuracy of target detection. Wolfe's significant contributions include the development of the Guided Search model, the identification of the prevalence effect, and pioneering work applying visual search principles to improve medical image perception.

0262.name: Hong-jin Sun
0262.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying mathematical and logical reasoning, employing a comparative and developmental approach. Their work addresses questions regarding the ontogenetic and phylogenetic origins of numerical cognition, hierarchical rule learning, and the influence of culture on these abilities. A significant contribution is the demonstration of shared numerical processing substrates between human children and non-human primates, alongside the development of novel methodologies for studying cognitive development in naturalistic settings and challenging populations.

0263.name: Hongjing Lu
0263.bio: Dr. Witt investigates the interplay between action capabilities and visual perception, particularly in the domains of spatial perception and information visualization. Her research addresses how an individual's ability to act influences their perception of spatial properties and how information visualizations can be optimized to facilitate understanding of complex data, especially in medical and environmental contexts. A significant contribution of her work is the demonstration of action-specific perception, where perceptual judgments are modulated by an individual's motor abilities, and the development of decisional clarity as a metric for evaluating decision aid effectiveness.

0264.name: Hsin-Hung Li
0264.bio: Dr. Choi investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual attention and ensemble perception. He seeks to understand how the visual system efficiently processes complex scenes and extracts statistical summaries of visual features. His research has significantly advanced the understanding of ensemble perception through the development of a population response model and has demonstrated the role of target identity in contextual cueing and visual search.

0265.name: Hsin-I Liao
0265.bio: Dr. Song investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying driver behavior, focusing on the interplay between vision, attention, and transportation safety. Her research addresses how depth perception, peripheral vision, and in-vehicle warning systems modulate driver situational awareness and hazard perception. Notably, she developed the Road Hazard Stimuli database and has demonstrated the influence of apparent distance on peripheral target detection, contributing to a better understanding of visual attention in dynamic, real-world environments.

0266.name: Hui Chen
0266.bio: Dr. Chen investigates human perception and cognition within interactive visualization and virtual reality environments. Their research addresses how humans perceive, process, and interact with visual information, particularly in the context of data exploration and analysis. Dr. Chen's contributions include developing novel methods combining cognitive experiments and mathematical modeling to improve visual data analysis, as well as designing innovative interactive metaphors for exploring complex datasets.

0267.name: Hyosun Kim
0267.institution: R&D center, Samsung Display, South Korea
0267.bio: Dr. Zhou investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual deficits associated with functional eye diseases, particularly focusing on binocular vision and visual plasticity. Their research addresses how conditions like amblyopia and myopia impact binocular visual function and explores the potential for leveraging visual plasticity for rehabilitation. Dr. Zhou's significant contributions include demonstrating the utility of short-term monocular deprivation for modulating ocular dominance and advancing the understanding of binocular vision deficits in amblyopia.

0268.name: Ian Pennock
0268.bio: Dr. Zhan investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying visual cognition, with a specific focus on face processing and social perception. Their research addresses how the brain dynamically represents facial expressions, constructs reduced representations for perceptual decisions, and how cultural factors modulate face perception and beauty judgments. Dr. Zhan's contributions include demonstrating the dynamic construction of reduced representations, developing task-generalizable computational models of face memory, uncovering cross-cultural differences in beauty perception, and identifying a novel neural pathway for dynamic facial movement processing.

0269.name: Ignacio Serrano-Pedraza
0269.department: Department of Experimental Psychology. Faculty of Psychology
0269.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying the interplay of visual perception, working memory, social cognition, and causal reasoning. Their work addresses how humans process social information in working memory, understand intention attribution, and integrate visual attention with higher-level cognitive processes. Key contributions include demonstrating the automatic processing of social information in visual working memory and elucidating cognitive mechanisms of intention attribution beyond desire.

0270.name: Ikuya Murakami
0270.institution: Department of Psychology, The University of Tokyo
0270.department: Center for Brain Science, RIKEN; Japan Society for the Promotion of Science
0270.bio: Dr. Ongchoco investigates the interplay between perception and higher-level cognitive functions, particularly how the segmentation of continuous sensory input into discrete objects and events shapes attention, memory, and decision-making. Their research addresses how perceptual features, such as event boundaries, influence cognitive processes like visual working memory and temporal experience. Ongchoco's work has demonstrated the existence of "scaffolded attention" in creating object representations and revealed the impact of visual event boundaries on memory, extending these principles to the temporal domain and auditory rhythms.

0271.name: Ilan Vol
0271.bio: This researcher investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying visuomotor control and perceptual decision-making in complex, dynamic environments. Their work addresses how humans integrate sensory information, particularly optic flow, to guide goal-directed actions and adapt to changing task demands. Key contributions include developing computational models of optic flow processing for interceptive actions, demonstrating the role of active sampling in visuomotor control, and elucidating sensorimotor adaptation mechanisms in virtual reality.

0272.name: Ilker Yildirim
0272.bio: Dr. Faubert's research investigates the lifespan trajectory of perceptual-cognitive abilities, with a particular emphasis on aging and neurological conditions. His work addresses how perceptual-cognitive training paradigms, often implemented within virtual reality environments, can enhance human performance across diverse populations, including athletes and older adults. Faubert's significant contributions include the development of the 3D-Multiple Object Tracking (3D-MOT) paradigm and demonstrating the efficacy of perceptual-cognitive training in improving performance, even to levels comparable to untrained younger individuals.

0273.name: Injae Hong
0273.bio: This researcher focuses on the computational principles underlying visual perception and cognitive development, particularly through the lens of neural plasticity and active efficient coding. Their work addresses how brains learn autonomously compared to current AI systems and seeks to elucidate the computational mechanisms driving cognitive development in early childhood. Key contributions include the development of computational models of human learning, pioneering "Developmental AI" approaches, and advancing active efficient coding models of sensory processing.

0274.name: Ione Fine
0274.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying perception-action interactions, particularly focusing on how visual information guides hand actions and tool use. Their research addresses questions regarding the neural differences in processing real objects versus 2D images and the distinct neural processes involved in planning versus executing actions. Their contributions include pioneering the use of fMRI to study real object interactions, characterizing the dorsal visual stream's role in action, and demonstrating neural differences in processing 3D objects versus 2D images.

0275.name: Ipek Oruc
0275.bio: Dr. Wagemans investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual perception, focusing on perceptual organization, figure-ground segregation, and the relationship between these processes and aesthetic experience. Their research addresses how the visual system constructs coherent percepts from complex stimuli, particularly exploring the principles of Gestalt psychology and their neural underpinnings. Wagemans' contributions include revitalizing Gestalt principles within contemporary cognitive neuroscience, developing quantitative models of perceptual organization, and elucidating atypical visual processing in autism spectrum disorders.

0276.name: Irene Sperandio
0276.bio: Dr. Burge investigates the computational mechanisms underlying depth and motion perception by the human visual system. Their research focuses on determining optimal computational strategies for processing natural visual stimuli and understanding how task-specific natural scene statistics influence visual processing and performance. A significant contribution is the development of image-computable ideal observers for visual tasks with natural stimuli, demonstrating near-optimal human performance in tasks such as speed estimation and establishing links between normative models and neural responses.

0277.name: Isabel Gauthier
0277.bio: Dr. Greenwood investigates the perceptual and neural mechanisms underlying visual processing in peripheral vision, with a particular focus on visual crowding. Her research addresses how crowding impacts object recognition and face perception, and how visual disorders like amblyopia disrupt these processes. Her work has significantly advanced the understanding of visual crowding by demonstrating its role in altering appearance through positional averaging and identifying shared topological constraints with other visual functions, while also pioneering binocular therapies for amblyopia.

0278.name: Isabella M Durda
0278.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying rapid visual scene processing and social cognition using electroencephalography (EEG). Their work focuses on elucidating the neural correlates of saliency and semantic extraction from visual scenes, as well as anticipatory attentional processing during social exclusion. A significant contribution lies in developing and evaluating methods for artifact correction and rejection in event-related potential (ERP) research, enhancing the rigor and reliability of EEG studies in cognitive and social neuroscience.

0279.name: Isabelle Boutet
0279.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying working memory and executive functions, with a particular focus on attention allocation and cognitive control. Their work addresses questions regarding the neural substrates of working memory, the trainability of executive functions, and the cognitive processes involved in distraction and interference resolution. Their contributions include pioneering neuroimaging studies of working memory, developing influential models of executive control, and elucidating the role of the prefrontal cortex in working memory processes.

0280.name: J. Anthony Movshon
0280.bio: Dr. Tsotsos investigates the computational mechanisms underlying visual attention and active vision, bridging biological and artificial systems. His research addresses the fundamental problems of how computational models can accurately represent human visual attention, the computational complexity of visual information processing, and how active vision principles can improve robotic perception. He is renowned for developing the Selective Tuning model of visual attention, establishing the NP-completeness of unbounded visual search, and pioneering active vision strategies for object recognition in robotics.

0281.name: Jack Gallant
0281.bio: Dr. Serences investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual attention, working memory, and perceptual decision-making. His research aims to elucidate how behavioral goals and attentional factors modulate sensory processing, information storage, and stimulus representation within the visual system. Serences' contributions include demonstrating feature-based attentional modulation across the visual field, elucidating the neural mechanisms of visual short-term memory, and developing advanced fMRI analysis techniques to reconstruct stimulus features from brain activity patterns.

0282.name: Jacob Feldman
0282.bio: Dr. Tsotsos investigates computational mechanisms underlying visual attention in both biological and artificial systems. His research addresses the computational complexity limitations of visual processing and explores how attentional mechanisms, particularly active vision strategies, overcome these constraints for object recognition and visual search. He is renowned for the Selective Tuning model of visual attention and for establishing theoretical proofs demonstrating the computational necessity of attentional mechanisms in vision.

0283.name: James Bisley
0283.department: Department of Neurobiology; Department of Psychology
0283.bio: The Cant Lab investigates the neural mechanisms underlying human visual perception and cognition, with a focus on object, scene, and material property processing. Their research addresses questions regarding the functional organization of the ventral stream, the neural substrates of visual attributes, and the interactions between different stimulus categories in high-level visual cortex. Dr. Cant's work has demonstrated functional independence between brain regions processing shape and surface properties, and has proposed a model of high-level visual cortex organization based on both stimulus category and attribute processing.

0284.name: James Gordon
0284.institution: Hunter College, City University of New York
0284.bio: Dr. Flombaum investigates the cognitive and computational mechanisms underlying visual perception, attention, and working memory. Their research addresses how the brain represents and tracks multiple objects, categorizes colors, segments events, and the limitations of visual working memory. Dr. Flombaum's contributions include demonstrating the attentional demands of object tracking through occlusion, revealing biases in color memory, and developing models of multiple object tracking that account for individual differences.

0285.name: James Herman
0285.bio: Dr. Cant investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception and cognition, specifically focusing on object and scene processing within the ventral visual stream. Their research addresses how the brain integrates shape, surface properties, and material information for object recognition and scene understanding, and how these processes interact. A significant contribution includes demonstrating distinct cortical pathways for processing object shape versus surface properties and proposing a model of high-level visual cortex organization based on both stimulus categories and their supporting attributes.

0286.name: James Hillis
0286.bio: This researcher focuses on decoding neural activity to understand cognitive processes and enhance human-autonomy teaming, particularly within military contexts. Their work addresses the problem of translating neuroscientific findings to ecologically valid settings and leveraging neurotechnology to improve situational awareness. Key contributions include the development of EEGNet, pioneering mega-analysis of EEG data, and advancements in decoding phasic and tonic neural responses related to visual target discrimination and internal latent states.

0287.name: James J. DiCarlo
0287.institution: McGovern Institute for Brain Research at MIT
0287.bio: This researcher investigates the neural coding and sensory processing mechanisms underlying higher brain function, particularly in vision and disorders of consciousness. Their work addresses how sensory information is represented in neural circuits and the design principles governing sensory processing from the periphery to the brain. Key contributions include the development of spike train metrics, characterization of visual sensitivity to image features and correlations, and advances in EEG analysis for disorders of consciousness.

0288.name: James W. Tanaka
0288.bio: Dr. Winawer investigates the neural computations and circuits underlying visual perception in the human brain, with a focus on understanding both typical function and the neural basis of visual disorders. Her research addresses how visual representations are transformed across cortical areas and aims to develop computational models that predict cortical responses to diverse visual stimuli. Her significant contributions include advancing the understanding of visual field map organization, developing computational models of cortical responses, and integrating multi-modal neuroimaging data to elucidate cortical visual information encoding.

0289.name: Jan R. Wiersema
0289.bio: Dr. Song investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visuomotor integration, particularly focusing on the interplay between visual attention, motor control, and decision-making during goal-directed actions. Their research addresses how attentional states modulate motor learning and memory formation, and how higher-order cognitive processes are integrated with visually-guided actions to enable seamless interaction with complex environments. Dr. Song's contributions include demonstrating the influence of focal attention on visually-guided reaching, revealing paradoxical benefits of dual-task contexts for visuomotor memory, and identifying neural correlates of reach target selection in the dorsal premotor cortex.

0290.name: Jan Theeuwes
0290.department: Institute for Brain and Behaviour; William James Center for Research
0290.bio: Dr. Morales investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying subjective experience, focusing on the interplay between visual perception, consciousness, and metacognition. Her research addresses fundamental questions regarding how the mind constructs visual reality, the nature of introspection, and the neural correlates of subjective confidence. Dr. Morales's contributions include developing theoretical frameworks for the scientific study of subjectivity and advancing our understanding of the relationship between consciousness and metacognitive processes, particularly in the context of mental imagery and aphantasia.

0291.name: Jared Medina
0291.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying oculomotor control and visual stability, focusing on the interplay between eye movements and visual perception. Their work addresses how the brain controls eye movements to achieve stable vision and how eye movement analysis can be used for clinical diagnosis. They have significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating the role of microsaccades in restoring foveal and peripheral vision and establishing a continuum between exploratory gaze shifts and fixational eye movements.

0292.name: Jarrod Lewis-Peacock
0292.bio: Dr. Geng investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying attentional control and its influence on visual perception. Her research addresses how goal-directed and sensory-driven information are integrated to guide attention, and how attentional control mechanisms balance the selection of relevant information with the suppression of distractors. Geng's work has significantly advanced the understanding of distractor suppression, the role of template-to-distractor distinctiveness in visual search, and the function of the temporoparietal junction in contextual updating within attentional networks.

0293.name: Jason B. Mattingley
0293.bio: The researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying attentional control and perceptual decision-making in visual search. Their work addresses how goal-directed and sensory-driven information are integrated to regulate the selection of task-relevant information while suppressing distractors. Key contributions include demonstrating the differential roles of template information in attentional guidance versus match decisions, elucidating mechanisms of distractor suppression, and identifying the role of the temporoparietal junction in contextual cuing.

0294.name: Jay Pratt
0294.bio: Dr. Tam investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual attention, working memory, and face perception. Her research addresses how attention interacts with working memory, the processes involved in incidental encoding, and the influence of task-irrelevant features on visual processing. Dr. Tam's work has demonstrated limitations in privileged location processing, failures in remembering attended faces, the role of proactive suppression in attention, incidental conceptual storage, and memorability effects in visual working memory.

0296.name: Jeffrey D. Wammes
0296.department: Department of Psychology; Centre for Neuroscience Studies
0296.bio: Dr. Mitchell investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual attention, active vision, and oculomotor control within cortical circuits. His research addresses how internal brain states, such as selective attention, modulate sensory processing and how different neuronal classes contribute to attentional modulation of neuronal spiking statistics. His significant contributions include pioneering the use of marmosets as a model for visual neuroscience, demonstrating that attention reduces correlated variability in visual cortex, and developing methods for extracellular classification of neuronal types.

0297.name: Jelena Ristic
0297.bio: Dr. Golomb investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying visual attention, perception, and spatial processing, particularly how the brain maintains perceptual stability despite constant retinal changes. Her research addresses how spatial information is encoded and updated across saccades, and how attention and working memory interact with object recognition. Her contributions include demonstrating the retinotopic encoding of location information, identifying the spatial congruency bias in object-location binding, elucidating spatial updating mechanisms, and developing advanced fMRI analysis techniques such as improved inverted encoding models.

0298.name: Jennifer K. E. Steeves
0298.institution: Centre for Vision Research; York University
0298.department: Centre for Integrative and Applied Neuroscience; Department of Psychology
0298.bio: Julie Harris investigates the perceptual mechanisms underlying 3D vision, with a focus on depth and motion perception in both static and dynamic scenes. Her research addresses how the visual system integrates binocular disparity, motion cues, and shading information to construct a coherent representation of the 3D world. Her work has demonstrated the importance of perceived target location in guiding locomotion, elucidated independent mechanisms for motion-in-depth perception, and advanced the development of effective camouflage strategies based on principles of visual perception.

0299.name: Jenny Bosten
0299.bio: Dr. Zhang investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying visual perceptual learning and plasticity. Their research focuses on elucidating how perceptual learning transfers across different stimuli, tasks, and retinal locations, and how top-down cognitive processes influence this transfer. Dr. Zhang's work has demonstrated that perceptual learning can occur at high cognitive levels, generalize between distinct stimuli, and transfer completely across retinal locations and orientations through specialized training paradigms.

0300.name: Jenny W. S. Chiu
0300.bio: Dr. Halberda's research investigates the cognitive and developmental underpinnings of numerical cognition, language acquisition, and object representation. His work addresses how humans represent approximate quantities via the Approximate Number System, how logical inference contributes to word learning, and how discrete object representations are constructed from continuous sensory input. He has demonstrated a correlation between non-verbal number acuity and math achievement, revealed parallel enumeration of spatially overlapping sets, and proposed a disjunctive syllogism account of word learning.

0301.name: Jeremy B Wilmer
0301.bio: Dr. Billino investigates the lifespan dynamics of visual perception and sensorimotor control, with a focus on cognitive aging. Her research addresses how sensory, motor, and cognitive mechanisms interact across the lifespan to influence individual differences in visual perception and how aging affects motion perception. Her work has demonstrated age-related changes in motion perception, linked these changes to cognitive control, and developed integrative models of perceptual aging that consider both decline and stability.

0302.name: Jeremy M. Wolfe
0302.institution: Brigham and Women’s Hospital; Harvard Medical School
0302.bio: This researcher investigates the developmental cognitive neuroscience of face processing and social cognition, particularly during childhood and adolescence. Their work addresses how the brain develops the capacity to perceive and interpret social signals, with a focus on the neural mechanisms underlying face recognition abilities in both typical and autistic populations. This research has elucidated the developmental trajectory of face processing and demonstrated the influence of pubertal development on social cognitive abilities, leading to the development of targeted interventions for improving social skills in autistic adolescents.

0304.name: Jessica K. Witt
0304.bio: Dr. Schloss investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual communication, focusing on how individuals derive meaning from visual features, particularly color, in information visualizations and other contexts. Her research addresses how observers' expectations and semantic associations impact the interpretation of visual information and how to optimize visual communication for effectiveness and efficiency. Schloss's significant contributions include the Ecological Valence Theory of color preference, the color inference framework for understanding color semantics, and advancements in semantic discriminability theory for effective visual communication.

0305.name: Jessica Montag
0305.bio: Dr. James investigates the influence of sensorimotor experience on cognitive development, particularly focusing on the role of action in shaping visual processing and learning. Her research addresses how handwriting experience impacts letter recognition and reading acquisition, and elucidates the neural mechanisms underlying learning through self-generated action versus passive observation. Dr. James's work has demonstrated that handwriting practice elicits greater fusiform gyrus activation during letter recognition compared to other methods in pre-literate children, highlighting the importance of sensorimotor integration in literacy development.

0306.name: Jeunghwan Choi
0306.bio: This researcher investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying visual perception, with a particular emphasis on color vision, eye movements, and sensorimotor integration in natural environments. Their work addresses fundamental questions regarding how the brain represents visual information, how this information drives motor behavior, and the relationship between low-level sensory processes and higher-level cognition. Key contributions include elucidating cortical mechanisms of color vision in area V3, demonstrating the role of color in object recognition, characterizing eye movement variability in dynamic scenes, and developing computational models of color discrimination and adaptation.

0307.name: Jiahui Guo
0307.institution: The University of Texas at Dallas
0307.bio: Dr. Bonnen investigates the neural computations underlying visual and sensorimotor processing, particularly in the context of 3D vision, eye movements, and walking behavior. Her research addresses how the brain processes visual information to guide complex behaviors like walking, and how sensory and motor systems interact during natural behaviors. Dr. Bonnen's contributions include developing novel paradigms for studying 3D motion perception and tracking, advancing understanding of visually-guided locomotion, and integrating behavioral measurements, computational modeling, and natural scene statistics.

0308.name: Jiali Song
0308.bio: Dr. Fiehler investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying perception-action coupling, spatial cognition, and eye-hand coordination. Her research addresses how sensory information is integrated for spatial perception and goal-directed actions, how the brain plans and controls natural actions, and how humans predict sensory outcomes of their actions. Dr. Fiehler's work has significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating the development of the dorsal action control system in the absence of vision, elucidating the neural correlates of error processing in action control, and revealing the role of kinesthetic working memory within the dorsal stream.

0309.name: Jian Chen
0309.bio: Dr. Watanabe investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying perception, decision-making, and social cognition, with a particular focus on implicit processes and individual differences. Their research addresses questions regarding cross-modal interactions, the neural substrates of reward-based decision-making, and the influence of social and cultural factors on perception and behavior. Dr. Watanabe's contributions include elucidating neural correlates of decision-making in the caudate nucleus, demonstrating the impact of auditory grouping on visual motion perception, and developing methods for studying implicit behavioral and emotional contagion.

0310.name: Jiawei Zhou
0310.department: School of Ophthalmology and Optometry and Eye Hospital; State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Vision Science
0310.bio: Keiko Tsuchiya investigates multimodal communication strategies within healthcare settings, focusing on discourse analysis, pragmatics, and sociolinguistics. Her research examines how linguistic and non-verbal features, including gaze behavior captured via eye-tracking, contribute to effective information sharing and decision-making in high-stakes medical environments, particularly comparing practices between the UK and Japan. Tsuchiya's significant contributions include pioneering the use of eye-tracking to analyze gaze behavior in emergency care, developing innovative multimodal approaches to healthcare communication research, and informing the development of more effective training protocols for healthcare professionals.

0311.name: jiayu Zhan
0311.bio: Dr. Schneider investigates the neural substrates of visual perception and attention, with a particular focus on subcortical structures. His research addresses how attention modulates visual processing and decision-making, and how specialized neural channels encode sensory information. His contributions include demonstrating attentional effects on decision criteria, identifying specialized channels in the MGN, revealing LGN asymmetries in dyslexia, and developing advanced fMRI methods for subcortical neuroimaging.

0312.name: Jifan Zhou
0312.bio: Dr. Bijanki investigates the intracranial neural substrates of affective processing and mood regulation in humans. Her research aims to elucidate the functional roles of specific brain circuits in emotional states and to develop targeted neuromodulation strategies for treatment-resistant psychiatric disorders. Her significant contributions include demonstrating the therapeutic potential of cingulum bundle stimulation for anxiety and mood enhancement, and characterizing electrophysiological correlates of affective states using human intracranial recordings.

0313.name: Joan Danielle K. Ongchoco
0313.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, particularly focusing on face recognition and visual attention. Their work addresses questions regarding the neural substrates of face processing, the nature of individual differences in visual abilities, and the interaction between visual perception and motor control. Their contributions include pioneering studies of binocular rivalry, priming of pop-out, and the development of the Cambridge Face Memory Test, significantly advancing our understanding of visual awareness, attention, and prosopagnosia.

0314.name: Joan López-Moliner
0314.department: Vision and Control of Action Group, Institute of Neurosciences
0314.bio: Dr. Miller investigates the computational functions and developmental mechanisms of cerebral cortical circuitry, particularly within the visual cortex. His research addresses how cortical circuits develop, self-organize, and perform fundamental computations in response to varying input signals. Miller's significant contributions include influential models of visual cortex development, the concept of balanced amplification in neural circuits, and advancements in understanding inhibitory circuit dynamics in cortical function.

0315.name: Joana Seabra
0315.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visuospatial attention and visual working memory, particularly focusing on attentional lapses and the selection of relevant information. Their work addresses how individuals filter distractions, detect attentional failures leading to memory errors, and optimize attentional resource allocation in complex visual scenes. A significant contribution lies in elucidating the relationship between attention and visual working memory, identifying neural mechanisms of attentional control, and demonstrating the impact of practice and feedback on visual working memory performance.

0316.name: Jocelyn Faubert
0316.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying primate visual object recognition, focusing on the interplay between the ventral visual stream and prefrontal cortex. Their work addresses how the brain achieves robust object recognition despite variations in viewpoint and context, and how this process is disrupted in atypical conditions. Their significant contributions include demonstrating the critical role of recurrent processing and ventrolateral prefrontal cortex feedback in visual object recognition, and developing brain-mapped artificial neural network models that closely mimic primate visual processing.

0317.name: Jochen Triesch
0317.bio: Dr. Derpanis's research focuses on developing computational models for motion analysis, 3D vision, and generative modeling within the field of computer vision. His work addresses fundamental challenges in understanding complex motion and human actions in video, improving 3D scene understanding from 2D images, and advancing representation learning for visual data. A significant contribution of his research is the development of spatiotemporal orientation analysis techniques for action recognition, 3D human pose estimation from monocular video, and innovations in generative modeling for image-to-video synthesis.

0318.name: Jody C. Culham
0318.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying perceptual decision-making, focusing on the interplay between prefrontal cortex and decision strategies. Their work addresses how prefrontal and superior medial frontal cortices causally influence decision-making strategies and the neural basis of the speed-accuracy tradeoff, while also considering the role of individual differences. They have demonstrated the causal involvement of specific cortical regions in adapting decision strategies and established links between individual differences in decision-making and underlying neurobiological factors, furthering understanding of the neural substrates of cognitive control.

0319.name: Johan Wagemans
0319.bio: Dr. Koida investigates the neural mechanisms underlying color perception and three-dimensional shape processing, focusing on the interplay between neural activity and perceptual experience. Their research addresses how the brain represents color information, how task demands modulate neural responses in visual cortical areas, and how material properties influence shape perception. Dr. Koida's contributions include demonstrating task-dependent modulation of color-selective neurons in the inferior temporal cortex and revealing that transparent objects are perceived as flatter than opaque objects, challenging existing models of visual processing.

0320.name: Johannes Burge
0320.department: Neuroscience Graduate Group; Bioengineering Graduate Group
0320.bio: Dr. Kelly investigates the neural and behavioral consequences of atypical visual experience in pediatric vision disorders. Her research addresses the etiology of reading and motor impairments associated with conditions such as amblyopia and strabismus, seeking to elucidate the underlying mechanisms. Dr. Kelly's contributions include demonstrating ocular motor deficits and identifying reading and fine motor skill impairments in children with amblyopia, alongside developing novel assessment methods for binocular vision and eye-hand coordination.

0321.name: John A. Greenwood
0321.bio: Based solely on the provided information, a technical summary cannot be generated. The information only indicates the individual was named to the President's List, which does not provide any details about their research focus, questions, or contributions to vision science.

0322.name: John E. Kiat
0322.bio: Dr. Nielsen investigates the function and development of neural circuits within higher-level visual cortex. Her research addresses how visual information is transformed across processing stages and how abstract visual representations are implemented in higher-order areas, with a focus on mechanisms of cortical plasticity. Dr. Nielsen has significantly contributed to the understanding of object encoding in inferotemporal cortex, the development of *in vivo* circuit analysis techniques using viral vectors and two-photon microscopy, and the micro-organization of orientation and spatial frequency in primate V1.

0323.name: John Jonides
0323.bio: Dr. Visscher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying flexible visual processing and plasticity in the context of aging and central vision loss. Her research explores how the brain adapts to visual system challenges, focusing on the influence of pre-stimulus brain states, task demands, and age-related changes on visual cortex function. Her work has significantly contributed to understanding functional connectivity changes following macular degeneration, the role of fronto-parietal networks in attentional modulation of visual cortex, and the impact of pre-stimulus brain states on visual processing.

0324.name: John K. Tsotsos
0324.bio: Due to the lack of available information regarding this vision scientist's specific research, core questions, and major contributions, a technical summary cannot be accurately generated. Insufficient data prevents the articulation of their research focus, the description of their research problems, or the highlighting of their significant findings. Further information is required to provide a meaningful and accurate three-sentence summary.

0325.name: John Maule
0325.institution: Statistical Perception Lab; University of Sussex
0325.department: The Sussex Colour Group & Baby Lab; School of Psychology
0325.bio: Dr. Huxlin investigates the capacity for neuroplasticity and functional reorganization within the adult human visual system following cortical damage, particularly in the context of cortically-induced blindness and occipital stroke. Her research addresses fundamental questions regarding the mechanisms underlying visual system plasticity, the extent to which damaged visual pathways can be rehabilitated, and the impact of cortical blindness on visually-guided behavior. Her work has demonstrated the efficacy of perceptual training paradigms in restoring visual function in cortically blind fields, revealed differences in plasticity between subacute and chronic stroke, and advanced understanding of retrograde degeneration in early visual pathways after V1 damage.

0326.name: John Serences
0326.bio: Dr. Trick investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying attention and working memory across the lifespan, with a particular focus on their impact on driving behavior. Her research seeks to elucidate the cognitive processes involved in multiple-object tracking, visual enumeration, and dual-task performance, especially as they relate to collision risk and road safety. Dr. Trick's contributions include pioneering work on age-related changes in multiple-object tracking capacity, developing models of visual enumeration processes, and applying cognitive principles to understand and mitigate attentional deficits in novice and older drivers through driving simulation.

0327.name: Jonathan Cant
0327.bio: This researcher investigates the interplay between attention, working memory, and cognitive processes in the context of driving behavior across the lifespan. Their work addresses questions regarding the cognitive mechanisms underlying attentional processes, the factors contributing to collision risk, and the impact of individual differences on driving performance. A significant contribution includes establishing the relevance of multiple-object tracking to real-world tasks and elucidating the effects of distraction and cognitive load on driving safety, often leveraging driving simulators and cognitive psychology paradigms.

0328.name: Jonathan Flombaum
0328.bio: Dr. Muckli investigates the influence of cortical feedback on sensory processing within early visual areas, particularly focusing on the information conveyed to different cortical layers. Her research addresses how internal models and predictions shape visual perception, utilizing high-resolution fMRI to reveal sub-millimeter functional organization. She has demonstrated contextual feedback to superficial layers of V1, provided evidence for predictive coding theories, and advanced methods for layer-specific fMRI analysis in humans.

0329.name: Jonathan Touryan
0329.institution: U.S. Army DEBCOM Army Research Laboratory
0329.bio: Dr. Klatt investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying selective attention and working memory, particularly within multisensory contexts. Her research addresses how attentional orienting shapes perception and facilitates the encoding and maintenance of multisensory information in working memory. Dr. Klatt's contributions include elucidating the neurophysiological correlates of attentional orienting in both perceptual and mnemonic space, and demonstrating cross-modal interactions between chemosensory and auditory processing using electrophysiological and behavioral measures.

0330.name: Jonathan Victor
0330.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visuospatial processing and action control, with a specific focus on the role of the parietal cortex. Their research addresses how lesions in the parietal cortex disrupt spatial cognition and visually-guided actions, particularly in conditions like optic ataxia and Bálint's syndrome. They have significantly contributed to the understanding of optic ataxia's impact on online movement control, elucidated the role of the posterior parietal cortex in spatial cognition, and advanced the characterization of Bálint's syndrome.

0331.name: Jonathan Winawer
0331.bio: Dr. Harris investigates the integration of visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive information in the perception of self-motion and spatial orientation. His research addresses how these sensory modalities combine to create a unified sense of body position and movement, particularly in altered environments like microgravity. He has significantly advanced the field through pioneering work on peripheral vision's role in balance, elucidating vestibular-visual interactions, and developing virtual reality paradigms for studying multisensory integration.

0332.name: Joo-Hyun Song
0332.bio: Dr. Wilcox investigates the psychophysical and neural mechanisms underlying stereoscopic depth perception, particularly the processing of binocular disparities. Her research addresses how the visual system extracts depth information from fine and coarse disparities, and how factors such as eye movements and amblyopia impact stereopsis. Her significant contributions include demonstrating distinct cortical pathways for processing fine and coarse disparities, revealing the functionality of coarse stereopsis without fusional eye movements, and elucidating the role of coarse stereopsis in depth perception for individuals with amblyopia.

0333.name: Jordan Deakin
0333.bio: Dr. Carver's research focuses on the developmental cognitive neuroscience of social cognition and memory during infancy and early childhood. Her work investigates the neural mechanisms underlying reward processing, social motivation, and explicit memory formation, particularly in relation to the etiology of autism spectrum disorders. Her significant contributions include demonstrating atypical neural responses to emotional stimuli and differences in reward anticipation in children with ASD, as well as linking early brain activity to behavioral outcomes in infancy.

0334.name: Jorge Morales
0334.bio: This researcher investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying human social vision, specifically how the brain extracts complex social information from visual input. Their work aims to elucidate the neural computations involved in recognizing human actions and social interactions, and to bridge the gap between human and artificial intelligence in processing social visual information. A key contribution is demonstrating the hierarchical processing of social interaction information along the lateral visual pathway and developing the SocialGNN model, which replicates human judgments of social interactions.

0335.name: Jorge Otero-Millan
0335.bio: Dr. Mudrik investigates the neural substrates and functional roles of consciousness, with a particular emphasis on volitional action and the content of conscious experience. Her research addresses fundamental questions regarding the neural correlates of consciousness, the distinction between conscious and unconscious processing, and the influence of cognitive states on subjective experience. Dr. Mudrik's contributions include developing ecologically valid methods for studying consciousness, contributing to large-scale theory arbitration efforts, and advancing understanding of the relationship between consciousness and decision-making.

0336.name: Joseph DeGutis
0336.institution: Boston Attention Learning Lab, VA Boston Healthcare System; Department of Psychiatry, Boston University Chobanian and Avedisian School of Medicine
0336.department: Department of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School
0336.bio: Dr. Wang investigates the neural mechanisms underlying the integration of perceptual and motor systems, with a focus on the modulatory roles of reward and attention. Her research aims to elucidate how disruptions in perceptual-motor integration contribute to cognitive deficits observed in mental disorders such as addiction and depression. Through neuroimaging and computational modeling, she has demonstrated the influence of reward on visual perception and cross-modal conflict resolution, and has developed innovative paradigms for studying spatial working memory.

0337.name: Joseph Pare
0337.bio: Dr. Sahakyan investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying human long-term memory, with a specific focus on intentional forgetting and memory control. Her research addresses how mental context shapes episodic memories, influences memory retrieval, and mediates intentional forgetting, particularly exploring the neural correlates of these processes. Dr. Sahakyan's major contributions include the development of the context-change account of directed forgetting, demonstrating the importance of mental context in memory, and establishing links between intentional forgetting abilities and vulnerability to psychopathology.

0338.name: Joy Geng
0338.bio: Dr. Smith investigates the dynamic interplay between perceptual, motor, and cognitive systems in early childhood development. Her research addresses how infants learn object names and categories, and how their motor abilities and embodied experiences shape learning through statistical regularities in natural environments. Smith's contributions include co-developing dynamic systems theory, discovering the shape bias in word learning, pioneering head-mounted camera studies of infant vision, and elucidating embodied and statistical learning mechanisms in cognitive development.

0339.name: Joyce Tam
0339.bio: Dr. Gan's research investigates the mechanisms underlying visual perception, specifically focusing on salience mapping and centroid judgments in feature integration. Their work addresses how the visual system prioritizes and processes multiple visual features simultaneously, exploring the relationship between salience map formation and concurrent centroid judgment performance. A significant contribution is the development of novel methodologies to quantitatively derive the number of salience maps an observer possesses based on their performance in concurrent centroid judgment tasks.

0340.name: Jr. Rhea T. Eskew
0340.bio: Dr. Swanson investigates the effects of psychedelic drugs on perception and cognition, with a particular emphasis on hallucination and altered states of consciousness. His research aims to elucidate the mechanisms underlying psychedelic-induced perceptual changes and integrate these findings with philosophical frameworks of mind and consciousness. Dr. Swanson's significant contribution lies in proposing a unifying theoretical framework for understanding the diverse neurobiological and cognitive effects of psychedelics, as articulated in his 2018 *Frontiers in Pharmacology* publication.

0341.name: Juan Chen
0341.department: Key Laboratory of Brain, Cognition and Education Sciences
0341.bio: Dr. Maman investigates the application of augmented reality (AR) to enhance visual perception and mobility for individuals with low vision. His research addresses the challenge of optimizing AR interfaces to provide effective visual assistance without overwhelming users, specifically focusing on methods for highlighting environmental features to improve navigation. Dr. Maman's work has demonstrated that AR-based enhancement of obstacle outlines improves mobility and navigation speed for individuals with low vision, contributing to a deeper understanding of how AR can augment residual vision.

0342.name: Jude Mitchell
0342.bio: Dr. Oakes investigates the development of visual cognitive abilities in infancy, focusing on the interplay between attention, memory, categorization, and perceptual-motor systems. Her research addresses how experience shapes infants' visual attention and learning, particularly within complex, naturalistic environments. Her work has demonstrated the interaction between attention and visual short-term memory, revealed the influence of daily experiences on infant learning, and shown that infants' fixations on natural scenes are influenced by local meaning.

0343.name: Julia-Katharina Pfarr
0343.institution: Neuro Data Science ORIGAMI Laboratory, McConnell Brain Imaging Centre, Faculty of Medicine, McGill University, Montréal, Canada; Philipps-University Marburg, Department of Psychology, General and Biological Psychology, Marburg, Germany
0343.bio: Dr. Renzi-Hammond investigates lifespan sensory and cognitive development, with a focus on neurological underpinnings. Her research addresses how brain structure, sensory integrity, and plasticity relate to disease risk and cognitive vitality across the lifespan, while also developing novel methodologies for assessing cognitive and sensory function. Her contributions include the development of innovative technologies for measuring visual, cognitive, and neurological function, as well as investigating the role of health literacy and patient autonomy in healthcare settings.

0344.name: Julie D. Golomb
0344.bio: This researcher investigates the developmental trajectory of perceptual and cognitive processes, particularly face and object processing, from infancy through childhood. Their work addresses questions regarding the influence of early experience on shaping perceptual abilities and the underlying neural mechanisms of perceptual narrowing. Notably, they have demonstrated the critical role of early experience in shaping face processing, elucidated neural correlates of perceptual narrowing, and identified mechanisms of perceptual expertise development across the lifespan.

0345.name: Julie M. Harris
0345.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms of visual neuroplasticity in cerebral/cortical visual impairment (CVI) and ocular blindness. Their work aims to elucidate the neurophysiological underpinnings of CVI, differentiate them from those of ocular blindness, and develop advanced neuroimaging and virtual reality-based methods for assessment. Their contributions include pioneering research on neuroplasticity in CVI, developing novel assessment tools, establishing a conceptual framework relating sensory function with brain reorganization, and contributing to the official definition of CVI.

0346.name: Jun-Yun Zhang
0346.bio: Dr. Merabet's research focuses on the neural mechanisms underlying visual neuroplasticity, particularly in the context of cerebral/cortical visual impairment (CVI). His work investigates how the brain reorganizes in response to visual impairment, with specific emphasis on the neurophysiological correlates of visual motion processing and the differential impact of ocular versus cerebral etiologies. Through the application of fMRI and virtual reality-based assessments, he has demonstrated differential recruitment of dorsal and ventral visual streams in CVI and contributed to establishing diagnostic criteria for this condition.

0347.name: Junxian Rao
0347.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, attention, and memory. Their work addresses questions regarding the influence of similarity on visual search, the neural basis of size constancy, and category-based attention in memory. Key contributions include demonstrating the impact of categorical and perceptual similarity on visual search efficiency, elucidating the spatiotemporal dynamics of neural representations during visual tasks, and providing evidence for category-level attentional selection in memory search.

0348.name: Justin Halberda
0348.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and developmental neuroscience of face processing, specifically focusing on the other-race effect (ORE). They examine the neural mechanisms and developmental trajectories underlying differential processing of own- and other-race faces, addressing questions about the influence of face race on neural processing across the lifespan. Their work has demonstrated early emergence of race-related processing differences in infants and identified multiple perceptual stages where processing difficulties for other-race faces occur in adults, contributing to a comprehensive understanding of the ORE.

0349.name: Jutta Billino
0349.bio: Dr. Melloni investigates the neural mechanisms underlying conscious perception, learning, and memory, with a particular focus on how cognitive processes and predictive coding influence subjective experience. Their research addresses fundamental questions regarding the neural correlates of consciousness and the development of rigorous methodologies for testing competing theories.
