Melloni's significant contributions include pioneering work on the neural correlates of consciousness using electrophysiology, advancing the understanding of predictive processing in perception, and championing open science practices within the field.

0351.name: Kacie Lee
0351.bio: Dr. Rutherford's research investigates the developmental trajectory of social cognitive abilities, with a particular focus on face perception, emotion recognition, and social attention. Her work addresses fundamental questions regarding the mechanisms underlying typical and atypical social cognitive development, including the influence of evolutionary pressures and the neural bases of social perception. Her significant contributions include identifying atypical social attention patterns in infants at high risk for autism, elucidating distinct biological motion perception mechanisms in autism, and advancing understanding of face and emotion recognition development across the lifespan.

0353.name: Karin James
0353.bio: Dr. Linka investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying individual differences in visual attention and gaze behavior. Her research addresses the consistency of individual eye movement patterns and their potential as diagnostic markers for neurodevelopmental disorders. She has demonstrated the reliability of brief eye-tracking tests for estimating individual differences in semantic salience and developed the OSIEshort stimulus set for efficiently capturing individual gaze biases, including those observed in super-recognizers.

0354.name: Karl R. Gegenfurtner
0354.bio: This researcher investigates the neural substrates of visual perception, particularly focusing on face recognition and the development of visual cortex. Their work aims to elucidate the neural mechanisms underlying the processing and encoding of visual information related to faces, objects, and scenes, as well as the impact of early visual experience on cortical organization. Their significant contributions include characterizing the parallel processing streams and specific connectivity between functionally distinct regions in the primate visual system, and demonstrating the experience-dependent development of face-selective regions.

0356.name: Katharina Dobs
0356.institution: Justus Liebig University in Giessen, Germany
0356.department: Center for Mind, Brain and Behavior, Universities of Marburg, Giessen and Darmstadt
0356.bio: A neurobiologist specializing in visual neuroscience, Dr. Livingstone investigates the neural mechanisms underlying object and face recognition, as well as the development of specialized visual processing regions in the primate brain. Her research addresses fundamental questions regarding how early visual experiences shape the functional organization of the visual cortex and the neural correlates of visual perception. Dr. Livingstone's seminal contributions include the discovery of parallel processing streams for color and form and the elucidation of experience-dependent development of face-selective domains within the primate visual cortex.

0357.name: Kathryn Bonnen
0357.bio: Dr. Robinson investigates the cognitive and computational mechanisms underlying visual cognition, with a particular focus on the interplay between attention, memory, and decision-making. Her research addresses fundamental questions regarding the formation of visual memory representations from sensory evidence and the development of rigorous, integrative theories of cognition. Dr. Robinson's contributions include developing quantitative models of ensemble perception, demonstrating the generalizability of local semantic network measures, and advancing methods for evaluating discrete-slot models of visual short-term memory.

0358.name: Katja Fiehler
0358.bio: Dr. Maertens investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying human visual perception, with a focus on lightness perception, edge detection, and spatial vision. Her research addresses how the visual system extracts perceptual categories from sensory input, elucidates the neural substrates of edge detection and lightness perception, and develops reliable psychophysical methods for measuring perceptual appearance.  Dr. Maertens has made significant contributions through the development and refinement of Maximum Likelihood Difference Scaling (MLDS) and Maximum Likelihood Conjoint Measurement (MLCM), the identification of the role of medium spatial frequencies in edge detection, and the exploration of the relationship between luminance increment thresholds and apparent lightness.

0359.name: Katsumi Watanabe
0359.bio: Dr. Rouault investigates the neurocognitive mechanisms underlying metacognition and confidence judgments in decision-making, particularly focusing on the interplay between local and global confidence estimates. Their research aims to understand how these metacognitive processes are related to psychiatric symptoms and how controllability influences information seeking under uncertainty. Rouault's work has demonstrated distinct neural substrates for global self-performance estimates, identified dissociable metacognitive shifts in psychiatric disorders, and developed computational models elucidating the construction and maintenance of confidence across decisions.

0360.name: Keiko Tsuchiya
0360.bio: The Carrasco Lab investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms by which voluntary and involuntary attention modulate visual perception. Their research addresses how attention influences fundamental visual attributes, such as contrast sensitivity and spatial resolution, and explores the temporal dynamics of attentional processing. Through psychophysical, neuroimaging, and computational modeling approaches, Dr. Carrasco has demonstrated that attention alters the perceived appearance of stimuli, elucidated differential effects of endogenous and exogenous attention, and established causal roles of specific cortical regions in attentional control.

0361.name: Keisuke Fukuda
0361.institution: University of Toronto Mississauga; University of Toronto
0361.bio: This researcher investigates the interplay of perceptual and cognitive processes in guiding human behavior, with a focus on visual attention, cognitive aging, and skill acquisition. Their work addresses how aging and distraction impact visual search, divided attention, and cybersecurity tasks like phishing detection, while also exploring methods to augment human performance through training and technology. Their contributions include demonstrating the role of depth information in visual working memory, elucidating cognitive mechanisms of distraction in driving, and developing interventions for improved phishing detection, alongside advancing understanding of age-related changes in visual cognition.

0362.name: Keith A. Schneider
0362.bio: Dr. Lescroart investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual processing, focusing on how the brain transforms retinal input into representations of object identity, shape, and spatial structure. Her research addresses how different brain areas encode information about objects, actions, and environments, and whether computational models can accurately predict neural responses to complex visual stimuli. She has significantly contributed to the field by developing computational models that predict fMRI responses in scene-selective areas, demonstrating the representation of 3D spatial structure in these areas, and mapping object contour representations in intermediate visual cortex.

0363.name: Kelly Bijanki
0363.bio: Dr. Greenlee investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception and multisensory integration, particularly focusing on motion processing. His research addresses how attention, adaptation, and vestibular input modulate visual processing, and how these processes are altered in visual disorders. He has significantly contributed to the field through pioneering work on contrast adaptation, elucidating neural correlates of visual motion processing using fMRI, and advancing understanding of vestibular-visual interactions.

0364.name: Ken Nakayama
0364.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying the interaction between attention, emotion, and motivation. Their work addresses how cognitive control processes, such as attention and inhibition, modulate affective evaluations and contribute to states like boredom. They have demonstrated that selective attention and cognitive inhibition can negatively impact affective responses to stimuli, and have explored the neural substrates of these interactions, contributing to models of boredom and translating neuroscientific findings to enhance performance.

0365.name: Kenneth D. Miller
0365.bio: Dr. Becker investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual attention and its interaction with emotion. Her research addresses how attentional resources are allocated in complex scenes, the influence of affective states on visual processing, and the factors modulating visual search efficiency. Becker's work has demonstrated the impact of media multitasking on mental health, revealed attentional biases toward mood-congruent stimuli, and identified limitations in visual short-term memory consolidation.

0366.name: Kevin Willeford
0366.institution: NOVA Southeastern University College of Optometry
0366.bio: Dr. Lappe investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception and its interaction with motor control, particularly in the domains of visual stability, biological motion, and spatial navigation. His research addresses how the brain maintains perceptual stability across saccades, processes optic flow for self-motion perception, and decodes biological motion cues. Lappe's significant contributions include demonstrating the role of post-saccadic references in pre-saccadic spatial compression, developing configural models of biological motion perception, and elucidating the neural processing of optic flow in the primate brain.

0367.name: Kia Nobre
0367.bio: Dr. Meister investigates the neural computations performed by retinal circuits and the superior colliculus to understand how sensory inputs are transformed into behavioral outputs. His research focuses on elucidating the principles governing the structure and function of these circuits using electrophysiological recordings, optogenetic manipulation, and computational modeling. His pioneering work with multielectrode arrays has revealed sophisticated retinal processing and led to a normative theory explaining the purpose of retinal information processing, significantly advancing the understanding of population coding in the mammalian superior colliculus.

0368.name: Kim Chai
0368.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural substrates of visual perception, focusing on object and face recognition. Their work addresses fundamental questions regarding the assembly of visual representations, the psychological and neural mechanisms underlying recognition processes, and the development of hemispheric specialization. Through neuropsychological, neuroimaging, and computational approaches, they have significantly advanced understanding of visual processing deficits in atypical populations and demonstrated neural plasticity in the visual cortex following brain damage.

0369.name: Kirsten Adam
0369.bio: Dr. Giese investigates the neural and computational substrates of action recognition and motor control, with a particular emphasis on biological motion perception. Their research addresses how the brain processes social signals conveyed through body movements and how computational models can elucidate motor learning and control. Dr. Giese's significant contributions include the development of influential neural network models of biological motion perception and the application of computational methods for analyzing movement patterns in clinical populations.

0370.name: Kohitij Kar
0370.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual attention, face perception, and the interaction between attention and working memory. Their work aims to delineate the neural substrates and temporal dynamics of attentional selection, target processing, and face recognition. A significant contribution is the pioneering use of the N2pc component as an electrophysiological marker of attentional selection, alongside advancements in understanding face processing and the development of the RSVP paradigm for studying attentional template activation.

0371.name: Konstantinos Derpanis
0371.department: Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science
0371.bio: Dr. Rolfs investigates the interplay between active vision, visual cognition, and eye movements, particularly focusing on oculomotor control and attention. His research addresses how eye movements influence visual perception, how attention is allocated across saccades, and how perceptual continuity is maintained despite constant eye movements. He is recognized for demonstrating predictive remapping of attention across saccades, elucidating the role of microsaccades in visual attention, and advancing understanding of visual stability across eye movements.

0372.name: Koralalage Don Raveen Amarasekera
0372.bio: This researcher investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying visual stability, spatial attention, and oculomotor control. Their work addresses how the brain remaps spatial information across saccades and maintains perceptual continuity despite eye movements. Key contributions include demonstrating the temporal dynamics of attentional remapping, providing evidence for spatiotopic apparent motion, and developing novel paradigms to study pre-saccadic attention shifts.

0373.name: Kowa Koida
0373.bio: Dr. Wiener investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying temporal and spatial cognition, with a particular focus on interval timing and magnitude processing. His research addresses how visual stimulus characteristics, movement, and memory interact to shape the perception and representation of time intervals. His work has demonstrated the influence of image memorability on perceived duration, the temporal sharpening effects of movement, and the role of time dilation in processing salient stimuli.

0374.name: Krista A. Ehinger
0374.bio: Dr. Poletti investigates the neural and oculomotor mechanisms underlying fine spatial vision, particularly within the foveola. Her research addresses how foveal processing, microsaccades, and attention interact to enable high-acuity vision and active visual exploration. Her work has demonstrated the functional role of microsaccades in optimizing foveal vision, revealed attentional selectivity within the foveola, and challenged the assumption of foveal uniformity by identifying asymmetries in its visual processing.

0375.name: Krista Kelly
0375.bio: Dr. Chun's research investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual attention, memory, perception, and decision-making using cognitive neuroscience approaches. He seeks to understand how brain imaging can decode cognitive states, predict behavior, and reveal neural correlates of individual differences in cognitive performance. His significant contributions include the development of the contextual cueing paradigm, pioneering the use of fMRI and machine learning for decoding mental states, and developing connectome-based predictive modeling to predict cognitive abilities from brain connectivity.

0376.name: Kristen Tehonica
0376.bio: Dr. Conte investigates the neural coding and sensory processing mechanisms underlying visual perception. Her research addresses how the visual system represents complex information and the neural basis of sensitivity to high-order image statistics. Her contributions include quantifying human visual sensitivity to local image features, identifying neural correlates of sensitivity to high-order correlations, and developing analytical methods for assessing information content in neural data.

0377.name: Kristina J. Nielsen
0377.department: Zanvyl Krieger Mind/Brain Institute; Kavli Neuroscience Discovery Institute
0377.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception and sensorimotor control during natural task performance. Their work addresses how humans utilize visual information to guide goal-directed behaviors, particularly examining the interplay between visual attention, eye movements, and cognitive processes like memory and decision-making. A significant contribution involves pioneering the use of virtual reality environments to demonstrate the task-dependent nature of visual attention and developing computational models of gaze allocation, advancing understanding of eye-head-hand coordination in complex environments.

0378.name: Kristina Visscher
0378.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, attention, and sensorimotor control during natural, goal-directed behavior. Their work addresses how humans integrate visual information with cognitive processes to guide eye movements and actions in complex, real-world environments. A significant contribution is demonstrating the task-dependent nature of visual attention and developing computational models of gaze allocation, furthering understanding of the interplay between cognitive goals, visual input, and motor output.

0379.name: Kristyo Mineff
0379.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive neuroscience of visual perception, focusing on object recognition and perceptual organization. Their work addresses how the brain resolves perceptual ambiguity, the role of memory in perceptual organization, and the interaction of feedforward and feedback mechanisms in visual processing. A significant contribution is demonstrating the iterative nature of perceptual processes, highlighting the influence of past experience and competitive mechanisms in shaping figure-ground perception and object recognition.

0380.name: Krystel Huxlin
0380.bio: Dr. Vaziri-Pashkam investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual object processing and action prediction, focusing on the interaction between visual cognition and motor behavior. Her research addresses how the brain integrates visual information for object recognition, action understanding, and the modulation of object representations by attention across visual cortical regions. Her significant contributions include demonstrating the capacity for action prediction from subtle preparatory movements, identifying functional differences in goal-directed processing between ventral and dorsal visual pathways, and developing object kinematograms to isolate motion-defined object category information.

0381.name: Lana M. Trick
0381.bio: Dr. Diamond investigates the neural mechanisms underlying tactile perception and decision-making, focusing on how sensory information is encoded and processed in the somatosensory system. His research aims to elucidate the neural circuits and computations involved in tactile discrimination, sensory memory, and multisensory integration, particularly concerning the differences between rodent and human tactile cognition. His work has significantly contributed to understanding the role of spike timing in tactile coding, the neural basis of texture discrimination in the rodent whisker system, and the contributions of different somatosensory cortical areas to tactile working memory.

0382.name: Lars F Muckli
0382.bio: This researcher investigates the interplay between oculomotor control, visual attention, and sensorimotor interactions in shaping visual perception. Their work addresses how eye movements and the foveated visual system contribute to a unified perceptual experience, and explores the sensorimotor determinants of visual perception in real-world contexts. Key contributions include demonstrating saccadic suppression of visual processing in early visual cortex, characterizing the time course of chromatic adaptation, and elucidating the dynamics of visual attention during smooth pursuit using EEG.

0383.name: Laura Dugué
0383.institution: Université Paris Cité, CNRS; Institut Universitaire de France
0383.bio: Dr. Davidson investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual awareness, attention, and active perception, particularly within the context of natural movement and immersive virtual reality. His research aims to elucidate how dynamic brain activity and active exploration shape our conscious experience and visual processing. He has significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating dissociations between attention and consciousness using bistable visual illusions and EEG, and by developing novel EEG-VR paradigms to study active perception.

0384.name: Laura Lewis
0384.institution: Boston University; Institute for Medical Engineering and Sciences, Massachusetts Institute of Technology; Massachusetts General Hospital
0384.department: Department of Biomedical Engineering; Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science; Athinoula A. Martinos Center for Biomedical Imaging, Department of Radiology
0384.bio: Dr. Riesenhuber investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying visual object recognition and intelligent behavior. His research focuses on elucidating how the brain extracts meaning from sensory stimuli and how these processes can inform artificial intelligence systems. A significant contribution is the development of the HMAX model, a biologically-inspired computational model of object recognition, and demonstrating the role of prior concept learning in improving generalization within these models.

0385.name: Laura-Isabelle Klatt
0385.institution: Leibniz Research Centre for Working Environment and Human Factors
0385.bio: Dr. Khosla investigates neural information processing and representational alignment between biological and artificial neural networks, particularly within the visual system. Her research addresses how the brain processes information across various domains by exploring the 'what', 'how', and 'why' of neural computation. Dr. Khosla's significant contributions include developing interpretable machine learning tools for understanding structured neural representations in human visual cortex and demonstrating the predictability of cortical responses to naturalistic stimuli using deep neural networks.

0386.name: Laure Pisella
0386.bio: Dr. Peters investigates the computational and neural substrates of perceptual decision-making, metacognition, and subjective conscious experience. Their research focuses on elucidating how the brain represents and utilizes uncertainty in decision processes, the mechanisms underlying confidence judgments, and the relationship between metacognitive processes and qualitative experiences. Dr. Peters has made significant contributions through the development of computational models of metacognition that integrate behavioral, neural, and phenomenological data, and by advancing understanding of the neural correlates of confidence using fMRI and computational approaches.

0388.name: Laurent Madelain
0388.institution: Aix Marseille Université, CNRS; Université de de Lille, CNRS, CHU Lille
0388.department: Institut de Neurosciences de la Timone; UMR 9193 - SCALab - Sciences Cognitives et Sciences Affectives
0388.bio: Dr. Michel investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying human visual perception, focusing on how the visual system integrates sensory information and exploits statistical regularities. Their research addresses fundamental questions regarding probabilistic inference, adaptation to altered statistical contingencies, and the neural basis of visual decision-making. Dr. Michel's significant contributions include developing probabilistic models of visual perception, demonstrating contrast gain control mechanisms in V1, elucidating the role of intrinsic position uncertainty in peripheral vision, and advancing understanding of how neural population response topography contributes to shape perception.

0389.name: Laurie M. Wilcox
0389.institution: Centre for Vision Research, York University; York University
0389.bio: Dr. Beck's research focuses on the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual attention, visual memory, and incidental learning. Her work investigates how attentional capture, memory representations, and prior knowledge interact to shape visual processing and influence performance in complex tasks. Her contributions include developing models of visual cognition in search and change detection, demonstrating the impact of incidental learning on attention, and applying these findings to improve user interface design and training protocols.

0390.name: Leslie Carver
0390.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual attention, memory, and decision-making, particularly their interactions during visual perception. Their research addresses how environmental factors guide attention, how the brain stores and processes visual information, and the role of metacognitive beliefs in visual cognition. Key contributions include elucidating the roles of memory and attention in visual search, developing models of visual clutter, and applying visual cognition research to optimize human-computer interfaces and training techniques.

0391.name: Leyla Isik
0391.bio: Dr. Andersen's research focuses on the intersection of developmental psychopathology and clinical child psychology, specifically concerning neurodevelopmental disorders and trauma. She investigates the efficacy of cognitive-behavioral and family-based interventions for children with autism spectrum disorders and ADHD, as well as the impact of traumatic experiences on psychological well-being. Her contributions include developing and evaluating social skills interventions for children with neurodevelopmental disorders and advancing understanding of family dynamics in these populations.

0392.name: Li Zhaoping
0392.department: Max Planck Institute for Biological Cybernetics
0392.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying cognitive impairment in neurodegenerative diseases and aging, with a focus on multiple sclerosis, mild cognitive impairment, and Alzheimer's disease. Their work addresses questions regarding the efficacy of cognitive rehabilitation strategies, the predictive value of neuroimaging markers for disease progression, and methods for enhancing visuo-spatial working memory. Key contributions include the development of a plasma-based assay for early detection of neuronal loss, demonstration of route-learning training efficacy in older adults, and investigation of sulcal morphometry as a predictor of MCI conversion to AD.

0393.name: Liad Mudrik
0393.bio: Dr. Crognale's research focuses on the neural mechanisms underlying color vision, visual development, and aging, utilizing comparative and human electrophysiological approaches. His work addresses fundamental questions regarding the physiological bases of visual processing, the maturation of chromatic pathways across the lifespan, and the impact of physiological stresses on visual function. A significant contribution is the development and application of the spatiochromatic visual evoked potential (VEP) technique, alongside longitudinal studies characterizing chromatic visual pathway maturation and comparative analyses of visual pigments in diverse species.

0394.name: Lihui Wang
0394.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive neuroscience of human vision, with a focus on visual adaptation, color vision, and face perception. Their work addresses how visual perception adapts to environmental changes and the neural mechanisms underlying these adaptive processes, considering individual and cultural influences. Their significant contributions include the discovery of novel forms of visual adaptation in color, blur, and face perception, alongside characterizing perceptual adjustments to environmental changes and advancing understanding of individual differences in color perception.

0395.name: Lili Sahakyan
0395.bio: Dr. Arcaro investigates the developmental origins of visual system organization using comparative and longitudinal approaches. Her research addresses how intrinsic factors and postnatal experience interact to shape neural architecture supporting visual perception and object recognition across mammalian species. Notably, Dr. Arcaro has demonstrated the experience-dependent emergence of face-selective regions and revealed a hierarchical retinotopic proto-organization in the developing primate visual cortex, highlighting the role of early maps in constraining higher-order visual area development.

0396.name: Linda B. Smith
0396.bio: This researcher investigates computational models of bionic vision and artificial vision systems, focusing on neural interfaces and visual prosthetics. Their work addresses fundamental questions regarding the optimization of visual information encoding in neural prostheses and the development of effective strategies for restoring sight in individuals with incurable blindness. A significant contribution includes the development of computational models that predict percepts elicited by retinal implants, accounting for axon pathway stimulation, and the advancement of scene simplification techniques for bionic vision using deep learning.

0397.name: Lingyu Gan
0397.bio: Dr. Bonner investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception and semantic processing, particularly focusing on object and scene representation in the human brain. Their research addresses how the visual system extracts semantic meaning from images and how high-dimensional representations in deep neural networks relate to visual cortex processing. Dr. Bonner's work has demonstrated the importance of high-dimensional representations for predicting cortical responses and learning, revealed the influence of vision-language co-occurrence on object representations, and identified computational mechanisms underlying cortical responses to affordance properties.

0398.name: Link Swanson
0398.bio: Dr. Crognale investigates the neural mechanisms underlying color vision, visual development, and the impact of physiological stressors on visual function. Their research addresses how chromatic visual pathways develop and change across the lifespan, the genetic underpinnings of color vision variations, and the physiological bases of visual processing in both normal and stressed conditions. Crognale's significant contributions include the development and application of electrophysiological methods to characterize chromatic processing, elucidating the maturation of visual pathways, and characterizing visual function in both marine species and aviation contexts.

0399.name: Lior Maman
0399.bio: The Bonner Lab investigates the computational and statistical principles underlying visual representations in the human cortex. This research aims to elucidate how the brain encodes abstract conceptual properties of objects and scenes, and to build theoretically-grounded models of visual processing from sensory inputs. Dr. Bonner's significant contributions include demonstrating the encoding of navigational affordances in the occipital place area, revealing the influence of vision-language co-occurrence on object representations, and identifying high-dimensional, scale-free representations in visual cortex.

0400.name: Lisa M. Oakes
0400.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying brain-body interactions, particularly focusing on interoception, emotion regulation, and stress responses. Their work aims to elucidate the relationship between mental processes and physiological states, as well as to understand how these interactions are modulated by emotional and social contexts. A significant contribution involves advancing the understanding of interoceptive processes and developing novel methodologies using virtual reality and ambulatory assessment to study brain-body interactions and their link to cardiac activity in cognitive processes.

0401.name: Lisa Renzi-Hammond
0401.bio: This researcher investigates the spatiotemporal mechanisms underlying feature integration, contextual modulation, and perceptual learning in human visual perception. Their work addresses fundamental questions regarding how the visual system binds features, processes information over time, and how contextual factors influence perception. Their contributions include demonstrating non-retinotopic feature integration, advancing understanding of visual crowding, and proposing novel theories of visual consciousness, significantly impacting the field's understanding of visual processing.

0402.name: Lisa S. Scott
0402.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, focusing on feature integration, contextual modulation, and temporal processing. Their work addresses how the visual system integrates information across space and time, the neural substrates of these processes, and the nature of visual processing deficits in schizophrenia. Key contributions include the development of a two-stage model of feature integration, demonstrating the role of global processing in visual crowding, and identifying specific visual processing abnormalities in schizophrenia.

0403.name: Lotfi B. Merabet
0403.bio: Arcaro's research investigates the developmental and comparative neuroscience of visual system organization across mammalian species. They examine how intrinsic mechanisms and environmental experience shape the neural architecture underlying visual perception and object recognition. Their work has demonstrated a hierarchical retinotopic proto-organization at birth, mapped scene area retinotopy in macaque inferior temporal cortex, and revealed contextual modulation of face-selective neurons, significantly advancing our understanding of visual cortex development and function.

0404.name: Lu-Chun Yeh
0404.institution: Justus Liebig University Gießen; Philipps University Marburg; Technical University Darmstadt
0404.department: Center for Mind, Brain and Behavior
0404.bio: This researcher investigates the neural, cognitive, and computational mechanisms underlying object and face recognition, perceptual expertise, and visual categorization. Their work addresses how the brain transforms 2D retinal images into coherent perceptions and how visual perception interacts with other cognitive processes. They have significantly contributed to the understanding of object recognition through perspective-based encoding, demonstrated the role of experience in face recognition, and advanced the understanding of the fusiform face area's function beyond face processing.

0405.name: Luana Serafini
0405.institution: University of Modena and Reggio Emilia
0405.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, working memory, and attention. Their work addresses questions regarding the processing and storage of visual information, the neural basis of working memory capacity, and the interaction between attention and perception. Key contributions include the development of statistical methods for analyzing visual working memory, demonstrating a discrete capacity limit in visual working memory, and identifying neural correlates of visual working memory in early visual cortex.

0406.name: Lucia Melloni
0406.institution: Research Center One Health Ruhr, University Alliance Ruhr, Ruhr-Universität Bochum; Department of Neurology, New York University Grossman School of Medicine
0406.bio: This researcher investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying visual and multisensory perception, particularly in the context of spatial vision and visually guided action. Their work addresses how humans integrate sensory cues, make perceptual decisions under uncertainty, and utilize visual information to control movements. A significant contribution is the development of influential cue combination models, including the "weak fusion" model, alongside advancements in understanding texture perception, depth cue integration, and sensory decision-making.

0407.name: Luiz Henrique Canto-Pereira
0407.institution: Brazilian Ministry of Development, Industry, Commerce and Services
0407.bio: Dr. Webster investigates the role of visual adaptation in shaping the perception of color, form, and faces. His research addresses how neural mechanisms, environmental factors, and individual differences modulate visual adaptation processes to optimize visual coding and processing. His significant contributions include demonstrating adaptation effects in face perception, characterizing color perception's adaptation to natural environments, and elucidating mechanisms of contrast adaptation's effects on color appearance.

0408.name: M.D. Rutherford
0408.bio: This researcher focuses on optimizing and applying functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) methodologies, particularly population receptive field (pRF) mapping, to investigate visual processing in the human brain. Their research addresses biases introduced by fMRI preprocessing steps and explores methods for maximizing reproducibility in neuroimaging studies. Key contributions include developing a containerized pRF mapping pipeline (CON-pRF) and demonstrating the impact of spatial upsampling on foveal pRF coverage, alongside contributing to the development of a 3D-printed brain phantom.

0409.name: Mabel Shanahan
0409.bio: Dr. Schallmo investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying visual perceptual abnormalities in psychiatric conditions, particularly psychosis and autism spectrum disorders. Their research aims to elucidate how neurotransmitter imbalances and altered neural circuitry contribute to atypical visual processing in these populations. Schallmo's work has demonstrated impaired surround suppression in both schizophrenia and autism, revealed the modulatory effects of psilocybin on surround suppression, and identified altered bi-stable visual perception in psychosis, providing valuable insights into the neurobiological basis of visual dysfunction in mental illness.

0410.name: Manish Singh
0410.institution: Rutgers University - New Brunswick; Rutgers University
0410.bio: Dr. Beauchamp investigates the neural substrates of multisensory integration, with a specific focus on audiovisual speech perception. Their research addresses how the superior temporal sulcus (STS) integrates auditory and visual information during human communication and the neural basis of individual variability in these processes. Their work has significantly advanced understanding of the patchy organization of multisensory neurons in the STS, the STS's role in the McGurk effect, and the development of novel analytical approaches for studying multisensory integration using fMRI and iEEG.

0411.name: Marcel Linka
0411.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual temporal processing, focusing on how the brain integrates visual information over time. Their work addresses questions regarding the relationship between aperiodic and oscillatory brain activity, individual differences in temporal processing, and the development of more naturalistic measurement methods. Key contributions include demonstrating a link between aperiodic EEG activity and visual temporal processing variability, and developing the Continuous Temporal Integration (CTI) task for measuring visual temporal integration windows.

0412.name: Margaret Livingstone
0412.bio: Rucci investigates the sensorimotor mechanisms underlying visual perception, focusing on the role of active vision and eye movements in shaping visual input. Their research addresses fundamental questions regarding the computational principles of visual functions, particularly how the brain transforms light into a meaningful visual world through motor behavior. Rucci's significant contributions include demonstrating the enhancement of fine spatial detail perception by fixational eye movements and developing a theory of visual encoding based on the coupling between sensory processing and motor behavior.

0413.name: Margaret McCray
0413.bio: Dr. Greene investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying rapid scene understanding. Her research addresses how humans efficiently extract visual, structural, and semantic information from complex scenes, and how prior knowledge influences perceptual judgments. Her work has demonstrated the role of global scene properties and functional scene characteristics in early visual processing, and has established computational models linking scene statistics to perceptual judgments, further contributing the Visual Experience Database for advancing vision research.

0414.name: Maria Robinson
0414.bio: This researcher focuses on the intersection of kinesiology, cognitive psychology, and neuroscience to investigate the neural mechanisms underlying mobility and cognitive decline in aging populations. Their research addresses the relationship between executive function, physical activity, and fall risk in older adults, utilizing neuroimaging techniques such as resting-state EEG to characterize brain activity patterns. Their contributions include elucidating the potential of exercise interventions to improve brain health, reduce dementia risk, and mitigate fall risk in aging populations.

0415.name: Marianne Maertens
0415.bio: Dr. Eckstein investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying human visual search, attention, and medical image perception. His research addresses how the brain efficiently processes complex visual information, focusing on the interplay between eye movements, covert attention, and decision-making in both controlled and naturalistic environments. He has significantly contributed to the field through computational models of visual search, demonstrating the role of internal noise in limiting search efficiency, and elucidating the influence of peripheral vision and reward on visual attention.

0416.name: Marieke Mur
0416.institution: Schulich School of Medicine & Dentistry; Western University; Faculty of Social Science; Western University
0416.department: Department of Physiology and Pharmacology; Department of Psychology
0416.bio: Due to the lack of specific information regarding this scholar's research, a precise three-sentence technical summary cannot be generated. The available data provides no details on their research focus within vision science. Consequently, their research questions and significant contributions remain unknown. Further information is required to accurately summarize their work.

0417.name: Marion Rouault
0417.bio: Dr. Uetsuki's research focuses on the cognitive processes underlying sentence processing and reading comprehension, particularly within the context of head-final agglutinative languages. Her work investigates the cognitive mechanisms involved in dynamic visual information processing during reading and seeks to quantify and visualize individual differences in reading ability and perceptual processing. Notably, she has developed models of functionally incremental sentence processing in Japanese, established links between reading ability and psychophysical measures of dynamic visual processing, and pioneered methods for visualizing individual perceptual differences using word-based systems.

0418.name: Marisa Carrasco
0418.department: Department of Psychology; Center for Neural Science
0418.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual attention and working memory, particularly focusing on attentional control, visual search, and spatial working memory. Their research addresses how spatial working memory interacts with visual search, the interplay of top-down and bottom-up attentional control, and the impact of cognitive load on selective attention. Their contributions include demonstrating the role of spatial working memory in visual search efficiency, elucidating the interaction between working memory load and selective attention, and clarifying attentional control mechanisms in visual search.

0419.name: Mark B. Neider
0419.bio: Dr. Meng investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying face and object perception, with a focus on developmental and cross-cultural influences. His research addresses questions regarding the neural substrates of visual awareness, the development of specialized brain regions for visual processing, and the cognitive bases of reading acquisition. He has significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating the role of ordinal contrast relationships in face encoding, elucidating neural mechanisms of visual phantoms, and advancing understanding of lateralization in face processing.

0420.name: Mark Beers
0420.bio: Dr. Ding investigates the neural mechanisms underlying cognitive functions and their disruption in neurological disorders, utilizing engineering approaches and advanced neuroimaging techniques. His research focuses on elucidating the role of neuronal oscillations and large-scale brain network dynamics in cognitive processes such as emotional processing, sensorimotor integration, and cognitive aging. A significant contribution is his pioneering application of Granger causality to neuroscience, providing a framework for analyzing directional influences within neural systems, alongside advancements in spectral analysis of cortical event-related potentials and characterization of alpha and beta oscillations.

0421.name: Mark D. Lescroart
0421.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual attention and perceptual decision-making. Their work addresses how attentional mechanisms modulate visual perception, the neural correlates of different forms of attention, and the interaction between spatial and feature-based attention. They have significantly contributed to the field by developing computational models of feature-based attention, demonstrating independent and interactive effects of attention, and elucidating the neural mechanisms of attentional resource deployment.

0422.name: Mark Greenlee
0422.bio: Dr. Kwon investigates the perceptual and neural mechanisms underlying visual deficits in low vision populations. Her research addresses how the visual system adapts to impairments such as blur, contrast loss, and visual field defects, and how these adaptations impact daily activities. Kwon's significant contributions include demonstrating oculomotor adaptability to simulated vision loss, linking retinal ganglion cell density to spatial integration, quantifying binocular imbalance in amblyopia, and characterizing glaucoma's effects on pattern vision and reading.

0423.name: Mark J. Fenske
0423.bio: Dr. Jameson's research investigates the cognitive and affective factors influencing mathematical learning, with a specific focus on math anxiety and mathematical dispositions. Her work examines the developmental trajectory of math anxiety, its relationship with self-efficacy and self-concept, and the impact of mathematical dispositions on academic performance and STEM career choices. Her significant contributions include the development of the Children's Anxiety in Math Scale and the demonstration of time perception's role in math anxiety among adult learners, as well as elucidating the mediating role of math self-efficacy in the anxiety-performance relationship.

0424.name: Mark W. Becker
0424.bio: Based on the provided information, it is impossible to generate a three-sentence technical summary suitable for publication. The information provided only details academic achievements and work history, lacking any specifics about research focus, questions, or contributions within vision science.

0425.name: Markus Lappe
0425.department: Otto Creutzfeldt Center for Cognitive and Behavioral Neuroscience
0425.bio: Dr. Wurm investigates the neural mechanisms underlying action recognition and understanding, focusing on how the brain represents action knowledge. His research addresses how action categories are organized in the lateral occipitotemporal cortex and how the brain encodes abstract representations of action effect structures. He has demonstrated the organization of action categories along sociality and transitivity in LOTC, identified distinct neural representations for body movements and action effects in parietal cortex, and developed dynamic RSA for investigating predictive representations of dynamic events.

0427.name: Marlene Behrmann
0427.institution: University of Pittsburgh; Carnegie Mellon University
0427.bio: Dr. Kapoor investigates the formation of professional identities in computing students across diverse learning contexts. Her research addresses the challenges of fostering equitable computing identities through innovative curricula, instructional strategies, and the strategic utilization of informal learning environments. Dr. Kapoor's work provides empirical insights into how formal and non-formal learning experiences influence students' computing identities and career trajectories, contributing to a more inclusive computing education landscape.

0428.name: Martin A. Giese
0428.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying executive function, particularly in the context of task switching and memory retrieval. Their work addresses how individuals prepare for and execute task switches, the neural substrates involved in cognitive control and conflict monitoring, and the interplay between memory retrieval and executive processes. A significant contribution is the development of a two-component model of task switching, along with investigations into the roles of the prefrontal and posterior parietal cortices in cognitive control.

0429.name: Martin Arguin
0429.institution: Université de Montréal; Centre de recherche, Institut Universitaire de Gériatrie de Montréal
0429.department: Département de psychologie and Centre interdisciplinaire de recherche sur le cerveau et l’apprentissage
0429.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying attentional control during visual search, with a particular emphasis on the interplay between working memory and attentional templates. Their work addresses how humans utilize both positive and negative cues to guide attention, and how these processes are modulated by factors such as reward and proactive control. A key contribution is demonstrating the dynamic shift from working memory to long-term memory representations in attentional control during repeated visual search, and elucidating the role of negative templates in improving attentional efficiency.

0430.name: Martin Eimer
0430.bio: Dr. Carlisle investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual attention and its interaction with working memory. Her research addresses how attentional control is implemented during visual search, focusing on the interplay between target enhancement, distractor suppression, and the influence of prior knowledge. Carlisle's work has significantly advanced understanding of attentional templates, demonstrating their storage in visual working memory and revealing the contributions of both target enhancement and distractor suppression to attentional selection.

0431.name: Martin N. Hebart
0431.institution: Max Planck Institute of Human Cognitive and Brain Sciences; Department of Medicine, Justus Liebig University Giessen; Center for Mind, Brain and Behavior, Universities of Marburg, Giessen, and Darmstadt
0431.bio: This researcher investigates the functional organization of the human brain, focusing on specialized cortical regions involved in visual perception and higher-level cognition. Their research aims to elucidate the neural mechanisms underlying face recognition, scene perception, and other domain-specific cognitive processes, as well as their developmental origins. A significant contribution is the identification of functionally selective cortical regions, including the fusiform face area (FFA), parahippocampal place area (PPA), and extrastriate body area (EBA), and the development of the fROI method for their investigation.

0432.name: Martin Rolfs
0432.bio: Dr. Al-Aidroos investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual attention, perception, and memory. His research addresses how attention modulates information processing, how attention and memory guide visual perception, and how the brain prioritizes relevant visual information. His work has demonstrated that attention is biased towards statistical regularities, that top-down attention can modulate coupling between visual cortical areas, and that visual working memory supports inhibition during visual search.

0433.name: Martin Szinte
0433.institution: Institut de Neurosciences de la Timone, CNRS, Aix-Marseille Université
0433.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying shape perception, particularly within peripheral vision and during visual search. Their work addresses how the visual system represents shape information and how contextual cues influence implicit learning in visual search tasks. A significant contribution is the development of Geometrically Restricted Image Descriptors (GRIDs), a novel method for quantifying shape appearance, alongside investigations into contextual cueing effects in manipulated visual search displays.

0434.name: Martin Wiener
0434.bio: Dr. Spiva investigates the cognitive and emotional dimensions of second language acquisition, specifically focusing on learners' self-perceptions. Her research examines how temporal factors and pedagogical approaches modulate learners' perceived competence and affective relationship with the target language. Her contributions advance understanding of the interplay between cognitive processes and emotional experiences in multilingualism, informing strategies for optimizing language learning outcomes.

0435.name: Martina Poletti
0435.bio: Dr. Roach's research focuses on the computational and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, sensory integration, and time perception. He investigates how the human visual system leverages statistical regularities to generate predictions and integrates multisensory information, particularly in audio-visual contexts. His significant contributions include demonstrating rapid statistical learning in perceptual decision-making, elucidating mechanisms of multisensory integration, and developing computational models of time perception and Bayesian inference.

0436.name: Marvin Chun
0436.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive architecture of working memory and its interaction with attention, particularly focusing on capacity limitations. Their research addresses fundamental questions regarding the nature of working memory capacity, its developmental trajectory, and its relationship to selective attention and higher-order cognition. A significant contribution is the proposition of a limited working memory capacity, often cited as the "magical number 4," and the development of the embedded-processes model integrating attention and long-term memory.

0437.name: Mary Conte
0437.bio: This researcher investigates the impact of environmental factors and technology use on cognitive function in older adults. Their work addresses the challenges of assessing cognitive performance in ecologically valid settings and understanding the predictors of technology adoption in aging populations. Key contributions include the development of the Mobile Device Proficiency Questionnaire (MDPQ), pioneering the use of ecological momentary assessment for cognitive studies, and identifying air pollution exposure as a significant factor affecting cognitive performance in older adults.

0438.name: Mary Hayhoe
0438.bio: Dr. Matthews investigates the psychophysical mechanisms underlying spatial and temporal vision, with a focus on perceptual learning. His research addresses how attentional processes modulate perception across visual and auditory modalities and explores the neural correlates of temporal order judgments. His work has demonstrated dissociable mechanisms for simultaneity and temporal order judgments, revealed a left visual field advantage in temporal processing, and shown that global depth perception modulates local timing sensitivity.

0439.name: Mary Peterson
0439.bio: Dr. Zhong investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying face perception, with a particular focus on the role of eye movements and individual differences. His research addresses how idiosyncratic fixation patterns relate to holistic face processing and influence both behavioral performance and underlying neural activity. Dr. Zhong's work has demonstrated that distinct eye-movement strategies during face viewing are associated with differential holistic processing abilities and modulate specific components of event-related potentials, notably the N170 and P1.

0440.name: Maryam Vaziri-Pashkam
0440.department: Movement and Visual Perception Lab; Department of Psychological & Brain Sciences
0440.bio: Dr. Gaspelin investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying selective attention and cognitive control during visual search. Their research addresses how the brain filters distracting sensory information, focusing on the interplay between salience, task relevance, and active suppression. A significant contribution is the development of the signal suppression hypothesis and the identification of the Distractor Positivity (PD) ERP component as neural correlates of active distractor suppression, providing evidence for proactive attentional control.

0441.name: Masataka Sawayama
0441.bio: Dr. Cruz investigates the cognitive science of reasoning and decision-making, specifically focusing on how humans process uncertain and changeable information. Her research addresses how individuals represent and learn concepts, particularly conditionals, and how these representations influence inference and decision-making under uncertainty. Dr. Cruz's significant contributions include developing Bayesian reasoning models with logical connectives, establishing probabilistic accounts of conditional reasoning, and proposing the centering account of conditionals, all supported by computational modeling.

0442.name: Mathew Diamond
0442.bio: Dr. Cesana-Arlotti investigates the ontogenetic origins and developmental trajectory of logical reasoning abilities in infants and young children. Her research addresses the fundamental questions of how logical cognition emerges, how it empowers early learning potentials, and how it interfaces with language and perception. Cesana-Arlotti's work has demonstrated that preverbal infants possess precursors of logical reasoning, utilize elementary logical representations for hypothesis pruning, and exhibit measurable physiological markers of logical inference, thereby establishing the presence of early logical competence.

0443.name: Matteo Valsecchi
0443.bio: Dr. Chen investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perceptual learning, cortical plasticity, and statistical learning in both normal and impaired vision. Her research addresses how perceptual learning modifies functional specializations in visual cortical areas, the neural basis of visual attention and crowding, and cortical reorganization following vision loss. Through combined fMRI, TMS, psychophysics, and computational modeling, she has demonstrated that perceptual learning can alter cortical tuning and enhance cortico-cortical communication, challenging traditional views of fixed cortical function.

0444.name: Matthew Davidson
0444.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception and attention, with a particular focus on the role of neuronal oscillations. Their work addresses how spontaneous brain oscillations, particularly in the alpha band, modulate visual processing and influence attentional selection. Key contributions include demonstrating the periodic sampling of visual attention by EEG oscillations and establishing the inhibitory role of prestimulus oscillations on perception through response gain modulation.

0445.name: Maurice Ptito
0445.institution: University of Montreal; Technical University of Denmark
0445.bio: This researcher focuses on computational neuroscience, specifically investigating the neural mechanisms underlying visual object recognition and scene understanding. Their work aims to develop computational models, particularly deep convolutional neural networks, that accurately predict neural representations and behavior in visual tasks. A significant contribution is the development and application of representational similarity analysis (RSA) for comparing neural activity patterns across modalities and species, alongside demonstrating the predictive power of deep learning models for primate visual cortex.

0446.name: Maximilian Riesenhuber
0446.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and computational mechanisms underlying human sensorimotor control, decision-making, and intuitive physics, particularly within the context of object interaction. Their work addresses how humans integrate these processes in naturalistic tasks and how intuitive physical reasoning influences visuomotor decisions. Key contributions include demonstrating the transfer of Newtonian physics-consistent intuitive physical reasoning to visuomotor tasks, developing mixed reality paradigms for studying sensorimotor behavior, and applying probabilistic generative models to understand human decision-making in complex tasks.

0447.name: Meenakshi Khosla
0447.bio: Dr. Atilgan investigates the psychophysical and cognitive factors influencing digital readability, particularly the impact of text format variables on reading performance. Her research addresses how individual differences and neural mechanisms mediate optimal reading formats, aiming to personalize digital reading interfaces for enhanced comprehension. Her contributions include developing psychophysical methods for assessing online format readability and demonstrating the benefits of personalized text formats on reading speed and comprehension, leading to adaptive reading technologies.

0448.name: Megan A. K. Peters
0448.institution: University of California, Irvine; Canadian Institute for Advanced Research
0448.bio: Dr. Rungratsameetaweemana investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying cognitive flexibility and adaptive behavior within the cortical hierarchy. Her research addresses how sensory information is transformed into optimal behaviors through specific coding schemes and how neural circuits flexibly map inputs to actions based on context and goals. Her contributions include demonstrating preserved statistical learning in hippocampal lesion patients, developing biologically-inspired deep learning architectures, and elucidating the role of noise in recurrent neural network stability for working memory.

0449.name: Megan Broderick
0449.bio: Dr. Cha investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual perception and visual awareness, with a particular emphasis on ensemble perception, object recognition, and attention distribution. Her research addresses questions regarding the distinctiveness of ensemble perception from object recognition, the relationship between attention distribution and multitasking ability, and the cognitive processes involved in masked face recognition. Through psychophysical methods and individual differences approaches, Dr. Cha has demonstrated a domain-general ensemble perception ability, a positive correlation between multitasking and attention distribution, and unique variability in masked face recognition, contributing to a more nuanced understanding of visual cognition.

0450.name: Megumi Kobayashi
0450.bio: This researcher investigates the neural and computational substrates of visuo-motor coordination and perceptual inference. Their work aims to elucidate the mechanisms by which the brain integrates sensory information to guide motor actions and construct a coherent understanding of the visual environment. Key contributions include characterizing neural mechanisms underlying visual perception and motor control, and developing computational models that simulate these complex processes.

0451.name: Melchi M. Michel
0451.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying voluntary behavior, particularly focusing on the role of the basal ganglia in oculomotor control and value-based decision-making. Their work addresses how emotional experiences and motivational states influence eye movements and object selection by examining the interactions between different brain areas. This scientist has significantly contributed to the field by elucidating the functions of the substantia nigra pars reticulata, caudate nucleus, and dopamine neurons in saccadic control, reward processing, and learning.

0452.name: Melissa Beck
0452.bio: Dr. Kreichman investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying visual processing across the visual field, with a particular emphasis on parafoveal and peripheral vision. Her research aims to elucidate how eccentricity modulates the processing of visual information in high-level areas such as the fusiform face area (FFA) and parahippocampal place area (PPA), and how it affects visual memory. Dr. Kreichman's work has demonstrated the impact of image properties like size and contrast on visual memory during naturalistic encoding, and revealed category-dependent differences in the BOLD eccentricity effect between FFA and PPA.

0453.name: Mette Elmose Andersen
0453.bio: Dr. Layton investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, particularly focusing on optic flow processing, self-motion perception, and figure-ground segregation. Her research addresses how the brain integrates motion signals for robust navigation and object perception, and how biologically-inspired algorithms can enhance machine vision systems. Dr. Layton's significant contributions include developing neural models of optic flow processing in areas MT and MSTd, elucidating mechanisms for perceptual stability during self-motion, and proposing neural dynamics models for figure-ground segregation.

0454.name: Micaela Mitolo
0454.bio: Dr. Binda investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception and attention, with a particular focus on pupillometry as a window into these processes. Her research addresses how top-down cognitive influences modulate pupil size and visual perception, and seeks to elucidate the neural substrates of individual differences in visual processing. Dr. Binda's work has significantly advanced the field by demonstrating the sensitivity of pupil size to perceptual and cognitive factors beyond luminance, establishing pupillometry as an objective correlate of conscious perception, and identifying links between pupillary responses, visual perception, and autistic traits.

0455.name: Michael A. Crognale
0455.bio: Dr. Mamassian investigates the computational mechanisms underlying human visual perception, focusing on how the brain interprets ambiguous sensory information and integrates information across time and sensory modalities. His research addresses how prior knowledge, expectations, and temporal dynamics influence perceptual inference and the formation of confidence judgments in perceptual decision-making. Mamassian's significant contributions include pioneering the application of Bayesian decision theory to model visual perception, elucidating the role of prior knowledge in 3D shape perception, and developing models of perceptual confidence and metacognition.

0456.name: Michael A. Webster
0456.bio: This researcher investigates the neural and perceptual mechanisms underlying motion perception, spatial vision, color vision, attentional processes, and object recognition, with a particular interest in the interplay between art and brain function. Their research addresses how the visual system constructs stable percepts from dynamic retinal input, elucidating the mechanisms of visual attention and the brain's representation of object position and motion. Notably, they are recognized for the discovery of the double-drift illusion, the development of attentional sprite theory, and pioneering work on parallel shape analysis in color and luminance pathways, significantly advancing the understanding of visual processing and attentional modulation.

0457.name: Michael Arcaro
0457.bio: This researcher investigates the impact of aging on visual perception and cognition, with a focus on face perception, perceptual learning, and visual attention. Their work addresses questions regarding the neural mechanisms underlying face recognition, the modification of visual processing through perceptual learning across the lifespan, and the factors influencing peripheral target detection. Their significant contributions include demonstrating age-related changes in face perception, developing computational models of perceptual learning, and elucidating the effects of noise on visual processing in both healthy aging and clinical populations.

0458.name: Michael Beyeler
0458.bio: Dr. Dassonville's research focuses on the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying spatial perception, sensorimotor integration, and visual awareness. His work investigates how the brain constructs spatial representations, how different reference frames interact in spatial cognition, and the neural correlates of visual awareness. He has significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating dissociations between action and perception, elucidating the role of distorted egocentric reference frames, and developing novel paradigms to study spatial cognition.

0459.name: Michael Bonner
0459.bio: This researcher investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying visual object and face recognition, particularly focusing on the role of experience and plasticity in shaping visual perception. Their work addresses fundamental questions regarding how the brain learns to interpret visual information, encode objects in memory, and develop visual function in the context of early visual deprivation. Through Project Prakash and computational modeling, they have demonstrated the remarkable capacity for visual learning even after prolonged blindness and proposed the high-initial acuity hypothesis, significantly advancing our understanding of critical periods and adaptive mechanisms in visual development.

0460.name: Michael Gaebler
0460.institution: Max Planck Institute for Human Cognitive and Brain Sciences
0460.bio: The researcher focuses on advancing functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) methodology for improved characterization of human neuronal function. Their research addresses fundamental questions regarding the sources of functional contrast and noise in BOLD fMRI, and seeks to enhance its utility as a clinical tool. This work has yielded significant contributions including pioneering fMRI experiments, the introduction of correlation analysis, development of multi-contrast sequences, and the mapping of cortical layer-specific activity using ultra-high-resolution fMRI.

0461.name: Michael H. Herzog
0461.bio: Dr. Peter Bex investigates the neural and perceptual mechanisms underlying visual processing in both healthy and visually impaired populations. His research addresses fundamental questions regarding the extension of sensory processing models to real-world conditions, the pathological processes in blinding eye diseases, and the development of sensitive diagnostic and rehabilitative methods. Bex's significant contributions include extending visual perception models to dynamic environments, advancing understanding of contrast sensitivity in natural scenes, pioneering work on crowding, and developing novel therapeutic approaches for maximizing residual visual function.

0462.name: Michael J Tarr
0462.bio: Dr. Bex investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying visual perception in dynamic, naturalistic environments. Their research addresses how the visual system processes information from real-world scenes, particularly in the context of eye movements and visual attention, and how these processes are disrupted in blinding eye diseases.  A significant contribution is their pioneering work on visual crowding and peripheral vision, alongside the development of innovative psychophysical methods and computational models for assessing and rehabilitating visual function in clinical populations.

0463.name: Michael K. McBeath
0463.institution: Arizona State University; Max Planck Institute for Empirical Aesthetics
0463.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying predictive processing in visual perception, focusing on how prior knowledge and expectations shape sensory experience. Their work addresses how the brain integrates sensory inputs with prior expectations, and how predictive representations are generated and utilized during visual processing. Using high-resolution neuroimaging and computational modeling, they have demonstrated the role of the hippocampus in generating visual expectations, revealed layer-specific cortical circuits mediating expectation effects, and shown that prior expectations induce prestimulus sensory templates in early visual cortex.

0464.name: Michael Pratte
0464.bio: Peter Neri investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying visual perception of natural scenes, with a specific focus on object segmentation and feature extraction. His research addresses how the human visual system reconstructs local image structure, integrates top-down and bottom-up processes, and achieves efficient feature extraction. Neri's work has demonstrated the critical role of object segmentation in image reconstruction and challenged traditional compartmentalized views of bottom-up and top-down processing in vision, contributing novel paradigms and stochastic characterization methods to the field.

0465.name: Michael S. Landy
0465.bio: Dr. Tse's research investigates the neural mechanisms underlying mid- and high-level human vision, particularly form-motion interactions and volitional mental operations. His work addresses how the brain constructs conscious visual experience, implements volitional attention, and supports imagination through distributed neural representations.  His contributions include the concept of criterial causation, the proposal of rapid synaptic reweighting as a neural code, and the demonstration of rapid form-motion computations in visual experience.

0466.name: Michael Woletz
0466.department: High Field MR Center; Center for Medical Physics and Biomedical Engineering
0466.bio: Dr. Vetter investigates the neural mechanisms underlying multisensory integration and predictive coding in visual perception. Her research addresses how non-visual information, such as auditory input and emotional stimuli, influences visual processing and awareness, particularly in the context of spatial representation and action. Her significant contributions include demonstrating auditory and imagery processing in early visual cortex, revealing unconscious emotional influences on eye movements, and identifying vision-independent action representations in the cortex of congenitally blind individuals.

0467.name: Michael-Paul Schallmo
0467.department: Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences
0467.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and perceptual mechanisms underlying object recognition, spatial reasoning, and perceptual learning. Their work addresses how humans acquire expertise through perceptual learning, particularly in complex domains such as medical image interpretation and STEM education. Key contributions include pioneering studies on infant object perception, the development of visual interpolation theories, and the creation of adaptive learning technologies validated through rigorous psychophysical and behavioral experimentation.

0468.name: Michael. S. Beauchamp
0468.institution: University of Pennsylvania Perelman School of Medicine
0468.bio: This researcher investigates the mechanisms underlying visual object perception, particularly how humans integrate fragmentary spatiotemporal information. Their work addresses how perceptual learning enhances cognitive performance and how these mechanisms can be leveraged across diverse domains. Key contributions include the theory of spatiotemporal relatability, pioneering studies on infant object perception, and the development of Perceptual Learning Modules (PLMs) for improving learning outcomes in mathematics, science, and medical training.

0469.name: Michele Deodato
0469.bio: Dr. Schyns investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying visual cognition, specifically focusing on face perception, object recognition, and scene categorization. His research addresses how the brain extracts and processes visual information, how top-down processes interact with sensory input, and how to reverse-engineer brain algorithms for visual tasks. Schyns's significant contributions include the development of the Bubbles technique, demonstrations of the task-dependence of visual representations, and pioneering work on spatial frequency processing, advancing the understanding of information processing in perceptual decisions.

0470.name: Michele Rucci
0470.bio: This researcher investigates the perceptual requirements for comfortable and realistic virtual and augmented reality experiences. Their work addresses problems related to eye-tracked distortion correction, world-locked rendering, and the behavioral correlates of visual discomfort in immersive environments. Key contributions include developing a VR display system simulator, conducting the first user study on perceptual requirements for eye-tracked optical distortion correction, and identifying behavioral correlates of visual discomfort in VR.

0471.name: Michelle Greene
0471.bio: Dr. Bao investigates the neural mechanisms underlying high-level visual cognition, specifically focusing on object recognition and scene categorization within the primate brain. His research addresses how complex visual objects are represented in the inferotemporal (IT) cortex and how these representations compare to those in deep learning networks. Dr. Bao's significant contributions include the discovery of the "no man's land" (NML) network in primate IT cortex, the identification of a "map of object space" within IT, and the demonstration of representational similarities between primate IT cortex and deep learning models.

0472.name: Michelle Wong
0472.bio: Dr. Hsieh's research investigates the neural mechanisms underlying consciousness, attention, and decision-making, with a particular focus on unconscious processing. Their work aims to elucidate the neural correlates of consciousness and explore the interactions between attention, consciousness, and multisensory integration. Dr. Hsieh has significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating unconscious processing of complex stimuli like facial attractiveness and syntactic structures, and by identifying pre-existing brain states predictive of subjective choices.

0473.name: Miguel Eckstein
0473.department: Department of Psychological and Brain Sciences; Graduate Program in Dynamical Neuroscience
