0473.bio: Verghese investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception and action, with a specific focus on attention and adaptation in both healthy and visually impaired populations. Her research addresses how the visual system integrates information for object interaction, target selection, and binocular vision, particularly in the context of uncertainty, texture segmentation, and age-related macular degeneration. Verghese's work has demonstrated the capacity for peripheral stereopsis in AMD patients, developed methods for mapping stereopsis across the visual field, and elucidated the role of segmentation and surface grouping in visual search strategies.

0474.name: Mihika Tewari
0474.bio: Dr. Zaidi investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying color vision and 3D shape perception. His research addresses how the visual system extracts three-dimensional structure from two-dimensional retinal images and achieves color constancy in complex, natural environments. His work has significantly advanced understanding of heuristic-based algorithms for color constancy, the role of oriented texture in shape perception, and neural decoding problems related to color and 3D form.

0475.name: Miki Uetsuki
0475.bio: Dr. Chen's research focuses on the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying reinforcement learning, cognitive flexibility, and numerical cognition. Their work aims to elucidate how computational models can explain psychological processes and how computational psychiatry can improve the understanding and treatment of mental disorders. Dr. Chen has significantly contributed to the field through the development of computational models of reinforcement learning, advancing the understanding of cognitive flexibility via neuroimaging, and contributing to the understanding of mental numerical representation.

0476.name: Min-Shik Kim
0476.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual processing, attention, perception, and memory, with a focus on applications to visual impairment and human factors. Their work addresses how visual processes are disrupted in visual disorders and how principles of visual processing can inform accessible design and autonomous driving safety. Key contributions include the discovery of the "weak conquer the strong" illusion, demonstrations of depth order storage in working memory, characterization of short-term memory for location in depth, and development of methods for simulating visibility under low vision conditions.

0477.name: Ming Meng
0477.institution: Philosophy and Social Science Laboratory of Reading and Development in Children and Adolescents, South China Normal University
0477.bio: Dr. Vuong's research investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual object and face perception, with a particular emphasis on multisensory integration. His work addresses how humans integrate spatial and temporal information across sensory modalities and how dynamic facial cues contribute to face recognition and social perception. Dr. Vuong has significantly advanced the field by demonstrating the independent contribution of motion to object recognition, revealing the robustness of nonrigid motion cues across species, and elucidating the role of surface pigmentation in object and face recognition.

0478.name: Mingzhou Ding
0478.department: J Crayton Pruitt Family Department of Biomedical Engineering
0478.bio: Dr. Conway investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, with a particular emphasis on color vision and sensory-cognitive information processing in the primate visual cortex. His research addresses how sensory signals are transformed into perceptions and actions, focusing on the organizational principles and information processing within inferior temporal cortex. He has significantly contributed to the field by characterizing color-selective regions in extrastriate cortex, elucidating the multi-stage parallel processing organization of inferior temporal cortex, and reconciling relativist and universalist accounts of color naming, including providing the standard scientific account of #TheDress.

0479.name: Miranda Scolari
0479.bio: Dr. Denison investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, attention, and decision-making, with a particular focus on temporal dynamics. Their research addresses how the brain generates ongoing visual experience, how attention selects relevant information in dynamic environments, and how these processes influence perceptual decisions. Denison's work has demonstrated the incorporation of attention-dependent uncertainty into perceptual decisions, advanced a dynamic normalization model of temporal attention, and challenged fixed-criterion models of perceptual decision-making, contributing significantly to our understanding of the temporal constraints on visual processing.

0480.name: MiYoung Kwon
0480.bio: Dr. Kelly's research focuses on the intersection of human cognition, perception, and motor control in the context of human-system interaction and risk communication. She investigates how cognitive and perceptual processes influence behavior in diverse settings, including product usage, driving, and occupational environments, with a particular emphasis on optimizing safety information. Her contributions include the development and evaluation of effective warning systems, accident analysis informed by human factors principles, and the application of cognitive neuroscience to understand skilled motor performance.

0481.name: Molly Jameson
0481.bio: Dr. Pye's research focuses on cross-linguistic manifestations of dyslexia and the development of culturally appropriate reading assessments for multilingual populations. Her work addresses the challenges of accurately identifying reading abilities in diverse linguistic contexts, particularly within Southeast Asia, and examines the influence of language profiles on assessment methodologies. Dr. Pye has significantly contributed to the field through the development of reading assessment methods for multilingual populations, investigation of digital reading's impact on comprehension, and exploration of neuromyth prevalence in educational settings.

0482.name: Molly Setchfield
0482.bio: This researcher investigates the influence of facial features, particularly eyebrows, on attractiveness perception within the frameworks of evolutionary psychology and social cognition. Their work addresses how eyebrow characteristics, sexual dimorphism, and facial contrast contribute to attractiveness judgments, considering the moderating effects of age and cultural context. They have significantly contributed to the understanding of how eyebrow characteristics, such as thickness and luminance, impact perceived attractiveness, demonstrating age-related differences in these perceptions.

0483.name: Monica D. Rosenberg
0483.institution: Department of Psychology, The University of Chicago
0483.department: Institute for Mind and Biology; Neuroscience Institute
0483.bio: Dr. Haefner investigates the neural computations underlying visual perception and perceptual decision-making, particularly focusing on how the brain represents and utilizes probabilistic information. His research addresses how populations of cortical neurons encode perceptual beliefs and implement hierarchical Bayesian inference for processing complex visual stimuli. He has made significant contributions by developing computational models linking neural population activity to decision-making, proposing a neural sampling framework for probabilistic inference, and elucidating the role of correlated neural variability in encoding probabilistic information.

0484.name: Moreno I. Coco
0484.bio: Dr. Starrfelt investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual recognition, with a particular focus on category-selective deficits such as alexia and prosopagnosia. Her research addresses questions regarding the organization of visual recognition in the brain and the impact of brain injuries and neurodevelopmental disorders on visual cognition. Her work has significantly advanced the understanding of developmental prosopagnosia, category-selective deficits, and the development of neuropsychological assessment tools for visual perceptual impairments.

0485.name: Moritz Wurm
0485.institution: CIMeC - Center for Mind/Brain Sciences, University of Trento, Italy
0485.bio: Dr. Carson investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms of visual attention and attentional control, particularly focusing on distractor suppression. Her research addresses how the brain selectively processes relevant information while filtering out irrelevant stimuli and the temporal dynamics of these attentional processes. Dr. Carson's significant contribution lies in developing EEG/ERP methodologies to isolate neural activity associated with attentional selection and distractor suppression, thereby elucidating the temporal dynamics of maintaining focus versus diverting attention.

0487.name: Mrigankshi Kapoor
0487.bio: Dr. Bootsma investigates the information-based regulation of human movement, particularly within the domains of perception-action coupling, coordination, and joint action. His research addresses how perceptual information prospectively guides movement, how affordances shape action selection, and the principles governing individual and joint task coordination. Bootsma's significant contributions include demonstrating the use of first-order time-to-contact information in movement regulation and advancing ecological approaches to understanding motor control, coordination, and visual control in interceptive tasks.

0488.name: Muzhen Ai
0488.bio: Dr. Allard investigates the psychophysical mechanisms underlying visual perception, particularly how aging affects motion perception, contrast sensitivity, and attentional processes. His research aims to disentangle the contributions of photon noise, neural noise, and computational efficiency to age-related declines in visual function using equivalent noise paradigms. He has demonstrated that reduced photon absorption in cones, rather than cortical changes, is a primary driver of age-related motion perception deficits and has provided evidence against a dedicated second-order motion system sensitive to high temporal frequencies.

0489.name: Myeong-Ho Sohn
0489.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying conscious visual perception and its relationship to working memory capacity. Their work addresses how the brain integrates information across the perceptual hierarchy to produce conscious experiences, with a focus on refining methodologies for studying neural correlates of consciousness. Key contributions include investigating the relationship between the P300 event-related potential and conscious perception and employing multimodal neuroimaging approaches to understand integration processes.

0490.name: Máté Lengyel
0490.institution: Central European University; University of Cambridge
0490.bio: Dr. Azadi investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, particularly face perception, within the inferotemporal cortex. Their research addresses how neural activity in face-selective populations causally influences visual perception, behavior, and eye movement patterns during face viewing, and how optogenetic stimulation of these regions modulates visual detection. Dr. Azadi's work has demonstrated that inactivation of face-selective neurons alters eye movement patterns and that the detectability of optogenetic stimulation is contingent on concurrent visual input, contributing to a deeper understanding of the causal relationship between neural activity and visual perception.

0491.name: N Apurva Ratan Murty
0491.department: Cognition and Brain Science, School of Psychology; Center of Excellence in Computational Cognition
0491.bio: This researcher investigates the neural circuits underlying visual spatial attention and oculomotor control, with a focus on subcortical contributions. Their work aims to elucidate the neural mechanisms coordinating different eye movement types and how subcortical structures contribute to visual attention and selection. Key contributions include the development of a computational model of smooth pursuit, demonstration of shared neural mechanisms between pursuit and saccades, and identification of the superior colliculus' role in microsaccades, fixation stability, and rapid face detection.

0492.name: Nancy B. Carlisle
0492.bio: Dr. Abrams investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying the interplay between visual attention, motor control, and perception. His research addresses how overt actions and perceptual features influence attentional allocation, motor planning, and the interaction between action and perception systems in guiding behavior. Abrams' significant contributions include the discovery of the "action effect," demonstrating that actions bias subsequent visual attention, and elucidating the interactions between eye movements and covert attention shifts.

0493.name: Nancy Kanwisher
0493.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, attention, and decision-making, with a particular focus on oculomotor control. Their work addresses how brain circuits coordinate visual processing and eye movements, especially the interaction between subcortical structures like the superior colliculus and cortical areas. They have significantly contributed to the field by elucidating the role of the superior colliculus in visual attention and perceptual decision-making, characterizing the neural basis of microsaccades, and developing computational models of smooth pursuit eye movements.

0494.name: Naseem Al-Aidroos
0494.bio: This researcher investigates the mechanisms underlying stereoscopic depth perception and multisensory integration, particularly within virtual and augmented reality environments. Their work addresses how the brain reconstructs three-dimensional percepts from two-dimensional retinal images, and how sensory cues combine to produce coherent spatial orientation. Their contributions include demonstrating the importance of vertical disparities in stereopsis, developing predictive head-tracking algorithms, and characterizing perceptual effects of distortions in stereoscopic displays.

0495.name: Natalia Melnik
0495.bio: A prominent cognitive neuroscientist, Dr. Desimone investigates the neural mechanisms underlying selective visual attention and its modulation by prefrontal cortex. His research addresses how the brain prioritizes relevant stimuli amidst distractions, focusing on feature-based attention and the role of neural synchronization in attentional processing. Dr. Desimone's significant contributions include the discovery of face-selective neurons, the development of the biased competition theory of attention, and the identification of neural synchronization as a key mechanism for attentional selection.

0496.name: Natalie Spiva
0496.bio: Dr. Alexander investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception and expertise, with a focus on oculomotor behavior. Their research addresses how eye movement patterns, particularly microsaccades, relate to visual search strategies, perceptual expertise development, and cognitive states, especially within medical image perception. Dr. Alexander's contributions include characterizing microsaccade behavior in disease, developing eye-tracking methodologies for assessing radiological expertise, and establishing empirical guidelines for eye-tracking research.

0497.name: Nathan H. Heller
0497.department: Center for Psychedelic and Consciousness Research
0497.bio: This researcher investigates the interplay between perceptual and conceptual learning, focusing on how these processes mutually influence each other. Their work addresses questions regarding the mechanisms by which cognitive science principles can be applied to enhance educational outcomes and how computational models can accurately represent human cognition. A significant contribution is demonstrating the bidirectional relationship between perception and cognition, showing how conceptual needs can flexibly tune perceptual processes, alongside developing computational models of concept formation and perceptual learning.

0498.name: Neda Nategh
0498.department: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering
0498.bio: Dr. McPeek investigates the neural substrates of visual attention, eye movements, and visually-guided actions, with a particular focus on sensorimotor integration. Her research addresses how the brain selects targets for saccades and reaches, the neural mechanisms underlying shifts of visual attention, and the interaction between cortical and subcortical areas in controlling these processes. Dr. McPeek's significant contributions include demonstrating the role of the superior colliculus in reach target selection, elucidating saccade trajectory modulation during sequential eye movements, and advancing understanding of inhibitory tagging in the superior colliculus during visual search.

0499.name: Neil W Roach
0499.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying face processing and visual perception, with a particular emphasis on cultural influences. Their work addresses questions regarding how culture shapes face scanning strategies, the neural substrates of face recognition, and the role of eye movements in visual information extraction. Key contributions include demonstrating cultural variations in face scanning patterns, developing the iMap method for statistical fixation mapping, and elucidating the neural representations of faces in relation to eye movements.

0500.name: Nelson Cowan
0500.bio: Dr. Rademaker investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual working memory and its interaction with perception and attention. Her research addresses how sensory and mnemonic representations coexist in visual cortex and how the brain flexibly utilizes different coding schemes for information storage. Rademaker's work has demonstrated coexisting sensory and mnemonic representations, revealed a transition toward categorical representations during working memory, and shown flexible use of spatial- and motor-based codes for visuo-spatial information.

0501.name: Nelson Roque
0501.bio: Dr. Zhang investigates the computational and neural substrates of visual cognition, working memory, and abstract reasoning. Her research addresses fundamental questions regarding the neural mechanisms underlying perceptual learning, prediction error processing, and the cognitive deficits observed in psychiatric disorders. Her significant contributions include developing computational models of visual working memory deficits in schizophrenia, elucidating neural prediction error mechanisms in abstract visual reasoning, and advancing methods for reconstructing continuous language from fMRI signals.

0502.name: Nestor Matthews
0502.bio: Dr. Coen-Cagli investigates the neural computations underlying visual processing, focusing on how the brain encodes, decodes, and integrates visual information within the context of natural scenes. His research addresses how probabilistic inference and statistical regularities shape neural responses and perceptual experience. His work has significantly advanced understanding of divisive normalization, neural representation of uncertainty, and the influence of natural image statistics on cortical processing, contributing to models of contextual modulation and perceptual segmentation.

0504.name: Nicholas Gaspelin
0504.bio: Dr. Rosenholtz investigates the computational mechanisms underlying human visual perception, with a particular focus on peripheral vision, visual crowding, and perceptual organization. Her research aims to elucidate how the visual system encodes information in the periphery and how this encoding impacts attention, clutter perception, and overall scene understanding. Her significant contributions include developing state-of-the-art computational models of peripheral vision and visual crowding, challenging traditional views on visual attention, and pioneering methods for quantifying visual clutter and its influence on perception and human-computer interaction.

0505.name: Nicole Cruz
0505.bio: Dr. Ash investigates the neural mechanisms underlying behavioral flexibility, particularly in the context of autism spectrum disorders, using non-invasive neuromodulation techniques. His research focuses on elucidating how attentional state modulates neuroplasticity induced by repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation and developing closed-loop transcranial ultrasound stimulation paradigms to modulate deep brain structures. Dr. Ash has demonstrated abnormally increased stability of cortical circuits in a mouse model of autism and pioneered applications of transcranial ultrasound stimulation for noninvasive modulation of deep brain structures in humans.

0506.name: Nicolò Cesana-Arlotti
0506.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual perception, focusing on how the visual system represents and encodes complex visual information as ensembles and feature distributions. Their work addresses questions regarding the representation of information in visual memory and the influence of probabilistic ensemble representations on perception and attention. Key contributions include demonstrating the capacity to learn detailed feature distributions of visual ensembles, revealing dissociations between implicit and explicit ensemble representations, and investigating the temporal integration of feature probability distributions in visual working memory.

0507.name: Nihong Chen
0507.bio: Dr. Pitzalis investigates the functional organization of the human visual cortex, with a particular focus on motion perception, egomotion, and visuomotor integration. Her research aims to delineate the neural mechanisms underlying visually-guided navigation and to characterize the spatiotemporal dynamics of visual processing using multimodal neuroimaging techniques. Her significant contributions include the identification of human cortical area V6, the development of wide-field retinotopic mapping techniques, and the elucidation of neural correlates of egomotion-compatible visual stimulation.

0508.name: Niki Pennanen
0508.department: Department of Computer Science; Department of Psychology and Speech-Language Pathology
0508.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying attentional control and working memory in dynamic environments. Their work addresses how prior experiences and temporal expectations proactively shape the prioritization of sensory and motor information in working memory. Key contributions include demonstrating the flexible prioritization of working memory contents based on task demands, revealing the early encoding of prospective action plans, and showing how associative memory templates are biased to optimize context-dependent task performance.

0509.name: Niko A. Busch
0509.bio: Dr. Ling investigates the neural mechanisms underlying the interaction between attention and visual perception, focusing on how limited processing capacity mediates the vast amount of visual information. His research addresses how attention modulates visual perception, alters neural response properties, and how normalization processes regulate competition for visual awareness. His work has demonstrated that attention enhances perceived contrast, impairs perception under certain conditions, alters orientation processing in the lateral geniculate nucleus, and affects the gain and tuning of population responses in visual cortex, further developing computational models of attentional influence on contrast response functions.

0510.name: Nikolaus Kriegeskorte
0510.bio: Dr. Yousif investigates the cognitive and perceptual mechanisms underlying spatial cognition, numerical estimation, and temporal memory. Their research addresses how humans represent area, shape, and topological relations, as well as the processes involved in event segmentation and the influence of visual adaptation. Yousif's work has demonstrated the use of additive area heuristics in area judgments, identified biases in spatial representations, revealed the role of topological features in visual tasks, and critically examined number adaptation effects, contributing to a deeper understanding of visuospatial processing.

0511.name: Nikolaus Weiskopf
0511.department: Wellcome Centre for Human Neuroimaging; Felix Bloch Institute for Solid State Physics
0511.bio: Dr. Yousif investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying spatial representation, numerical cognition, and temporal memory. Their research addresses how humans perceive and represent spatial information, the formats of visuospatial representation, and the development of these cognitive abilities across the lifespan. Dr. Yousif's contributions include demonstrating systematic biases in spatial perception, revealing the flexibility of spatial representation formats, and critically examining the implications of number adaptation for numerical cognition.

0512.name: Nils Neupaertl
0512.bio: Dr. Chong investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual perception, particularly how the visual system processes complex scenes despite limited attentional resources. Her research addresses how ensemble perception interacts with focused attention, influences category learning, and modulates visual awareness, including the neural correlates of these processes. Dr. Chong's work has significantly advanced the understanding of ensemble perception as a fundamental mechanism for extracting statistical summaries from visual scenes and has elucidated the role of attention in binocular rivalry and visual consciousness.

0513.name: Nilsu Atilgen
0513.bio: Dr. Lee investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, with a particular focus on binocular rivalry and perceptual decision-making. This research aims to determine how the brain resolves conflicting visual information, identify neural correlates of visual awareness, and model the interaction between top-down and bottom-up processes. Dr. Lee's work has demonstrated multi-stage competition in binocular rivalry, identified neural signatures of visual awareness in V1, and developed computational models predicting the dynamics of bistable perception and visual grouping.

0514.name: Nitzan Censor
0514.institution: Tel Aviv University; Tel Aviv University
0514.department: Sagol School of Neuroscience; School of Psychological Sciences
0514.bio: Dr. Inati investigates the neurophysiological mechanisms underlying drug-resistant focal epilepsy, focusing on improving seizure focus localization. Her research addresses the optimization of neuroimaging and neurophysiological techniques for identifying epileptic foci and elucidating the pathophysiology of interictal discharges and seizure propagation. Dr. Inati has significantly contributed to the field by developing novel methods for analyzing interictal discharges using traveling wave and white matter propagation models, and by advancing multimodal neuroimaging techniques for presurgical evaluation in epilepsy.

0515.name: Nuttida Rungratsameetaweemana
0515.bio: Dr. Lamer investigates the visual and cognitive mechanisms underlying the perception of social categories and the transmission of social biases. Their research addresses how subtle visual cues, such as spatial position and nonverbal behavior, shape the perception of gender, race, and group emotions, and how these perceptions contribute to the maintenance of social inequities. Lamer's contributions include demonstrating the influence of vertical spatial cues on gender perception, identifying the role of emotional patterns in social media in transmitting race essentialism, and developing the cultural snapshots methodology for analyzing social contexts.

0516.name: Oakyoon Cha
0516.bio: Dr. Shomstein investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying attentional selection, focusing on how the brain prioritizes information for processing. Her research addresses how factors such as reward, uncertainty, and object knowledge modulate attentional allocation and interact with perception and decision-making. Her work has demonstrated the influence of reward history on attentional selection, elucidated the role of parietal cortex in auditory attention, and developed a dynamic prioritization framework for understanding attentional control.

0517.name: OH-SANG KWON
0517.institution: Ulsan national institute of science and technology
0517.bio: Dr. Waugh's research focuses on the psychophysical mechanisms underlying spatial vision and visual development, particularly in paediatric populations with amblyopia. Her work investigates the impact of visual acuity and crowding on reading development, the neural substrates of amblyopia, and the processing of first- and second-order spatial information. Dr. Waugh has significantly contributed to the field through the development of novel visual acuity and crowding tests for amblyopia detection, insights into spatial scale shifts in peripheral vernier acuity, and advancements in understanding temporal modulation processing at varying retinal illuminance levels.

0518.name: Okihide Hikosaka
0518.bio: Dr. Varma investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying human cognition, with a specific focus on mathematical reasoning and language understanding. Their research addresses how computational models can elucidate human cognitive processes, particularly in complex domains, and how human cognitive principles can enhance machine learning models. Dr. Varma's significant contributions include developing computational models of mathematical thinking, investigating the cognitive alignment between humans and language models, and applying cognitive science principles to improve machine learning and educational practices.

0519.name: Olga Kreichman
0519.bio: Dr. Murray investigates the neural and computational bases of visual perception and attention, with a particular focus on neurodevelopmental conditions such as autism spectrum disorder. Their research addresses how contextual modulation shapes visual processing, how attention influences neural activity in visual cortex, and the nature of atypical visual processing in neurodivergent populations. Dr. Murray's contributions include demonstrating attentional modulation of neural selectivity in the lateral occipital complex and elucidating the role of divisive normalization in visual processing, including its potential alterations in autism.

0520.name: Oliver Layton
0520.bio: This researcher focuses on the application of plasma science to enhance combustion processes, utilizing advanced laser diagnostics. Their work addresses fundamental questions regarding plasma-chemistry interactions in low-temperature plasmas and the enhancement of combustion efficiency through plasma-assisted processes. A significant contribution includes the development and application of ultrafast coherent anti-Stokes Raman scattering (CARS) spectroscopy for investigating plasma-assisted combustion, particularly in the context of deflagration to detonation transition.

0521.name: Olivia S. Cheung
0521.department: Center for Brain and Health, NYUAD Research Institute
0521.bio: Dr. Paik investigates the computational and theoretical neuroscience of visual perception and cognitive intelligence, focusing on the development of functional architectures in biological and artificial neural networks. Their research addresses the origins of innate cognitive functions, the mechanisms shaping visual cortex organization, and the principles governing behavioral diversity in multi-agent systems. Paik's significant contributions include demonstrating spontaneous emergence of facial image selectivity in untrained deep networks, identifying the retino-cortical mapping ratio as a determinant of cortical organization, and explaining the role of spontaneous retinal waves in visual cortex development.

0522.name: Olivier Collignon
0522.department: The Sense Innovation and Research Center
0522.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying episodic memory, particularly focusing on memory search and retrieval processes. Their work addresses how the brain targets and retrieves specific memories, the reasons for retrieval failures, and the dynamics of memory search. A significant contribution is the development of the Context Maintenance and Retrieval (CMR) model of organizational processes in free recall, alongside advancements in understanding associative retrieval and the neural representation of context, often employing multi-voxel pattern analysis of fMRI data.

0523.name: Paola Binda
0523.bio: Dr. Frank investigates the neurobiological underpinnings of perceptual and tactile learning, with a specific focus on lifespan changes in brain plasticity. Their research aims to elucidate the neural mechanisms governing learning transfer and the modulatory role of neurotransmitters, such as GABA, across different age groups. Through multimodal neuroimaging and psychophysical techniques, Dr. Frank has demonstrated age-related differences in GABAergic modulation during visual perceptual learning and revealed cortical coactivation patterns supporting tactile learning transfer.

0524.name: Pascal Mamassian
0524.bio: This researcher focuses on applying computational neuroscience techniques to the early detection and classification of cognitive impairment. Their work addresses the challenges of identifying mild cognitive impairment and understanding long-term memory deficits through the integration of neuropsychological assessments and neuroimaging data. A significant contribution includes the development of a novel deep learning framework incorporating multiple neuropsychological tests to detect MCI with improved accuracy and interpretability.

0525.name: Patrick Bennett
0525.bio: Dr. Tagoh investigates the effects of visual adaptation on fundamental aspects of visual perception, including acuity and crowding. His research aims to elucidate the mechanisms by which flicker adaptation modulates visual function and to quantify the prevalence and determinants of refractive errors in underserved populations. Dr. Tagoh's contributions include demonstrating that flicker adaptation can improve visual acuity by reducing crowding and characterizing the significant burden of uncorrected refractive errors in rural Zimbabwe.

0526.name: Patrick Cavanagh
0526.bio: Dr. Favila investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying the formation, transformation, and retrieval of episodic memories, particularly focusing on the interplay between visual perception and memory-guided behavior. Her research addresses how cognitive demands and neural constraints shape the content and format of memories, and how these memory transformations influence subsequent adaptive behavior. Dr. Favila's contributions include demonstrating the flexible alignment of parietal cortex representations during memory retrieval, characterizing distinct spatial tuning properties in visual cortex for perception and memory, and identifying hippocampal mechanisms that prevent interference during learning.

0527.name: Paul Dassonville
0527.bio: This researcher investigates the developmental interrelations between visual attention and visual short-term memory in infancy. Their work addresses how attentional mechanisms influence the emergence of foundational cognitive skills and the underlying neural substrates supporting these developments. A significant contribution includes the development of the Infant Orienting With Attention (IOWA) task and the demonstration of attentional phenotypes in infancy that predict visual cognitive performance.

0528.name: Paul Linton
0528.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual information processing, perceptual learning, and conscious perception across the cortical hierarchy. Their work seeks to elucidate how the brain represents visual stimuli, acquires perceptual skills, and integrates conscious and unconscious processes in visual cognition. A significant contribution is the Reverse Hierarchy Theory of visual perceptual learning and conscious perception, alongside pioneering work on retinal ganglion cell classifications and attentional control in early perceptual learning.

0529.name: Pawan Sinha
0529.institution: MIT Department of Brain and Cognitive Sciences
0529.department: Department of Brain and Cognitive Sciences
0529.bio: Dr. Wallace investigates personalized information interactions, focusing on optimizing digital text formats for diverse readers. Her research addresses the challenges of tailoring readability to individual needs and developing public research systems that augment human information processing. A significant contribution is the development of the Virtual Readability Lab and the demonstration of substantial reading speed improvements through personalized font selection, alongside contributions to collaborative data maintenance systems.

0530.name: Peter Bandettini
0530.bio: Dr. He's research investigates the neural substrates of visual perception, attention, and awareness, with a particular focus on elucidating the mechanisms underlying face processing and binocular rivalry. His work addresses fundamental questions regarding how the human brain processes visual information, including the contributions of distinct visual pathways and the neural correlates of conscious and unconscious perception. Dr. He has significantly advanced the field through the identification of attentional resolution limits, the characterization of cortical responses to invisible stimuli, and the development of novel paradigms for studying visual awareness.

0531.name: Peter Bex
0531.bio: Dr. Cao investigates the developmental cognitive neuroscience of multisensory processing, specifically focusing on visual, auditory, and crossmodal integration in infants and young children. Their research addresses the neural mechanisms underlying the development of multisensory processing and how this integration contributes to early cognitive development. Dr. Cao's work has significantly contributed to understanding the development of audiovisual integration, identifying neural correlates of multisensory processing, and establishing methodological approaches for studying multisensory perception in young populations.

0532.name: Peter J Kohler
0532.bio: This researcher investigates the perceptual and neural mechanisms underlying color vision, face processing, and material perception, employing psychophysical and neuroimaging techniques. Their work addresses fundamental questions regarding the processing of color information, the neural basis of visual perception, and the development of technologies for visualizing invisible information. Key contributions include the development of a color vision deficiency simulation filter, advancements in understanding visual preference and material perception, and pioneering the application of spectral imaging for diverse applications.

0533.name: Peter Kok
0533.institution: UCL Queen Square Institute of Neurology, University College London
0533.bio: This researcher investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, with a particular emphasis on material (*Shitsukan*) and motion processing. Their work addresses how the visual system integrates complex information about object properties across space, time, and sensory modalities. Key contributions include pioneering the study of *Shitsukan* perception, developing novel psychophysical methods for motion analysis, and elucidating temporal binding mechanisms in multisensory integration.

0534.name: Peter Neri
0534.bio: Dr. Shimojo investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying multisensory integration, emotional decision-making, and implicit cognitive processes. His research aims to elucidate how the brain integrates information across sensory modalities, how implicit processes influence perception and decision-making, and the neural correlates of social cognition. Shimojo's significant contributions include the discovery of the gaze cascade effect in preference formation, the elucidation of postdictive processes in visual perception, and the development of the different pathway model for crossmodal interactions.

0535.name: Peter U. Tse
0535.bio: This researcher investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying human sensory perception, with a focus on visual, tactile, and multisensory integration. Their work addresses fundamental questions regarding the processing of motion, shape, color, and material properties (*Shitsukan*) within the human brain. Key contributions include elucidating higher-order motion processing, advancing understanding of temporal binding, and pioneering interdisciplinary approaches to material perception, integrating psychophysics, neuroscience, and computer science.

0536.name: Petra Vetter
0536.bio: Dr. Yuval-Greenberg investigates the interplay between oculomotor control, visual perception, and attention using a multi-modal approach. Her research addresses how eye movements, particularly microsaccades, influence and reflect cognitive processes such as attention, memory, and temporal perception. A significant contribution is the identification of microsaccades as a source of induced gamma-band activity in EEG, fundamentally altering the interpretation of high-frequency neural oscillations and establishing eye movements as retrieval cues in episodic memory.

0537.name: Philip J. Kellman
0537.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying the perception and processing of facial and bodily expressions of emotion. Their work addresses how sensorimotor experiences shape emotion recognition and social cognition, and seeks to identify biomarkers for disorders affecting facial expression processing. Key contributions include demonstrating the causal role of the superior temporal sulcus in facial expression processing and identifying parallel processing streams for facial expression and head orientation.

0538.name: Philipp Kaniuth
0538.institution: Justus Liebig University Giessen; Max Planck Institute for Human Cognitive and Brain Sciences Leipzig
0538.department: Center for Mind, Brain and Behavior, Universities of Marburg, Giessen and Darmstadt
0538.bio: Dr. Buetti investigates the cognitive and attentional mechanisms underlying visual search, with a particular focus on parallel processing and distractor rejection. Her research aims to elucidate how emotional factors, individual differences, and top-down/bottom-up processes modulate attentional allocation and visual search performance in complex scenes. Dr. Buetti's significant contributions include the Target Contrast Signal Theory, demonstrating exhaustive parallel processing of distractors, quantifying distractor-distractor interactions, and elucidating the role of perceived control in emotion-induced temporal distortions.

0539.name: Philipp Kreyenmeier
0539.department: Graduate Program in Neuroscience; School of Audiology; Djavad Mowafaghian Center for Brain Health; Institute for Computing, Information, and Cognitive Systems
0539.bio: Dr. Garobbio investigates the neural and perceptual underpinnings of individual differences in human vision, particularly in the contexts of healthy aging and schizophrenia. Her research seeks to elucidate the factors contributing to the heterogeneity of visual processing across individuals and populations. Her doctoral thesis, "Diving into the heterogeneity of the visual system," provides insights into the variability of visual perception and its potential neural correlates, with implications for understanding visual processing in both healthy and clinical populations.

0540.name: Philippe G. Schyns
0540.bio: This researcher investigates the neural substrates of sensorimotor integration, focusing on the interplay between action and perception in object processing. Their work addresses how the brain transforms sensory information into motor commands and how intended actions modulate visual processing within the cortex. Key contributions include demonstrating the activation of early visual cortex during haptic exploration and revealing action-specific modulations in visual areas during object-oriented tasks.

0541.name: Phillip Guan
0541.bio: This researcher investigates the neuroplastic mechanisms underlying chronic pain conditions, focusing on maladaptive changes within the motor cortex. Their work aims to identify cortical biomarkers predictive of pain sensitivity and chronicity risk, and to optimize non-invasive brain stimulation techniques for pain management. Key contributions include the identification of peak alpha frequency and corticomotor excitability as predictive biomarkers, the demonstration of motor cortex reorganization in chronic pain, and the development of novel brain stimulation protocols to modulate pain processing.

0542.name: Pinglei Bao
0542.institution: School of Psychological and Cognitive Sciences; Peking Univ., Beijing, China
0542.department: IDG/McGovern Institute for Brain Research; Peking-Tsinghua Center for Life Sciences
0542.bio: Dr. DeWitt investigates the neural mechanisms underlying olfactory system dysfunction following traumatic brain injury and hypoxic exposure. Her research aims to elucidate the processes of neurodegeneration and neuroinflammation within olfactory circuits in response to injury. While early in her doctoral career, her work promises to contribute to understanding the relationship between brain trauma, sensory processing, and potential therapeutic interventions.

0543.name: Po-Jang (Brown) Hsieh
0543.bio: Dr. Park investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying high-level visual cognition, specifically focusing on visual scene understanding. Their research addresses how the brain constructs scene representations by integrating visual, spatial, temporal, and functional properties to guide action and navigation. Dr. Park's contributions include demonstrating the constructive nature of scene understanding beyond physical input and elucidating the complementary roles of brain regions in multi-level scene processing, particularly regarding functional entities within scenes.

0544.name: Preeti Verghese
0544.bio: Dr. Vinci-Booher investigates the neural substrates of literacy acquisition, focusing on the interplay between motor experience, visual perception, and cognitive development. Her research addresses how written communication tasks, particularly handwriting, shape the neural systems underlying letter perception and literacy skills throughout childhood. Vinci-Booher's work has demonstrated the contribution of visual experiences during letter production to neural systems supporting letter perception and characterized the protracted development of dorsal motor systems during handwriting.

0545.name: Qasim Zaidi
0545.institution: State University of New York, College of Optometry
0545.bio: Dr. Van der Stigchel investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual attention, oculomotor control, and spatial cognition. Their research addresses how attention shapes visual perception and awareness, particularly concerning the interaction between attention and eye movements in healthy individuals and patient populations with attentional deficits. Their contributions include advancing the understanding of transsaccadic integration, elucidating the relationship between attention, eye movements, and visual working memory, and developing novel methodologies for studying attentional processes.

0546.name: Qi Chen
0546.institution: South China Normal University, Guangzhou, China
0546.bio: Dr. Bracci investigates the neural organization of object representations within the human visual cortex, particularly focusing on the occipitotemporal regions. Her research addresses how the brain recognizes objects, especially tools and body parts, by examining the contributions of domain-specific and domain-general processes and comparing biological and artificial vision systems. Her work has demonstrated overlapping representations of hands and tools in lateral occipitotemporal cortex based on object manipulation properties and revealed fine-grained representational similarities of body parts, while also showing that deep convolutional neural networks capture aspects of human visual representations.

0547.name: Quan Lei
0547.bio: Dr. Lourenco's research investigates the ontogeny of spatial and numerical cognition, focusing on the developmental interplay between these cognitive domains. Her work addresses fundamental questions regarding the nature, origins, and developmental trajectories of spatial and numerical abilities, including how infants represent magnitude and how visuospatial processing supports mathematical development. Dr. Lourenco has significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating the influence of socioeconomic status on sex differences in spatial skills, establishing evidence for general magnitude representation in infancy, and elucidating the relationship between nonsymbolic number representations and symbolic math competence.

0548.name: Quoc Vuong
0548.bio: Dr. Adamo's research investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual search and attention, particularly within the context of medical image perception. He examines factors contributing to errors in multiple-target visual search, such as satisfaction of search and the influence of different imaging modalities on performance. His work has identified the self-induced attentional blink and advanced the understanding of subsequent search misses, while also analyzing the impact of 3D tomosynthesis on visual search strategies in mammography.

0549.name: R. Conway Bevil
0549.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception and plasticity in the human brain. Their work addresses how experience-dependent plasticity modifies visual processing, the limits of neuroplasticity, and the potential for visual remapping to treat visual disorders. A significant contribution includes pioneering the use of functional MRI to study human visual perception and characterizing the effects of visual deprivation and perceptual learning on cortical processing.

0550.name: Rachel Denison
0550.bio: Dr. Heinen investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying smooth pursuit eye movements, visual attention, and decision-making processes within the oculomotor system. His research addresses how visual perception and cognition guide accurate eye movements, focusing on the integration of visual information and the allocation of attentional resources. Heinen's significant contributions include demonstrating the influence of peripheral motion on smooth pursuit, revealing shared attentional resources between saccadic and pursuit systems, and identifying neural correlates of decision-making in oculomotor behavior.

0551.name: Rachel Kelly
0551.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual search, attention, and perception, with a focus on individual differences and real-world applications. Their work addresses critical questions regarding target detection in complex search scenarios, the factors influencing search performance variability, and the potential for training to enhance visual-attentional abilities. Key contributions include the identification of the "ultra-rare-item effect," the development of a taxonomy of errors in multiple-target search, and demonstrations of enhanced multisensory temporal processing in video game players, significantly impacting both theoretical understanding and applied practices in domains such as security and sports.

0552.name: Rachel Pye
0552.bio: Dr. Mitroff investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual search, attention, perception, and memory, with a focus on individual differences and malleability. His research addresses critical questions regarding target detection rates, factors influencing multiple-target search efficacy, and the potential for cognitive enhancement through targeted training interventions. Mitroff's significant contributions include the identification of the "ultra-rare item effect," demonstrating the impact of target prevalence on detection, and the application of cognitive principles to improve visual search performance in applied domains such as security screening and medical imaging.

0553.name: Rachel Salamone
0553.bio: A cognitive neuroscientist, Dr. Luck investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying attention and working memory, particularly in the context of cognitive dysfunction observed in psychiatric disorders such as schizophrenia. Their research addresses fundamental questions regarding attentional control, working memory capacity limitations, and the neural correlates of cognitive processes using electrophysiological measures. Dr. Luck's significant contributions include pioneering change detection paradigms for assessing visual working memory capacity, developing influential theories of attentional control, and advancing event-related potential (ERP) methodology through methodological refinements and seminal texts.

0554.name: Ralf M. Haefner
0554.bio: Shevell's research investigates the neural mechanisms underlying human color and brightness perception, with a focus on contextual influences and binocular interactions. His work addresses how the visual system resolves ambiguity in chromatic information and how divisive normalization contributes to perceptual stability. Shevell's significant contributions include pioneering models of color constancy, elucidating mechanisms of chromatic adaptation, and advancing understanding of binocular color vision through psychophysical and computational approaches.

0555.name: Ralph G. Hale
0555.bio: Dr. Pesina investigates the cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying photoreceptor regeneration using zebrafish models of retinal degeneration. Their research aims to determine how the immune response and specific genetic pathways regulate successful photoreceptor regeneration in *gosh* mutant zebrafish. Dr. Pesina's contributions include characterizing the role of the immune system and identifying key genetic factors involved in retinal regeneration, as well as developing improved zebrafish models for studying retinal degenerative diseases.

0556.name: Randi Starrfelt
0556.bio: Shevell's research investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, with a particular emphasis on color vision, chromatic adaptation, and binocular integration. This work addresses how the visual system resolves ambiguity in neural representations to generate coherent percepts of color and brightness, and how contextual factors modulate color appearance. Shevell's contributions include pioneering models of chromatic adaptation and binocular color vision, along with advancements in psychophysical methodologies for studying perceptual ambiguity resolution.

0557.name: Ratan Murty
0557.department: Cognition and Brain Science, School of Psychology; Center of Excellence in Computational Cognition
0557.bio: Dr. Han investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying the interplay between visual attention and working memory. His research addresses how working memory contents modulate visual perception, how stimulus-driven and goal-directed factors interact during visual search, and the neural bases of attentional control. Dr. Han's work has demonstrated that working memory contents can enhance perception under stimulus-driven competition, identified the functional fractionation of the cingulo-opercular network in alerting and updating, and elucidated the role of cognitive control in attentional capture by working memory.

0558.name: Rebecca Carson
0558.bio: Dr. Gayet investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying conscious visual perception, particularly focusing on the interplay between attention, working memory, and statistical learning. Her research addresses how the brain integrates sensory information to generate conscious experiences, examining the factors that influence attentional selection and access to visual awareness. A significant contribution is her demonstration that statistical probabilities modulate conscious access, alongside the development and application of the breaking continuous flash suppression (b-CFS) paradigm for studying visual awareness.

0559.name: Rebecca Roy
0559.bio: Dr. Chung investigates the psychophysical and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception and reading, particularly in the context of normal and impaired vision. Her research addresses the limiting factors on visual performance in individuals with visual impairments and explores methods to enhance functional vision through cortical adaptation and plasticity. Dr. Chung's contributions include elucidating the spatial-frequency properties of crowding, demonstrating perceptual learning's efficacy in improving reading speed for those with central vision loss, and characterizing the preferred retinal locus in macular degeneration.

0560.name: Reinoud Bootsma
0560.bio: Dr. Chung investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual function in both normally sighted individuals and those with visual impairments, particularly focusing on reading and pattern vision. Her research addresses the limiting factors on visual performance in individuals with low vision, including the development of surrogate foveas and cortical adaptation to central vision loss. Her significant contributions include demonstrating the efficacy of perceptual learning for improving reading speed in individuals with central vision loss, characterizing fixational eye movements in amblyopia and macular disease, and developing methods for measuring three-dimensional scotomas.

0562.name: Renate Rutiku
0562.bio: Dr. Candy's research focuses on the development of visual function, particularly accommodation and binocular vision, in human infants and young children. Her work investigates the mechanisms underlying normal and abnormal visual development, with a focus on understanding how the developing brain coordinates visual experience through ocular motor responses. Dr. Candy has made significant contributions to the field through the development of novel methods for assessing infant visual function and elucidating the role of accommodation and defocus in early visual development, ultimately contributing to strategies for the early detection and intervention of amblyopia and strabismus.

0563.name: Reza Azadi
0563.bio: Dr. Murdison investigates visuomotor integration and oculomotor control, particularly within virtual and augmented reality environments. Their research addresses how humans integrate visual information for motor planning, optimize gaze-based interactions, and mitigate the effects of display technologies on eye movements and perception. Dr. Murdison has demonstrated the impact of stochastic reference frame transformations on perceptual decisions, developed methods for assessing eye tracking accuracy in VR, and revealed motor constraints on fixation selection.

0564.name: Ricahrd J Krauzlis
0564.bio: The Sprague Lab investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, attention, and working memory, focusing on how neural systems represent information to support dynamic behavioral goals. Their research addresses how actions and brain networks influence the representation of environmental information and the prioritization of items in working memory. Sprague's significant contributions include developing multivariate analysis methods for neuroimaging, demonstrating latent neural codes for remembered items, and elucidating the feature-specific interactions between working memory and attention.

0565.name: Richard A. Abrams
0565.bio: This researcher investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying visual perceptual learning, with a particular focus on learning that occurs outside of conscious awareness. Their work addresses how attention, reinforcement, and sleep modulate perceptual learning processes, and how these processes can be enhanced through techniques like decoded neurofeedback. A significant contribution includes the discovery of task-irrelevant perceptual learning and the development of decoded neurofeedback techniques to induce targeted brain activity patterns and associated perceptual improvements.

0566.name: Richard Krauzlis
0566.bio: Dr. Boger investigates the cognitive underpinnings of visual perception, focusing on how the mind segregates content from form in object representation. Their research addresses how physical and causal reasoning, along with higher-level cognitive factors, modulate object perception, style perception, and the generation of random sequences. Boger's contributions include demonstrating the incorporation of state-space transformations in object representation, modeling individual differences in random sequence generation, and identifying novel phenomena in style perception, such as style tuning and extrapolation.

0567.name: Robert Allison
0567.department: Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science; Department of Psychology
0567.bio: Konkle's research investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying high-level visual representation, particularly focusing on object, action, and scene perception. Her work aims to elucidate how the human visual system organizes information, the relationship between cortical topography and visual representation, and the influence of biological and ecological constraints. Konkle's significant contributions include demonstrating the link between object processing and real-world object size, pioneering research on massive visual memory emphasizing conceptual over perceptual properties, and developing novel fMRI and computational modeling approaches to study visual cortex organization.

0568.name: Robert Desimone
0568.bio: Dr. Gao develops computational models of human social perception, focusing on how humans extract social cues and infer intentions from visual input. Their research addresses how to imbue artificial intelligence systems with human-like commonsense reasoning grounded in visual perception and how to enable effective human-robot collaboration through shared visual understanding. Dr. Gao's significant contributions include computational models of animacy and intention detection, advancing the "strong cognition" perspective in AI, and elucidating the neural basis of intentionality detection in the right posterior superior temporal sulcus.

0569.name: Robert G. Alexander
0569.bio: Dr. Liu investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying visual selective attention, working memory, and perceptual decision-making. Their research addresses how attention modulates sensory processing, the computational principles governing visual working memory, and the neural basis of evidence accumulation during perceptual decisions. Dr. Liu's contributions include demonstrating feature-based attention's enhancement of neural responses in visual cortex, characterizing the temporal dynamics of spatial and feature-based attention, and developing computational models of visual working memory capacity.

0570.name: Robert L. Goldstone
0570.bio: Dr. Ooi's research focuses on the psychophysics and neural mechanisms underlying binocular vision, visual space perception, and visual plasticity. Her work investigates how the visual system integrates binocular information, resolves interocular conflict, and perceives spatial relationships, particularly distance. Her contributions include the development of a "push-pull" perceptual learning paradigm for amblyopia treatment, the demonstration of angular declination's role in distance perception, and the elucidation of surface representation and attention modulation in binocular rivalry.

0571.name: Robert M. McPeek
0571.bio: This researcher focuses on the developmental trajectory of visual processing, sensory integration, and visuomotor coordination in childhood. Their research investigates the neural mechanisms underlying the development of cue integration and sensory fusion in the visual cortex, as well as the development of visuomotor decision-making. This researcher has demonstrated that adult-like precision gains from visual cue combination emerge late in childhood, linked to the development of sensory fusion in cortical area V3B, and has advanced methods for assessing visual function in children.

0572.name: Robert Volcic
0572.department: NYUAD Center for Artificial Intelligence and Robotics; NYUAD Center for Brain and Health
0572.bio: Dr. Houghton investigates the neural circuitry of the *Drosophila* central complex to elucidate mechanisms of multisensory integration. His research addresses how the central complex weighs and combines inputs from various sensory modalities, particularly in the context of assessing sensory reliability. Dr. Houghton's work is contributing to the understanding of central complex circuitry, neural mechanisms for assessing sensory reliability, and adaptive sensory processing within the insect brain.

0573.name: Roberto Caldara
0573.department: Eye and Brain Mapping Lab, Department of Psychology
0573.bio: Dr. Sprague investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual cognition, with a focus on how the brain represents visual information to guide goal-directed behavior. Their research addresses how task demands and behavioral goals modulate neural representations of visual stimuli, particularly concerning resource allocation in visual working memory and the formation of attentional priority maps.  Dr. Sprague's significant contributions include the development and application of inverted encoding models to reconstruct population-level stimulus representations, demonstrating attentional modulation of spatial priority maps, and elucidating neural signatures of visual salience and behavioral relevance in attentional processing.

0574.name: Roland Fleming
0574.institution: Justus Liebig University Giessen; University of Marburg; Justus Liebig University Giessen; TU Darmstadt
0574.department: Center for Mind, Brain and Behavior, Universities of Marburg, Giessen, and Darmstadt
0574.bio: This researcher investigates the spatiotemporal dynamics of visual attention, perception, and memory using behavioral and neurophysiological methods. Their work addresses fundamental questions regarding the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying phenomena such as the attentional blink, inhibition of return, and visual masking. Key contributions include demonstrating that attentional blink magnitude is independent of T1 difficulty, elucidating the role of expectation in inhibition of return, and revealing the detrimental effects of route familiarity on driving performance.

0575.name: Ronald A. Rensink
0575.bio: Dr. Sprague investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual working memory and attention using computational neuroimaging. Their research addresses how behavioral goals and contextual factors modulate neural representations of visual information and how brain networks support the representation of environmental information. Sprague's work has significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating the degradation of visual spatial working memory reconstructions with increasing memory load and establishing the attentional modulation of spatial priority maps across occipital, parietal, and frontal cortices.

0576.name: Rosanne L. Rademaker
0576.institution: Ernst Strüngmann Institute for Neuroscience in Cooperation with the Max Planck Society
0576.bio: Dr. Collins investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, particularly focusing on the integration of information across saccadic eye movements and the influence of past experiences on current perception. Her research addresses how the visual system achieves perceptual stability, the neural basis of serial dependence, and the role of predictive processes in shaping visual experience and decision-making. Her work has demonstrated the object-independence of serial dependence, the ensemble-based nature of ensemble perception, and the rapid incorporation of past stimulus history into current object representations, while also dissociating shared neural codes between memory and perception.

0577.name: Roy Luria
0577.bio: Dr. Grootswagers investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual object perception and representation, focusing on the temporal dynamics of information processing in the brain. Their research addresses how the brain rapidly decodes and prioritizes task-relevant visual information, and how these representations are utilized in decision-making. A significant contribution includes developing methods for decoding dynamic brain patterns from evoked responses using multivariate pattern analysis of time series neuroimaging data, and demonstrating asymmetric compression of representational space for object animacy categorization.

0578.name: Roy Mukamel
0578.institution: Sagol School of Neuroscience, Tel-Aviv University
0578.bio: Dr. Brady investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual working memory, visual long-term memory, scene perception, and the encoding of ensemble statistics. Their research addresses questions regarding the capacity and structure of visual memory systems, the influence of prior knowledge on memory performance, and the nature of scene representation. Brady's work has demonstrated high-fidelity visual long-term memory, revealed the stimulus-dependent nature of visual working memory capacity, and proposed continuous-strength, population-based, hierarchical models of memory representation.

0579.name: Ru-Yuan Zhang
0579.bio: Dr. Sweeny investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying visual perception, particularly focusing on ensemble coding and social vision. Her research addresses how humans extract statistical summaries from groups of objects and faces, and how emotion influences these perceptual processes. Sweeny's work has demonstrated ensemble coding mechanisms for biological motion and gaze direction in crowds, revealed amplification effects in crowd emotion perception, and elucidated the time course of fleeting emotion detection, significantly advancing understanding of visual perception's role in social cognition.

0580.name: Ruben Coen-Cagli
0580.bio: Dr. Brady investigates the encoding, capacity, and representational format of visual information in working and long-term memory. Her research addresses questions regarding the limits of visual memory, the influence of prior knowledge, and the role of ensemble statistics in perception and memory. Her work has demonstrated the high capacity of visual long-term memory, revealed the flexible nature of visual working memory, and proposed a continuous, population-based model of visual memory representations.

0581.name: Ruggero Micheletto
0581.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying face perception and object recognition within the visual cortex. Their work addresses how the brain transforms visual stimuli into perceptual representations and the functional organization of high-level visual processing areas. Key contributions include elucidating the functional properties of face-selective regions, demonstrating the importance of internal facial features, and identifying neural correlates of perceptual aftereffects using fMRI and multivariate pattern analysis.

0582.name: Ruth Rosenholtz
0582.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, particularly in the context of social cognition and ensemble coding. Their work addresses how the brain integrates information from multiple faces or objects to form ensemble representations and how these perceptual processes guide social and emotional behaviors. Key contributions include demonstrating ensemble coding mechanisms for biological motion and gaze perception, identifying differences in gaze perception in autism spectrum disorders, and elucidating the role of visual perception in social and emotional processing.

0583.name: Ryan Ash
0583.bio: The Perception and Learning Lab investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, attention, and memory, with a particular emphasis on visual statistical learning and perceptual organization. This research aims to elucidate how incidental learning, perceptual grouping, and object representations influence visual processing, spatial perception, and the interaction between working memory and attention. Dr. Vickery's significant contributions include demonstrating the role of the inferior parietal lobule in decision-making under uncertainty, identifying ubiquitous reinforcement signals in the brain during decision-making, discovering the phenomenon of supercrowding, and revealing that associative grouping can occur based on learned associations.

0584.name: Sabrina Hansmann-Roth
0584.department: Icelandic Vision Lab, School of Health Sciences
0584.bio: Dr. Tina Plank investigates neuroplasticity and perceptual learning mechanisms in the context of visual impairment, particularly central vision loss. Her research addresses how the brain reorganizes functionally and structurally following macular degeneration and how perceptual learning can be harnessed to improve peripheral vision. Plank's work has demonstrated functional and structural changes in early visual cortex in patients with macular degeneration and has linked cortical thickness to compensatory eccentric viewing strategies.

0585.name: Sabrina Pitzalis
0585.bio: Dr. Zickler's research focuses on developing computational methods for scene understanding through physics-based vision, computational imaging, and applied optics. Her work addresses the fundamental problems of recovering 3D shape, material properties, and scene structure from 2D images by modeling and exploiting the physics of light transport. A significant contribution is the development of Helmholtz stereopsis, a technique for shape reconstruction independent of surface reflectance, alongside pioneering advancements in computational relighting, image-based rendering, and multi-view stereo algorithms.

0586.name: Sage E.P. Boettcher
0586.bio: Dr. Franken investigates the neural mechanisms underlying figure-ground segregation and object perception in primate visual cortex. Their research focuses on elucidating how border ownership is computed and propagated across visual areas, with a particular emphasis on the role of feedback and horizontal connections. Dr. Franken's significant contributions include the discovery of a columnar organization of border ownership selectivity in macaque V4 and the demonstration that these signals emerge first in deep cortical layers.

0587.name: Sam Ling
0587.bio: Dr. Pansell's research focuses on the neuro-ophthalmological consequences of acquired brain injuries and neurological diseases on visual function and eye movement control. Her work investigates the mechanisms underlying visual dizziness, the manifestation of ocular changes, and the potential of color filter treatments to improve visual perception in these patient populations. Dr. Pansell has significantly contributed to the field through characterizing torsional eye movements, developing methods for evaluating visual dizziness, and advancing the understanding of oculomotor dysfunction following mild traumatic brain injury.

0588.name: Sami R. Yousif
0588.institution: University of North Carolina, Chapel Hill
0588.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying high-level vision, specifically focusing on holistic processing, semantic influences, and spatial representation. Their work addresses how semantic knowledge modulates holistic word perception, how spatial features are maintained in visual working memory, and how the parahippocampal place area and retrosplenial cortex contribute to panoramic scene integration. Utilizing fMRI and behavioral experiments, they have demonstrated the influence of semantics on holistic word perception, elucidated the role of spatial features in visual working memory, and characterized neural mechanisms of panoramic scene integration.

0589.name: Samuel McDougle
0589.bio: This researcher investigates the interplay of attention, metacognition, and sensory integration in shaping visual perception within the periphery. Their work addresses how perceptual sensitivity and response bias modulate color perception and change detection as a function of eccentricity. Their contributions include characterizing the limitations of color perception in peripheral vision and demonstrating potential overestimations of peripheral visual capabilities in naturalistic settings, with published findings on perceptual sensitivity and response bias.

0590.name: Sang Chul Chong
0590.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying cognition during naturalistic, continuous experiences, with a focus on inter-individual brain coupling and communication. Their work addresses how the brain processes information over extended timescales, how shared neural responses emerge during shared experiences, and how memories are updated. They are recognized for pioneering the use of naturalistic stimuli in neuroimaging and developing intersubject correlation analysis, revealing neural coupling and temporal receptive windows in cortical processing.

0591.name: Sang Wook Hong
0591.institution: Florida Atlantic University; Stiles-Nicholson Brain Institute
0591.bio: Dr. Rodriguez's research investigates the development of civic agency and engagement within democratic learning communities in higher education. Her work explores the relationship between student engagement, civic literacy, and equity/inclusion, particularly examining how universities can foster just and democratic environments. Rodriguez's significant contribution lies in demonstrating how structured civic pathways within higher education can influence student habitus and promote conscious civic action, particularly within Hispanic-Serving Institutions.

0592.name: Sang-Hun Lee
0592.bio: This researcher investigates the neural and computational mechanisms underlying atypical visual context processing in psychosis spectrum disorders. Their work addresses how low-level brain circuits contribute to altered visuospatial perception and cognition in individuals at risk for or diagnosed with psychosis. They have demonstrated weakened contrast surround suppression as a potential neural mechanism underlying atypical perceptual experiences in schizophrenia and developed computational models to elucidate the interaction between visual acuity, focal attention, and untuned gain control in psychosis.

0593.name: Sara Inati
0593.institution: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke
0593.bio: Dr. Plaza's research investigates learning and memory processes, specifically face recognition memory, in individuals with developmental disabilities. Her work addresses how the differential outcomes procedure can be leveraged to enhance cognitive performance in these populations.
