Her significant contributions include demonstrating the efficacy of the differential outcomes procedure in improving face recognition memory in individuals with Down syndrome and advancing the understanding of cognitive processes in this population.

0594.name: Sarah Lamer
0594.bio: This researcher focuses on the developmental psychophysics of infant vision and oculomotor control, specifically investigating the integration of visual and oculomotor systems during early development. Their work addresses the neural mechanisms underlying the maturation of visual attention and eye movement control, and how early experiences shape visual development. Key contributions include characterizing infant gaze behavior during natural viewing, developing methodologies for assessing visual function in pre-verbal infants, and advancing understanding of the neural substrates of visual attention development in the first year.

0595.name: Sarah Shomstein
0595.bio: Dr. Mittal investigates the neurodevelopmental pathophysiology of psychosis risk, focusing on early identification and prediction of transition to psychosis in high-risk youth. Her research addresses the underlying neural and cognitive mechanisms contributing to psychosis development, with a particular emphasis on the role of motor abnormalities, stress biology, and developmental changes in psychotic-like experiences. Dr. Mittal's contributions include the advancement of motor function as a biomarker for psychosis risk, the development of assessment tools for identifying individuals at clinical high risk, and the application of computational approaches to analyze vocal and linguistic features as indicators of psychosis risk.

0596.name: Sarah Waugh
0596.bio: Dr. Störmer investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying selective attention and its influence on perception and visual working memory. Their research addresses questions regarding the principles governing attentional selection, the capacity limits of visual working memory, and the integration of multisensory information. Störmer's work has demonstrated that feature-based attention can distort feature representations, that visual working memory capacity is modulated by stimulus meaningfulness, and that attentional selection operates through both feature enhancement and suppression in early visual cortex.

0597.name: Sashank Varma
0597.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying perception, attention, and memory, with a particular focus on visual cognition, auditory processing, and crossmodal attention. Their work addresses fundamental questions regarding selective attention, multimodal integration, and the capacity limits of visual working memory. Key contributions include demonstrating the influence of auditory stimuli on visual perception, revealing feature-based surround suppression, identifying limits on multiple object tracking, and establishing the role of meaningfulness in expanding visual working memory capacity.

0598.name: Scott O Murray
0598.bio: This researcher investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying human visual perception of material properties, intuitive physics, and their influence on visuomotor control. Their research addresses how humans integrate visual and haptic information to perceive object properties, plan grasping actions, and utilize efficient visual heuristics for physical object understanding. Key contributions include demonstrating the visual judgment and refinement of grasp quality, elucidating neural correlates of visually guided grasping, and advancing the understanding of liquid perception from static images.

0599.name: Scott Steinmetz
0599.bio: This researcher investigates the neural and cognitive mechanisms underlying attention and sensory processing, with a particular focus on cross-modal and object-based attention. Their work addresses how these mechanisms develop from infancy to adulthood and how emotional cues modulate sensory processing across modalities. Key contributions include demonstrating selectivity for configural facial cues in visual cortex, elucidating mechanisms of object-based attention in early visual cortex, and exploring cross-modal correspondences between abstract shapes and auditory stimuli.

0600.name: Scott Watamaniuk
0600.institution: Smith-Kettlewell Eye Research Institute; Wright State University
0600.bio: This researcher focuses on developing robust and generalizable visual recognition systems, particularly within the domains of computer vision, machine learning, and artificial intelligence. Their work addresses the challenges of open set recognition, media forensics, and the ethical implications of AI by creating algorithms that can handle novel classes, detect manipulated media, and align with human values. A significant contribution is the pioneering development of algorithms for open set recognition, including the Extreme Value Machine (EVM), and advancing techniques for image manipulation detection, thereby impacting both the technical and ethical landscape of visual intelligence.

0601.name: Se-Bum Paik
0601.institution: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology
0601.bio: Dr. Inoue investigates the neurobiological mechanisms of stress-induced neural plasticity within the paraventricular nucleus of the hypothalamus (PVN). His research aims to elucidate the cellular and molecular underpinnings of how stress modifies neural circuits, leading to maladaptive plasticity and stress-related disorders. He has demonstrated state-dependent activity dynamics of hypothalamic stress effector neurons, identified the role of prostaglandin E2 in modulating GABAergic transmission in the PVN, and elucidated mechanisms of synaptic plasticity in corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) neurons.

0602.name: Sean Polyn
0602.bio: Dr. Zhu's research investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual awareness and unconscious processing, particularly within the context of continuous flash suppression (CFS). Her work aims to elucidate the spatiotemporal dynamics of visual information processing and the conditions under which visual stimuli reach conscious awareness. Dr. Zhu has demonstrated the importance of feature-based interactions and mask properties, such as spatial density and temporal frequency, in modulating the effectiveness of CFS, identifying an optimal mask frequency of approximately 6 Hz.

0603.name: Sebastian M. Frank
0603.bio: This researcher focuses on the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual working memory and attention. Their work investigates how visual information is encoded, maintained, and retrieved, and how individual differences impact cognitive performance. A significant contribution includes the development of the Variable Precision model of visual working memory and demonstrating the role of familiarity in visual short-term memory consolidation, alongside utilizing neuroimaging techniques to elucidate neural correlates.

0604.name: Sedthapong Chunamchai
0604.department: Cognitive Clinical & Computational Neuroscience Lab, Chula Neuroscience Center, King Chulalongkorn Memorial Hospital
0604.bio: Dr. Xie investigates the neural mechanisms underlying the encoding, maintenance, and retrieval of precise memories, particularly focusing on visual short-term memory. His research addresses how cognitive processes like attention and working memory interact with long-term memory formation, and how these processes are affected in healthy individuals versus those with neurological or psychiatric conditions. Xie's contributions include demonstrating the role of the hippocampus in precise visual short-term memory, elucidating the relationship between neural oscillations and memory precision, and developing novel analytical approaches like the inverted encoding model for studying memory representations.

0605.name: Selassie Tagoh
0605.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception, specifically focusing on face and object recognition, as well as visual attention. Their work addresses questions regarding holistic processing in face perception, ensemble perception of emotions, and the neural substrates of visual attention. Key contributions include advancing the understanding of viewpoint-dependent object recognition, elucidating holistic processing in face perception, and investigating the other-race effect in ensemble emotion perception.

0606.name: Serra Favila
0606.bio: This researcher investigates the visual control of human locomotion and navigation within complex environments, focusing on perception-action coupling. Their work addresses how visual information guides adaptive locomotor behavior, how collective behavior emerges in crowds, and how spatial knowledge is represented for large-scale navigation. Key contributions include pioneering virtual reality techniques for studying locomotion, developing optic flow-based models of steering, demonstrating the role of body-scaled information in affordance perception, and formulating a vision-based model of pedestrian behavior.

0607.name: Shahin Nasr
0607.institution: Athinoula A. Martinos Center for Biomedical Imaging, Massachusetts General Hospital; Harvard Medical School
0607.bio: Dr. Tuten investigates structure-function relationships in the human retina at the cellular level using adaptive optics retinal imaging and psychophysical techniques. Her research addresses fundamental questions regarding the neural mechanisms underlying color and spatial vision, particularly in the fovea, and how retinal diseases impact these processes.  She is recognized for developing adaptive optics microperimetry and demonstrating spatiochromatic interactions between individual cone photoreceptors, contributing significantly to the understanding of foveal vision and retinal pathology.

0608.name: Shannon Ross-Sheehy
0608.bio: The Tuten Lab investigates the neural mechanisms underlying spatial and color vision by examining retinal structure-function relationships at the cellular level. Their research addresses how optical factors, eye movements, and post-receptoral processing limit visual performance and influence the perception of cone-sized stimuli. Dr. Tuten's group has significantly contributed to the field through the development of adaptive optics scanning laser ophthalmoscope-based microperimetry techniques and the demonstration of spatiochromatic interactions between individual cone photoreceptors.

0609.name: Shaul Hochstein
0609.bio: This researcher investigates the visual control of locomotion and navigation, particularly within complex virtual environments and human crowds. Their work addresses how humans utilize visual information, such as optic flow, to steer, avoid obstacles, and form spatial knowledge for navigation. They have significantly contributed to the field through the development of models for visual control of steering, elucidation of local interaction rules in crowd behavior, and advancement of understanding regarding the geometry of cognitive maps.

0610.name: Shaun Wallace
0610.bio: Dr. Bainbridge investigates the cognitive neuroscience of perception and memory, with a focus on visual memory, face perception, and object recognition. Her research addresses the intrinsic memorability of visual stimuli, the neural mechanisms underlying the perception-memory bridge, and the potential of memorability to enhance learning. Bainbridge has pioneered the study of image memorability, developed computational models for its prediction, identified neural signatures using fMRI, and established drawing as a tool for studying visual memory.

0611.name: Sheng He
0611.institution: Institute of Biophysics, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences
0611.bio: This researcher investigates the computational and neural mechanisms underlying visual perception in natural environments. Their work addresses how the visual system efficiently processes information from complex scenes, focusing on optimal strategies for visual search and the relationship between natural scene statistics and visual performance. A significant contribution includes the development of ideal observer models and the application of natural scene statistics to elucidate visual processing, alongside advancements in understanding optimal visual search and contrast gain control mechanisms in visual cortex neurons.

0612.name: Shibo Cao
0612.department: Department of Psychology, Developmental and Brain Sciences
0612.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying face recognition and its role in social cognition, employing a systems neuroscience approach. Their work addresses how the brain extracts social meaning from faces, focusing on the functional interactions within face-selective brain regions and the modulation of face processing by attention. A significant contribution is the identification and characterization of a specialized, interconnected face-processing network ("face patches") in the primate brain, elucidating their distinct roles in various aspects of face perception.

0613.name: Shigeki Nakauchi
0613.department: Department of Computer Science and Engineering
0613.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying sensory processing and cross-modal plasticity, particularly in the context of atypical development and sensory deprivation. Their work aims to elucidate how the brain adapts to altered sensory input, understand atypical sensory processing in autism spectrum disorder, and determine the computational constraints on cross-modal compensation. Their significant contributions include characterizing atypical visual motion prediction in ASD, revealing enhanced auditory motion perception in early blind individuals, and developing computational models of sensory resource reallocation following neural adaptation.

0614.name: Shin'ya Nishida
0614.bio: Dr. Fu investigates the interplay between cognitive and emotional processes, particularly within the domains of attention, memory, and deception. Her research addresses the psychological mechanisms underlying deception, the interaction of cognition and emotion, and the influence of cultural context on these processes. Dr. Fu's contributions include developing theoretical models of cognition-emotion interaction, advancing understanding of cultural differences in implicit learning, and pioneering the field of lying psychology in China through computational and neuroimaging methodologies.

0615.name: Shinsuke Shimojo
0615.bio: Dr. Gao's research investigates the impact of early visual experience on the development of high-level visual functions, particularly face perception, and its underlying neural mechanisms. He seeks to elucidate how early visual deprivation and atypical sensory experiences affect face processing, multisensory integration, and the functional organization of the occipitotemporal cortex. His work has demonstrated prolonged plasticity in face processing systems following early visual deprivation, revealed altered patterns of multisensory integration in individuals with congenital cataracts, and developed a highly efficient fast periodic stimulation fMRI paradigm for mapping face-selective brain regions.

0616.name: Shin’ya Nishida
0616.bio: Dr. Wei investigates the computational principles underlying neural information processing, with a focus on efficient coding and statistical learning in the brain. Their research addresses how the brain exploits environmental regularities and task structures to support adaptive behavior, particularly within spatial navigation and working memory systems. Dr. Wei's significant contributions include pioneering the use of optimization-based recurrent neural networks to model spatial navigation, developing methods like pi-VAE for extracting low-dimensional neural manifolds, and formulating computational models based on efficient coding to explain neurophysiological data.

0617.name: Shlomit Yuval-Greenberg
0617.bio: Dr. Yeshurun's research focuses on the interplay between visual attention and spatiotemporal aspects of perception. Her work investigates how attentional allocation affects perceptual tradeoffs between spatial and temporal resolution, as well as the impact of perceptual load on attentional selection. She has significantly contributed to the field by demonstrating that transient attention enhances spatial resolution while potentially impairing temporal resolution, and by elucidating the effects of attention on texture segmentation and crowding.

0618.name: Shruti Japee
0618.bio: Dr. Sun's research focuses on leveraging machine learning and big data analytics to advance educational evaluation, particularly within STEM disciplines. His work addresses the challenge of objectively and efficiently assessing complex cognitive skills, such as scientific argumentation and higher-order thinking, through data-driven methodologies. Dr. Sun has significantly contributed to the field by developing machine learning algorithms for automated grading of scientific argumentation and applying cognitive diagnostic modeling to analyze students' cognitive patterns.

0619.name: Shuhao Fu
0619.bio: Yaoda Xu investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual cognition, particularly focusing on visual working memory and the differential roles of the occipito-temporal and posterior parietal cortices. Her research addresses how the brain extracts and represents visual information at multiple levels, and how task context and attention modulate these representations. Xu's significant contributions include the neural object file theory, characterization of distinct roles for inferior and superior intraparietal sulcus in visual working memory, and demonstration of posterior parietal cortex's control over visual representations in the occipito-temporal cortex.

0620.name: Shui'er Han
0620.institution: Institute for Infocomm Research, Agency for Science, Technology and Research, Singapore
0620.department: Centre for Frontier AI Research, Agency for Science, Technology and Research, Singapore
0620.bio: Dr. Xiong's research focuses on the perceptual and cognitive mechanisms underlying sensory integration and rehabilitation in individuals with low vision and dual sensory loss. Her work investigates how sensory impairments impact object localization, reading performance, and everyday tasks, particularly exploring the interplay between visual and auditory processing. Dr. Xiong's contributions include the development of a clinically-driven low-vision simulation for predicting reading performance and elucidating the relationship between contrast sensitivity and visual acuity in low vision populations, informing the design of effective rehabilitation strategies.

0621.name: Shuo Wang
0621.bio: Dr. Fu investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying selective attention and its interaction with working memory. Her research addresses how the brain filters and prioritizes information for encoding into working memory, particularly examining the role of active inhibition. Dr. Fu's work has demonstrated the active suppression of outdated attended information and revealed a developmental reversal in memory selection abilities, contributing significantly to understanding cognitive selectivity across the lifespan.

0622.name: Simona Buetti
0622.bio: Dr. Wang's research focuses on the intersection of visual psychophysics, retinal imaging, and machine learning to investigate macular function in both healthy aging and retinal diseases. Her work addresses fundamental questions regarding the mechanisms of shape perception and perceptual organization, particularly in the context of age-related macular degeneration and diabetic retinopathy, and seeks to develop sensitive methods for early disease detection. Dr. Wang has made significant contributions through the development of shape discrimination hyperacuity tests for early AMD detection, the elucidation of local feature contributions to shape integration, and the application of machine learning techniques to retinal image analysis.

0623.name: Simona Garrobio
0623.bio: Dr. Shi focuses on developing novel computational frameworks for neuroanatomical mapping and connectome analysis. Their research addresses fundamental problems in brain connectivity analysis, multimodal neuroimaging integration, and the application of machine learning to neuroimaging data. Dr. Shi's significant contributions include pioneering work in diffusion MRI analysis, advanced 3D shape analysis of brain structures, and advancements in brain mapping techniques within the Human Connectome Project.

0624.name: Simona Monaco
0624.bio: This researcher investigates the design of interactive content and human-computer interaction, with a focus on 3D user interfaces and virtual reality. Their work addresses challenges in creating natural 3D interfaces, improving nonverbal communication in virtual environments, understanding cultural influences on motion perception, and developing adaptive interfaces responsive to affective states. Key contributions include the development of novel 3D interaction techniques, pioneering work in tangible user interfaces and spatial augmented reality, and advancements in affective computing and motion capture analysis, particularly within cross-cultural contexts.

0625.name: Siobhan M. Schabrun
0625.bio: Dr. Li investigates the neural mechanisms underlying high-level cognitive functions, with a specific focus on speech and language processing in the human cortex. Their research addresses how the brain encodes and processes speech, particularly tonal language, and seeks to develop computational models of these processes using machine learning. Dr. Li's significant contributions include the discovery of cortical encoding of pitch in tonal languages, the development of methods for decoding and synthesizing tonal language speech from brain activity, and the elucidation of how endogenous activity modulates neural tuning and perceptual behavior.

0626.name: Skylar DeWitt
0626.bio: Yuguang Zhao investigates the perceptual and cognitive mechanisms underlying human appreciation of visual art, focusing on artistic style and material perception. Their research addresses how humans perceive artistic style, how material perception varies across artistic media, and how insights from art perception can inform human-computer interaction. Zhao's work has demonstrated that humans perceive artistic style from local image features and identified key perceptual dimensions influencing style judgments, while also revealing differences in material property perception across painting and engraving.

0627.name: Soojin Park
0627.bio: Kazuhisa Sasaki investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual perceptual learning and memory consolidation, particularly focusing on the role of sleep. His research addresses how different sleep stages and associated brain oscillations contribute to the acquisition and stabilization of visual skills. Sasaki's work has demonstrated the complementary roles of REM and non-REM sleep in perceptual learning, identified specific brain oscillations involved in learning consolidation, and elucidated the neural basis of the "first night effect" in sleep studies.

0628.name: Sophia Vinci-Booher
0628.department: Dept. of Psychology and Human Development
0628.bio: Dr. Zaghloul investigates the neural mechanisms underlying human episodic memory formation and retrieval, leveraging intracranial electrophysiology. His research addresses how cortical networks activate during memory encoding and recall, and the role of the basal ganglia in learning and decision-making. His significant contributions include demonstrating replay of cortical spiking sequences during human memory retrieval and identifying large-scale communication rhythmically modulated through alpha coherence.

0629.name: Stefan Pollmann
0629.bio: Dr. Gao's research investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual working memory and attention, with a particular focus on feature binding and object-based processing. This work addresses fundamental questions regarding the nature of representations in visual working memory, the interplay between attention and memory, and the application of these principles to human-AI interaction. Dr. Gao's contributions include demonstrating object-based storage in visual working memory, establishing microsaccades as indices of covert attention, and advancing the understanding of human-AI joint cognitive systems.

0630.name: Stefan Van der Stigchel
0630.bio: Dr. Sun investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying the perception and representation of complex visual scenes. Her research addresses how the human mind constructs mental representations of scenes and actions over time, and how visual complexity influences cognitive processing. Dr. Sun's work has demonstrated the canonical ordering in scene representation construction and elucidated the cognitive consequences of varying levels of visual complexity.

0631.name: Stefania Bracci
0631.bio: Dr. Saygin investigates the developmental cognitive neuroscience of brain connectivity and functional specialization. Her research addresses how structural and functional brain architecture develops, how early brain data predicts later neural responses, and what neural markers predict clinical outcomes or shape brain plasticity. Dr. Saygin's work has demonstrated that anatomical connectivity patterns predict functional selectivity, particularly in visual areas, and has identified early neural markers predictive of reading ability and dyslexia.

0632.name: Stella F. Lourenco
0632.bio: Dr. Wang investigates the neural mechanisms underlying visual plasticity and perceptual learning, with a particular emphasis on the role of neuromodulatory systems. His research addresses how the brain implements plasticity, how neuromodulation interacts with plasticity, and how neurofeedback can be utilized to probe and modify visual processing. He has demonstrated the feasibility of inducing perceptual learning of basic visual features in early visual cortex using decoded neurofeedback and has identified differential neural mechanisms of face detection learning in individuals with body dysmorphic disorder.

0633.name: Stephen Adamo
0633.bio: This researcher investigates the computational mechanisms underlying visual perception, attention, and perceptual learning. Their work aims to develop computational brain models that elucidate how the brain computes for perception and cognition. A significant contribution is the development of the external noise method for characterizing perceptual template models, along with advancements in understanding visual motion perception, perceptual learning theories, and Bayesian active learning applications in vision testing.

0634.name: Stephen Engel
0634.bio: This researcher investigates the neural mechanisms underlying active vision, with a specific focus on the role of fixational eye movements in visual perception and sensorimotor integration. Their research addresses how microsaccades and superior colliculus function contribute to visual stability, attention, and orienting behaviors. Key contributions include identifying a neural substrate for microsaccade generation in the superior colliculus and demonstrating that microsaccades reflect covert attentional shifts, thereby linking oculomotor control to cognitive processes.

0635.name: Stephen Heinen
0635.bio: Dr. He investigates the cognitive and neural mechanisms underlying visual and spatial perception, with a particular focus on intermediate distance space perception. Their research addresses how humans perceive objects and environments, integrate visual and non-visual cues, and how attention modulates visual space perception. Dr. He's significant contributions include the discovery of ground-based spatial coding, demonstration of allocentric visual odometry, and elucidation of attentional modulation in perceived visual space, often achieved through novel psychophysical paradigms.

0636.name: Stephen Mitroff
0636.bio: This researcher investigates the cognitive mechanisms underlying visual perception, attention, and eye movements, particularly in the context of visual search and foraging. Their work addresses how perceptual history, working memory, and probabilistic representations influence visual processing and decision-making during active exploration of the environment. They have significantly contributed to the understanding of priming effects in visual search, the role of working memory in attention, and the dynamics of visual foraging behavior, often employing computational modeling and eye-tracking methodologies.

0637.name: Steven Dakin
0637.institution: School of Optometry & Vision Science, University of Auckland; Institute of Ophthalmology, University College London
0637.bio: Dr. Tomaz investigates the perceptual and neural mechanisms underlying binocular vision, particularly focusing on stereopsis and visual appearance in amblyopia. Her research addresses how interocular suppression affects depth perception and the quantification of visual appearance at the limits of spatial resolution. Dr. Tomaz has significantly contributed to the field through the development of novel methodologies for capturing and analyzing visual appearance, elucidating the relationship between suppression and stereopsis in amblyopia, and advancing understanding of visual function at the spatial resolution limit.

0638.name: Steven J. Luck
0638.bio: Dr. Tanrikulu investigates the mechanisms underlying perceptual organization, depth perception, and history effects in visual processing. Her research addresses how the visual system groups elements, assigns depth relationships, and integrates past experiences to construct coherent representations of the environment. Her contributions include demonstrating enhanced serial dependence in depth perception using virtual reality, elucidating the interaction of contour geometry and optic flow in depth judgments, and exploring the role of secondary features in serial dependence effects.
